responseSet.title: Response to Special Editorial (Vol. 83, Pt. 5, p. 1001)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Special Editorial (Vol. 83, Pt. 5, p. 1001)

Marco Chiesa

Marco Chiesa

Richmond
TW107JF
UK
m.chiesa@ucl.ac.uk

Marco Chiesa  

Dear Sirs,

The proposed boycott of Israeli scientific institutions may be rightly criticised on several grounds. For one, it would be a serious error to close channels of communication with enlightened sectors of Israeli society. Among these are several academics who would want to see a just peace in the Middle East based on a two-state political settlement in which the rights of both populations are upheld and respected, and in which both Israelis and Palestinians enjoy a right to self-determination, in line with several UN resolutions and with the international consensus.

Nevertheless, some information concerning the complex context and timing that shaped the call for the boycott is needed and should not merely be dismissed in less than a sentence (‘in support of one side in the Middle East conflict’) implying that it stems from ‘deep-seated sources of hatred and prejudice’. It is important to remind ourselves that the reasoning at the root of the proposed discontinuation of the co-operation with official Israeli institutions (which, by the way, does not exclude ties with individual Israeli academics) is the belief that the recent military reoccupation of Palestinian territories in the West Bank and Gaza by the Sharon governmentish in defiance of UN resolutions and the Geneva Convention, and the dramatic increase in human rights violations needed a response from the international community.

To date, hundreds of signatures have been obtained from academics worldwide (details are available on www.pjpo.org) and, remarkably, a number of Israeli academics have signed the European petition calling for a moratorium on European support to the Israeli academia. One of them is Tanya Reinhart, professor of linguistics at Tel-Aviv University, who explains in a long and detailed letter her reasons for joining this type of action (www.pjpo.org/letter_reinhart).

It is argued that Israel itself has been guilty of curtailing academic freedom in the occupied territories for decades with little or no reaction from its own academics or by and large from the outside world. The Norwegian Academy of Science recently denounced the damage inflicted academic institutions in the West Bank and Gaza, and the siege conditions experienced at Bir Zeit University. Over the years, Bir Zeit has been a favourite target of attacks, with disruption of classes by the incursion of Israeli military personnel, night raids on men's and women's dormitories, confiscation of students' IDs, arbitrary search, arrest, imprisonment and daily humiliations inflicted on students (Jerusalem Post, 13 July 1982). In 1982, the entire foreign faculty was expelled for refusing to sign a statement that they would not support the PLO (New York Times, 20 November 1982). Improvements in the level of teaching at university level have been considerably hampered by the Israeli authorities' refusal to grant the right to return to Palestinian graduates who were training abroad when Israel occupied the West Bank and Gaza in 1967. This, coupled with expulsions of Palestinian intellectuals and lecturers, has had a serious depleting effect on the quality of teaching in academic institutions.

responseSet.title: Response to Maizels
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Maizels

Krin Gabbard

Krin Gabbard

Stony Brook,
NY 11794-3355
kgabbard@notes.cc.sunysb.edu

Krin Gabbard  

Dear Sirs,

Neil Maizels reads Saving Private Ryan as an artistic artifact sealed off from the rest of the world. His emphasis on the mother as the driving force behind the narrative therefore makes perfect sense. Maizels is certainly entitled to an interpretation that brings a great deal of credulity to the events depicted in the film. I just wish he had brought as much credulity to his reading of my review and not misrepresented it so drastically. I certainly did not characterise ‘Spielberg's (and his generation's) “Oedipal” rivalry with the father as the “real” cause of War’. Rather, I asked why SPR should give us such a highly positive view of American soldiers, especially when compared to earlier films such as Catch-22 (1970) and Full Metal Jacket (1987). SPR's reverential treatment of the American fighting man was driven at least in part by the death and dying of the last veterans of World War II, many of whom were the fathers of middle-aged men like Steven Spielberg, who created SPR, as well as Tom Brokaw and those who have also idealised the men who fought that war. This shift in representations ought to be placed in historical context—satirical and irreverent views of World War II gave way to accounts such as those of Spielberg and Brokaw at the end of the twentieth century when baby boomers were mourning the passing of fathers who came of age during the war. Both conscious and unconscious forces lie behind the need to reconcile with ageing fathers, especially those fathers who were in conflict with their children during the years of the American invasion of Vietnam. SPR provided those children—now in their forties and fifties and surely revising the attitudes of their youth—with appealing images of fathers such as Capt. Miller (Tom Hanks) and Gen. Marshall (Harve Presnell). The film makes it easy for baby boomers to reconcile with these paragons. Unfortunately, the film's celebration of white male courage and integrity does not make it so easy to celebrate the postwar progress made by women, African Americans and other groups who were excluded from the US Army as well as from most of Spielberg's film.

responseSet.title: Response to Bott
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Bott

David Millar

David Millar

Adult Department
The Tavistock Clinic
120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA
UK
dmillar@tavi-port.org

David Millar  

Dear Sirs,

I am most grateful to James Bott for his interesting response to my paper on biblical myth. In my paper I make the argument that a particular pattern emerges in both the Old and New Testament myths. In this pattern, first, where the group is threatened with loss, ultimately loss of the good object, there is a to and fro movement between persecutory and more depressive anxieties. We see apocalyptic threats linked to a paranoid-schizoid state of mind, but also movement in a direction out of the omnipotent defensive organisation and concrete thinking towards greater separation from the good object. I illustrate, for example, how in the New Testament the idea of the Kingdom of God occurs in both contexts. Second, in this pattern—at a point in history where there is an experience of massive loss—there is a retreat towards a state of mind dominated by the omnipotent belief that the good object can be exclusively possessed. I argue that the Jewish post-exilic myth, with its ‘overriding virtue of close kin endogamy’ and belief in descent from Abraham, and the Jesus myth, with that group's belief that it was founded by the Son of God, can both be understood as defensive retreats from depressive anxieties with their full impact of loss and guilt. If I have understood Bott's letter correctly, he agrees with a number of the underlying hypotheses and he adds some very interesting points of his own. However, again if I understand his argument correctly, his thinking differs in significant ways from my own. When Bott says he wants to ‘to point out how the myths of that [Christian] belief system can be understood as an attempt to struggle with the difficulties of the depressive position’, I think he wants to introduce a more familiar and less disturbing perception. His suggestion that my concept of hardened myth is ‘a particular group use of a myth’ is likewise an invitation to return to this perception. However, it is this familiar perception and the nature of its viewpoint that my research questions.

responseSet.title: The Logics of the Mind: Jorge L. Ahumada. London: Karnac Books. 2001. Pp. 167.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Logics of the Mind

Jorge L. Ahumada. London: Karnac Books. 2001. Pp. 167.

Viviana Minerbo

Viviana Minerbo

5 Ave St Honoré d'Eylau
75116 Paris France

Viviana Minerbo  

Ahumada can be considered a scholar in view of his vast knowledge of psychoanalysis as well as of other disciplines, his critical ability, thorough method of study, vast clinical experience and his original oeuvre as a whole.

The Logics of the Mind was written over a span of sixteen years, from 1979 to 1996, and has been published in various languages. It is a collection of eight papers, plus a foreword by Horacio Etchegoyen, an introduction and an epilogue. The style of writing taxes the reader's understanding and demands great concentration, even if one is well acquainted with the psychoanalytic theories of the many eminent authors who have inspired Ahumada in one way or another. He gives credit to these authors, citing all his sources in the long list of references at the end of the book.

All the papers presented are illustrated with clinical vignettes from different patients. I shall focus my attention here specifically on one patient, Mrs A, who is referred to in different chapters of the book, at different moments of her analysis, showing her slow and difficult evolution. This testifies to what a fine analyst Ahumada is, and how his reflection on the dream and other clinical material made him build up his specific theoretical ideas inductively.

The author's peculiar approach to the topography of the mind is based on a notion that permeates his understanding of the patient's psychic dynamics. He posits a transposition of self and object as a two-person psychological structure, in which a central false self usurps the place of the object, and splits off the self's unconscious needy emotional aspects, projected into an external object.

To be ‘successful’, this psychic operation requires a double cleavage: the projection of an envious aspect of the self delusionally identifies with—and usurps—the kindness of a primal internal object, giving place to a delusion of one's omnipotent goodness, and the cleavage and projective identification of one's needy infantile self into a suitable partner. Thinking in terms of a double cleavage and projective identification is useful as the concept of projective identification alone does not lend itself to conceptualisation in terms of psychic structures.

responseSet.title: Stepchildren of Nature: Krafft-Ebing, Psychiatry, and the Making of Sexual Identity: Harry Oosterhuis. Chicago: University of Chicago Press. 2000. Pp. 321.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stepchildren of Nature: Krafft-Ebing, Psychiatry, and the Making of Sexual Identity

Harry Oosterhuis. Chicago: University of Chicago Press. 2000. Pp. 321.

Eric Engstrom

Eric Engstrom

Klingsorstrasse 119
12203 Berlin
Germany

Eric Engstrom  

In 1992 Harry Oosterhuis, professor of History at the University of Maastricht, discovered just under 1,400 patient records from Richard Freiherr von Krafft-Ebing's private practice. In about 200 of these case histories he found patients’ letters and autobiographical accounts. Many of these accounts, dating from 1871 to 1902, were written in response to Krafft-Ebing's Psychopathia Sexualis. As such, they represent the faint surviving traces not only of the personal lives of late nineteenth-century patients but also of their encounters with Krafft-Ebing's published work. These autobiographical accounts comprise the heart and soul of this well-organised and articulate book, and they have provided Oosterhuis with the material for what is an insightful study of sexual identity and its making in the late nineteenth century.

Oosterhuis's book is revisionist in two basic respects. First, he suggests that our understanding of Krafft-Ebing needs to undergo a fundamental reorientation. Indeed, in many ways the book is an attempt to rehabilitate Krafft-Ebing. It takes issue with the interpretations made by historians of psychiatry such as Szasz and Shorter, both of whom have had little good to say about Krafft-Ebing, as well as with the work of social and cultural historians such as Toulmin, Schorske, and Rider, who have all but ignored Krafft-Ebing or obviated his influence by reducing the formation of sexual identity to an epiphenomenon of social and cultural trends. Oosterhuis is especially critical of ‘presentist’ accounts, which hold Krafft-Ebing to contemporary standards of virtue and chastise him for not standing the test of modern psychology. Oosterhuis sees the sexual revolution of the nineteen sixties as having relegated sexual repression to the ‘dustbin of history’, and hence as having made it more difficult to judge Krafft-Ebing in the context of his own age. Krafft-Ebing's bad press is thus in part due to his being viewed from the perspective of an ‘ideology of sexual liberation’, which has judged him—to Oosterhuis's mind falsely—a pre-modern Victorian prude. responseSet.documentRef: Pujante, J. T. (2002) La clínica y el proceso psicoanalítico.: Pedro Guillem Nácher. Valencia: Promolibro. 2001. Pp. 276.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  83:1215-1217

responseSet.title: La clínica y el proceso psicoanalítico.: Pedro Guillem Nácher. Valencia: Promolibro. 2001. Pp. 276.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La clínica y el proceso psicoanalítico.

Pedro Guillem Nácher. Valencia: Promolibro. 2001. Pp. 276.

Jaime Pujante

Jaime Pujante

Rios Rosas, 19 - 2° K
28003 Madrid
Spain

Jaime Pujante  

In parts of this book its author continues to develop his reflections on narcissism and its pathology, which he has expressed in previous publications, notably in his Fundamentos narcisistas y espejos rotos (1996). Alongside ideas set out in earlier works, we find in Pedro Guillem Nácher's present work, La clínica y el proceso psicoanalítico, a new focus on self-esteem and its management, on the mixing and unmixing of instincts, and on the relationship between ego and superego and between ideals and object relations.

This is a virtually unpublished text. Only chapters 3 and 8, and part of 4, have previously appeared—in the Revista de Psicoanálisis of the Madrid Psychoanalysis Association (APM); and chapter 6, on the psychoanalytical process, was circulated as a monograph among those attending the 7th Symposium of the APM, in November 1998.

With a simplicity one sees only in authors of great experience, Pedro Guillem brings us closer to his work as a psychoanalyst of the last few years as he outlines the process that has led to the development and integration of the theoretical and technical criteria influencing the construction of his referential frame—this last being especially evident in the opening chapter, devoted to the analysis of an agoraphobic patient. The analysis unfolds in two stages. In the first stage, the establishment of a sado-masochistic dynamic between patient and analyst, with predominantly oedipal interpretations, culminates in destructive behaviour on the part of the analysand, which interrupts the cure. In the second stage, the patient's return to analysis enables the therapist to deepen his knowledge of the former's manic defence mechanisms. This has a decisive impact on his technique, which, this time, enables the favourable development of the analytical process and access to the elaboration of the patient's melancholic nucleus. The elaboration of the countertransference is very well described, as difficult as it is important in these cases. Pedro Guillem accompanies this description with observations on the technique he used, and this enriches our understanding of the case with this new perspective that presupposes an ‘a posteriori’ personal supervision.

[[RunningHead]]

The Discourse and the Psychoanalytic Method

Raquel Goldstein and Licenciada Fischbein

Licenciada Fischbein

Buenos Aires

(Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina)
Av. Las Heras 3901, Piso 15.
(1425) Buenos Aires, Argentina.

Licenciada Fischbein  

The chair, Dr Raquel Goldstein, opened the panel by commenting very briefly on its purpose, ‘to widen the conceptual foundations of psychoanalysis along its edges’, this endeavour being imperative due to the concerns emerging from praxis.

Myrta Casas, the first speaker, presented her paper, ‘Discourse and the psychoanalytic method’, based on both semiotic and pragmatic views. The author's proposal, synthesised in Benveniste's epigraph, clearly illustrated her ideas: discourse should be considered simultaneously as a message and a tool for action. Analytic transference is the element that enables the actualisation of the object both in the structuring and in the symptomatic aspects of subjective organisation. It is in the analyst's speech, she said, bearing all the properties inherent to discourse (word, gesture, intonation, syntax and silence) that the dimension of the object may be inferred, by causing it to get closer through the act of speaking.

Though Casas acknowledged the roots of discourse in various disciplines, psychoanalysis paves its own way, where the symbol acquires an entirely new connotation regarding the divided subject and the unconscious wish. From Casas's perspective, discourse, inhabited by the (unconscious) subject, is defined as a token of intertexruality where the dialogic vicissitudes develop an essentially dynamic and productive profile.

In her presentation, Casas articulated different theoretical lines of thought: Winnicott's ideas on the (unconscious) subject's need to create and lose the (transitional) object; together with Lacan's conceptualisation of the role of the Other and the three elements in Peirce's model (body—sensation and perception; object and interpretant). The subject gradually creates (is created as) an interpretant, while the icon and the index display the circulation of wish. She emphasised that in this interplay, ‘the presence of the wish in the Other is essential to materialise the work of the drive in its circulation (a reference to Peirce's firstness) and allows for experimenting on the object (secondness), resulting in a subjectivisation-symbolisation, i.e. the emergence of a wishing subject (thirdness)’.

In-Patient Treatment of Substance Abuse

Patricia Grieve and Lee Jaffe

Lee Jaffe

Madrid

3262 Holiday Court,
Suite 210 La Jolla,
California 92037-1807

Lee Jaffe  

The Moderator, Patricia Grieve, opened the panel by introducing Wilhelm Burian and Gerardo Rubinstein. She highlighted the importance of an opportunity to discuss the application of psychoanalytic concepts to the in-patient treatment of problems because, by and large, they are not seen as responsive to traditional psychoanalysis. Burian then described a psychoanalytically informed, one-year in-patient programme for substance abuse, during which patients participate in group work, group therapy, psychoanalytic psychotherapy and the therapeutic community of the treatment centre. The programme is based on the principle that treating drug dependency inevitably involves the treatment of antisocial personality as well. Burian emphasised the complementary interplay of all elements in the treatment programme, specifically pointing out the importance of not seeing one aspect of the treatment as more important than any of the others.

Turning to conceptual considerations, he described the original and modern psychoanalytic concepts of a stimulus barrier, emphasising that current formulations are of a far more complex protective mechanism that must regulate aggression as well as libido. In his view, the addict must rely on developmentally immature psychic structures to fend off primitive, threatening affects and psychic fragmentation, which is a goal that drugs can also temporarily accomplish. In fact, drugs serve multiple functions that include blocking the perception of disruptive affects, dulling the result of painful affects and providing affective nourishment. In his view, the effect of drugs is ‘not merely analgesia but a much more highly complex phenomenon including sadistic and masochistic wish-fulfilment, and the fantasised reunion and merging with the nurturing mother’. Essentially, he concludes that almost all drug-dependent people have a borderline personality organisation, as formulated by Kernberg.

Burian explicated three clinical concepts that are key to understanding the treatment model for drug addicts: the repetition of trauma, the addictive fantasy and the addictive relationship. Because the addict's capacities for self-regulation are severely impaired, there is an ongoing struggle to fend off fragmentation of the self-representation. One such struggle involves a fantasy of merger with an idealised maternal object that is temporarily enacted with the drugs. The enactment is accompanied by a fear of lost autonomy and hatred of the dependence, while at the same time, the addictive fantasy is used to re-experience and repeat a kind of excitement that accompanies emotional chaos, an excitement that is used to heighten a sense of being alive and vital.

responseSet.title: Reply to Norman and Thomson-Salo
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Norman and Thomson-Salo

Per-Olof Flink

Per-Olof Flink

Malmskillnadsgatan 60
S-111 38 Stockholm

Per-Olof Flink  

Dear Sirs,

Thomson-Salo has submitted a contribution (2001) to my discussion with Norman (Norman, 2001a, 2001b; Flink, 2001) endorsing Norman's ideas. Briefly her points are as follows.

First, Trevarthen's research (1998, 2000) proves convincingly that there is non-verbal communication with very small children (infants) that neuroscience and specialists doing research on small children consider meaningful.

Second, ‘Flink's statement that the infant is not yet able to understand any kind of “meaning” seems to me to be one that can only be made from outside the field’. She says also: ‘It seems important to reiterate that we cannot use the premises of another discipline to ignore the empirical research of the field in which we are working, as well as the clinical experience gathered there’.

Norman's reply to me (2001b) makes it clear that there are concepts in our debate that are based on differing assessments and implications. To these we assign terms such as ‘meaning’, ‘mental’ and even ‘concept’. Two other questions also pose difficulties: what does empiricism mean? And what constitutes the basis for empirical assertions? Cavell (1993), to whom I refer, and I have employed other bases for our use of the word ‘mental’ to those employed by Norman and Thomson-Salo. Norman's elucidation of these is the point of departure for this discussion. Definition of terms is important in scientific work and in communication.

Norman was provoked by my warning against too strict a reading of Freud—that is, interpreting his works as being literally true. I can understand his reaction. Perhaps Norman and others, too, think that in connection with his first reply to me I went too far in my comments about his dismissal of knowledge in areas other than psychoanalysis. Therefore I think there is reason to pay attention to what Thomson-Salo says about other disciplines in her interpolation. Is it not the case that knowledge from other disciplines and the value of such other disciplines depend on their arguments and possible empirical bases? Has psychoanalytical empiricism some special value? The reader must decide how this should be interpreted; for my part I find my warning entirely justified.

responseSet.title: William Gillespie ((1905-2001)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

William Gillespie ((1905-2001)

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920

Robert Wallerstein  

With the passing of William Gillespie, the eighth President of the International Psychoanalytical Association (1957-61), on 17 July 2001, not quite a month before his ninety-sixth birthday, the psychoanalytic world lost its major remaining link to the early and mid-twentieth-century era, when organised psychoanalysis was an informal, elite grouping dominated, often oppressively, by the members of the ‘I knew Freud’ club. And it was largely Gillespie's independence of mind, and his courtly but stubborn persistence to bring order and to ‘do the right thing’ that started the transition of the governing bodies of psychoanalysis into the modern democratic scientific and professional organisation that we have today, worthy of the intellectual discipline that it represents to the world.

William was born in post-Boxer Rebellion China in 1905, as the fourth child and only son, of parents who were both passionate missionaries of the Irish Presbyterian Church. He spent six of his first ten years in China—there had been a furlough period back in Ireland between 1908 and 1912—and grew with the conviction that he would expectedly succeed into the missionary life. The family returned to Britain in 1915 and settled in Edinburgh in order to obtain more formal educations for the children, and William lived there until 1930. In 1920, his father, who had returned to missionary work in China, was brutally murdered when confronting household intruders. This precipitated William's turn away from religion and from the God who had failed to preserve his good and faithful servant, and turned him towards a medical career, in which his oldest sister, nine years his senior, was already enrolled.

Although the family's means were now very modest, William was able, on the basis of his outstanding academic record, to obtain scholarships and bursaries and to graduate in medicine at the University of Edinburgh in 1929. By that time he had become deeply interested in the writings of psychoanalysis and, after short periods as a house physician, in both a general hospital and then a mental hospital, he applied for a travelling scholarship under the auspices of the University of Edinburgh, for a period of study in Vienna, ostensibly to study psychiatry and neurology, but really to pursue his interest in psychoanalysis. He received the award, and luckily his lack of knowledge of German—having studied by then just the first six Linguaphone records—was not challenged.

responseSet.title: Comments on Millar's ‘A psychoanalytic view of biblical myth’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comments on Millar's ‘A psychoanalytic view of biblical myth’

James Bott

James Bott

40 Margaret Street, Moonee Ponds
Melbourne, Australia 3068

James Bott  

Dear Sirs,

In his article ‘A psychoanalytic view of biblical myth’ (2001) David Millar presents some interesting and coherent ideas about particular group experiences and functioning and their connection with an idealised and punitive superego. In his concept of a ‘hardened myth’ I wonder if what he is actually describing is a particular group use of a myth. Bion suggests that myth, for the group, has the kind of role a model performs in scientific work: ‘an abstraction from an emotional experience or as a concretization of an abstraction’ (1984, p. 79). With reference to the themes of Millar's article, the emotional experience of living within the kind of ‘hardened’ state of a rigid defensive organisation, whether socially or individually constructed, is of a sense of certainty and security existing alongside feelings of instability and discontent. These feelings being both the product of and a reaction to the rigidity of the defensive organisation that also attacks the possibility of the emotional growth that might come from an attempt to integrate these disparate feelings. Instead myths become reinterpreted in an attempt to dispel difficult states of mind rather than as a way of exploring dilemmas. An example of this is how the myth of a messiah can be used to dispel a contemporary sense of discontent by holding out the hope of a future relief. I think it is possible to see the Jesus myth as an exploration of what this might actually mean and a kind of ironic reversal as it redefines the problem as freeing oneself from a particular state of mind, this state of mind including the wish for a messiah. To be more explicit, the death of Jesus could be seen as a ‘concretization of an abstraction’, the loss of the external ‘good object’. The resurrection myth understood as ‘an abstraction from an emotional experience’ represents the internalisation of a ‘good object’ in the recovery from the emotional experience of loss and disillusionment. In this understanding of the Jesus myth the notion of ‘the Kingdom of God’ represents a secure internalisation of a good object. I do not wish to make any particular case for Christian beliefs, however I do wish to point out how the myths of that belief system can be understood as an attempt to struggle with the difficulties of the depressive position. They have been used in the process of institutionalisation to claim special privilege and a promise of immortality but perhaps this can be seen as a concretisation of an emotional experience without any intervening process of thought or abstraction.

responseSet.title: Objects of Hope: Exploring Possibility and Limit in Psychoanalysis: Steven H. Cooper. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 2000. Pp. 321
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Objects of Hope: Exploring Possibility and Limit in Psychoanalysis

Steven H. Cooper. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 2000. Pp. 321

Barbara Stimmel

Barbara Stimmel

1185 Park Avenue
New York, New York 10128

Barbara Stimmel  

I opened Objects of Hope with a sense of hopefulness, having in the past enjoyed several of Steven Cooper's articles in the literature. This book is for the most part well reasoned, comprehensive and, at times, thought provoking. I found myself reading with increasing interest as Cooper invites us on a journey through comparative analytic theory as filtered through a relational prism. Its title, including hope, possibility and limits, will be part of my review since they are Cooper's links between different theoretical models.

Early on Cooper begins the work of setting the record straight regarding relational psychoanalysis.

It is unfortunate that many classical analysts still associate the major tenets of American relational theory with a kind of naive hopefulness. The use of transference authority, an emphasis on suggestion, and a circumvention of defense and resistance while implying at the same time that American relational theory de-emphasizes conflict as a ubiquitous intrapsychic phenomenon (p. 12).

This is a hopeful beginning to a book that maintains a tone of respect for misunderstanding coupled with the intention of correcting, educating and creating connectors between different schools of thought. Cooper goes on to point out what, for him, is the significant difference between classical Freudian theory and relational, object-relations-based schools of thought—all of which are implicit in the title. Freud based his model of the mind on a ‘kind of tragic utopia’ (p. 14). Cooper has full respect for the conflict model but believes there are more and other kinds of human interactions which lend themselves to a ‘quite different vision of human growth and possibility’ (p. 14).

However, Cooper soon slips into some misunderstanding of his own when choosing to quote Bowie, ‘Romancing the matter only a little, we could say that for Freud the past is “a character”, while the future is a cipher and something of a bore’ (p. 16). This reading of Freud is perplexing.

[[RunningHead]]

Le passé recomposé, pensées, mythes, praxis [The Past Reconstructed: Thoughts, Myths and Practice]

Francis Pasche. Foreword by Didier Anzieu. Collection: Le fil rouge. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1999. Pp. 270

Marie-Lise Roux

Marie-Lise Roux

137 Bld St Michel
75005 Paris, France

Marie-Lise Roux  

This volume is the third collection of articles by Francis Pasche, after A partir de Freud (1969) and Le sens de la psychanalyse (1988). Unfortunately it is a posthumous work as Francis Pasche died in 1996.

In 1950, he became an elected member of the SPP (Paris Psychoanalytic Society) and was its President from 1960 to 1964. For thirty years, he was the director of consultations at St Anne's hospital, in the famous department run by Professor Jean Delay, where he trained a large number of psychiatrists and psychoanalysts, of both French and foreign origin. During his time there, he created a supervision group for psychiatric cases for which standard treatment was not indicated—psychotics, perverts, patients with severe character disorders and so on.

Before this, he had had an unusual background as a pharmacist and philosopher, which not only left him with a wide culture but also a taste for clear, reality-oriented thinking.

His experience with psychotics and the psychiatric world led him to take a renewed interest in Freudian metapsychology and the origins of the structure of the mind. He never regarded psychoanalysis as a closed system or as an isolated discovery, but saw it as an extension of human knowledge that had remained unconscious in man; knowledge that had been partially revealed by religions—monotheistic religions in particular—and mythologies.

In an interview with Denys Ribas in 1992, which appears at the end of the book (but which may also be read as an introduction to the author's thinking), he admits with a touch of humour that he had been a ‘guardian of orthodoxy’ but only on the condition of remaining open to all schools of thought. This did not prevent him from resisting the intellectual modes of his time and from being an eloquent example of the liberty and creativity of psychoanalysis.

The book is divided up into six parts, each of which deals with a new theme.

537

responseSet.title: Plenary Session: ‘The Link and the Other’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Plenary Session

‘The Link and the Other’

Robert Wallerstein and Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Belvedere, CA

6 Narcissus Road
London NW61TH
UK
janemilton@compuserve.com

Jane Milton  

Following an introduction by the Chair, Robert Wallerstein, Isidoro Berenstein of Buenos Aires presented his paper as published (2001), with the addition of a short clinical vignette. Berenstein referred first to a solipsism in analytic theory. Most analysts, Berenstein thinks, regard the patient's transference as pre-determined in childhood, immutable and insensitive to new ‘inscriptions’ arising within current relationships. He went on to observe that most analysts apply traditional theories, but that occasionally an innovator like Melanie Klein, working with new sorts of patients (in her case children), is able to extend psychoanalytic concepts. Berenstein believes that his introduction of ‘couple and marital analysis’ has, in a similar way, revealed new clinical phenomena such that he is obliged to develop new conceptualisations.

Berenstein introduced a new term: ‘link’ (vinculo), an unconscious structure joining two or more persons. He added that, although other authors have used this word, his own use of the term is new. Berenstein's understanding appears to be that when analysts talk about person X's ‘internal object relationships’ manifest in a current relationship with person Y, they tend to be solipsistic, because they habitually fail to take into account the real, external presence of Y, differing as it does from X's preconceptions of Y, and hence they ignore Y's new effects on X. Berenstein stressed that we are profoundly influenced, from infancy onwards, both by the ‘imposition’ of unconscious stamps by others and by the stamp of our culture. The ‘link’ concept takes this into account. He contrasted the result of impositions to our ‘identifications’ with others, as he apparently takes this latter term to imply that the other becomes subsumed as an already-familiar part of the ego. Similarly, in Berenstein's view, the terms ‘introjection’ and ‘projection’ (if I have correctly understood the way Berenstein understands them) refer simply to the ego making something its own by imposing its own pre-existing content on it and incorporating it, rather than taking anything truly new and different in.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Epitre aux insensés [Epistle to the Insane].

Gérard Bayle Collection: Epitres. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1998. Pp. 189.

Albert Louppe and Andrew Weller

Andrew Weller

108 bis, Avenue du Sergent Maginot
35000 Rennes
France

Andrew Weller  

Like the other Epistles in this series, this work takes the form of an open letter which is also a reflection on the author's own trajectory. This Epistle to the Insane thus revolves around the themes of splitting, narcissism and the functions of the ego. By ‘insane’, Gérard Bayle means that which is capable of creating a ‘kind of contagiousness affecting the other’ and results in a loss of ‘the direction of meaning, of the senses and of common sense’ (p. 17) when the processes of symbolisation are undermined in one of the protagonists of the treatment. Apart from the metapsychological rigour and pertinence of the clinical illustrations, while reading the book one gets a good sense of the author's creativity and freedom of tone. He is a titular member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society, a former co-director of the Revue francaise de psychanalyse and secretary for the Congress of French-speaking analysts. In addition, Gérard Bayle has written numerous psychoanalytical articles and books including Formation au psychodrame analytique (1988), ‘Les clivages et les défaillances de la fonction synthétique du Moi’ (1996) and Le trésor des phobies (1999). In the introduction, the author explains that the aim of his study is to reflect on the splitting of the ego (Freud, 1938) and the process of identifying this in treatment on the basis of the fluctuations in the analyst's sense of identity in the session or even between sessions. The author develops a fine and pertinent analysis of the countertransferential dimension of the relation of each practitioner to the theory, and to his own process of theorisation—the bedrock of his analytic identity. Both excessive complacency towards theoretical constructions, as well as the rejection of them, are signs of temporary splitting in the analyst's functioning. The perception, over time, of the gap between his usual functioning in analysis and this ‘other aspect of himself, which is full of certainty’ reveals failures of symbolisation in the analytic process.

responseSet.title: On Freud: Michael S. Trupp, M.D. Belmont, California: Wadsworth, Thomson Learning. 2000. Pp. 84.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Freud

Michael S. Trupp, M.D. Belmont, California: Wadsworth, Thomson Learning. 2000. Pp. 84.

Elizabeth Auchincloss MD

Elizabeth Auchincloss

525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
USA

Elizabeth Auchincloss  

The Wadsworth Philosophers Series, a division of Thomson Learning Inc. edited by philosopher Daniel Kolak from The William Paterson University, consists of a matching set of about forty slim volumes (all fewer than 100 pages), each a discourse ‘on’ a single important thinker from ancient times to the present. The series website, ‘philosophy. wadsworth.com’, lists titles ranging from On The Buddha and On Socrates to On Rorty and On Quine. Included is also On Freud, by Michael S. Trupp. The Philosophers Series promises not only to meet ‘the practical needs and demands of present-day college students’ (website) but also ‘to empower the reader when analyzing and discussing original works’ (inside cover). Unfortunately, Trupp's book is unlikely to meet either the practical needs of young readers struggling to understand Freud's thinking, or the more complex needs of students attempting to understand Freud's place in the Western intellectual tradition. As an explication of Freud's ideas, Trupp's introduction is idiosyncratic and often misleading. As a meditation ‘on’ Freud, Trupp's discourse is overwritten to the point of being exhausting rather than empowering.

On Freud consists of 84 pages divided into a preface, an introduction and five chapters entitled: ‘Who You Are’, ‘Trauma’, ‘Words and Things’, ‘Bilaterality’ and ‘A Mirror Cannot be a Photographic Plate’. The most striking feature of this introduction to Freud is that Trupp allows the reader to hear so little from the great man himself. While each chapter begins with a quote from Freud, most often from The Interpretation of Dreams, these quotes are never explained or even referred to in the subsequent text, and are therefore quickly forgotten. If Trupp does not let us hear directly from Freud, neither does he tell us much about him.

960

[[RunningHead]]

Errant Selves: A Casebook of Misbehavior

Arnold Goldberg. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 2000. Pp. 212.

Edward Khantzian MD

Edward Khantzian

365 East Street
Tewksbury, MA 01876
USA

Edward Khantzian  

This volume, as the subtitle states, is ‘a casebook of misbehavior’. At the outset the authors propose that a range of deviant behaviours, including perversions, delinquency and addictions are rooted in attempts to deal with or ward off painful affect states or dysphoria. Contrary to the antipathy such cases more often engender, the authors of the book empathically explore how such behaviours split a person in two, abhorring or disavowing the part of self that needs to be deviant. The editor, Arnold Goldberg, and his collaborators display the heuristic value and utility of self-psychology in understanding such individuals.

963

responseSet.title: Remembering a depressive primary object
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Remembering a depressive primary object

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue, #2220
Chicago, Illinois 60601
USA
belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Dear Sirs,

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber and Rolf Pfeifer (2002, 83(1): 3-33) have done the readers

1184

of this Journal a genuine service by providing a clear exposition of the latest models of how memory functions, articulating the advances of these ‘embodied’ models from cognitive neuroscience over earlier ones based on computer science (‘classical cognitive science’). In the process, the reader gains a sense of the historical progression of such modelling, a movement from computer science to cognitive science to cognitive neuroscience.

Two additional qualities of their presentation, beyond its clarity, stand out as particularly helpful. First, the authors anchor the theoretical information in clinical material, enabling us as readers to explore abstract (and perhaps novel) concepts in a context that is meaningful to us. Second, the authors bring a critical reading to these models and their usefulness for psychoanalytic practice. As they themselves conclude, one wants to avoid ‘the danger for psychoanalysis of an over-hasty adjustment to viewpoints which are culturally and “scientifically” accepted but foreign to our profession’ (p. 29). It is in light of this concern that I make the following comments about ‘categories’ and ‘value’ (a concept about which the authors acknowledge that there is ‘extended debate’ (p. 18, fn. 9). As the authors point out, earlier computer science-generated models (cf. Rumelhart, Lindsay & Norman; Schank) were problematic. Those models postulated memory as static storage places for large databases that were structured by external experts (programmers), whose goals were to enable efficient access, search and retrieval of data, as well as the inference of new data. Early structures that focused on internal relations among data (such as ‘isa’ for taxonomy) were replaced by meta-structures such as ‘scripts’ (Schank & Abelson), ‘frames’ (Minsky) and ‘schemes’ (Rumelhart).

Unfortunately, more structure led to less efficiency and this approach was replaced by neural or connectionist models, designed to be capable of learning and generating structures from experience. These second-generation models sought not only to simulate memory functions but also to do so in ways that simulated (with silicon) how the neuronal architecture accomplishes these functions. Edelman's Neural Darwinism extends the neurological basis for cognitive models by basing models on the neurobiology of actual brains, rather than on computers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La vie opératoire [Operative life].

Claude Smadja. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2001. Pp. 272.

Alain Fine

Alain Fine

1 rue Mizon
75015 PARIS
France

Alain Fine  

As its title indicates, this book is about operative functioning (l'opératoire), a concept theorised by French psychoanalysts involved in psychosomatic research during the nineteen sixties. The subtitle, ‘psychoanalytic studies’, suggests an approach that claims to lie squarely within the psychoanalytic theoretical field, at the heart of Freudian metapsy-chology, in contradistinction to other models used for understanding psychosomatic phenomena, as well as models that are somewhat further removed from this field. The author sets out the different models in his introduction, mentioning, in particular, the American studies by F. Dunbar and F. Alexander, without, however, invalidating them.

This concept of operative functioning underlies the models of somatic processes advanced by Pierre Marty—concepts and models which have also found currency outside France. It is a concept which has to be defined within an epistemological context, and over the course of time has proved to be of paradigmatic value in French circles. As the author points out, it is worth noting that some of these approaches are not dissimilar to the hypotheses of Sifneos on alexithymia, with which American psychoanalysts will be familiar.

Claude Smadja, a psychiatrist by training who has since become a full-time psychoanalyst (titular member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society), has also chosen to work at the Institut de psychosomatique (IPSO) together with the Pierre Marty Hospital.

1217

Response to Tamara Feldman

Georges Abraham

Georges Abraham

Avenue Krieg 13
1208 Geneve
Switzerland
psycabr@caramail.com

Georges Abraham  

Dear Sirs,

First of all, I would like to thank Dr Feldman for her appreciation of my paper on ‘The psychodynamics of orgasm’, and also for its ‘message’: to encourage further psychoanalytical research and reflections in this field. In a more specific and detailed aspect, I am grateful to Dr Feldman because she underlines what I consider as the main issue of the entire problem concerning orgasm. In other words. Dr Feldman points out that the female orgasm seems to be wholly erotic as opposed to the male orgasm, which is more linked to reproduction because of sperm ejaculation and the fact that someone (in this case my patient) remains orgastically responsive despite a profound conflict around the orgasm itself.

1436

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Annie Anargyros-Klinger's ‘Depression and Leo Tolstoy’

Neville Symington

Neville Symington

88 B, Warragal Road
Turramurra
Sydney
NSW 2074
Australia
nevillesymington@hotmail.com

Neville Symington  

Dear Sirs,

I would like to make some comments on this article, which appeared in Int. J. Psycho-Anal. in April 2002 (Vol. 83, Pt. 2).

First, it should be noted that there is a pejorative tone in the author's description of Tolstoy. For instance, she starts by saying that ‘The prestige and fame of the great novelist have left the vast majority of his readers with the unfailing image of an artist of genius. And yet…’. The implication here is clearly that this image of Tolstoy as an artist of genius is a misapprehension. Later she comments that ‘He devoted himself to writing innumerable and turgid religious and philosophical works…’. The adjective ‘turgid’ suggests that these works lack worth, yet the two essays from this period to which the author is referring, ‘What is religion?’ and ‘What is art?’ are two of the most brilliant essays ever written upon the essence of religion and the essence of art.

Elsewhere in the article, the author says that ‘The interest in following the thread of depression throughout the course of his life and works is that of glimpsing the particular path that led Tolstoy to use this banal destiny to create his unique work’. The adjective ‘banal’ carries the same pejorative tone used throughout the article. The reader is, therefore, invited to believe that Tolstoy's works are not what they purport to be; they are flawed and should not hold the place that they do within the canons of literature. The author goes on to explain why the general opinion is misplaced.

She offers several reasons and her explanation leads to the second point that I would like to mention here. First, she says that Tolstoy was world-weary and depressed. That he suffered from depression is incontestable but ‘world-weary’ is not an adjective I would ever use of him.

1438

[[RunningHead]]

The Patient–Therapist Relationship: The Field of Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytical Psychotherapy. [La relación paciente terapeuta: El campo del psicoanálisis y la psicoterapia psicoanalítica.]

Joan Coderch. Ed. Paidós and Fundació Vidal i Barraquer. 2001. Pp. 271.

Guillermo Bodner

Guillermo Bodner

Josep Irla i Bosch 2, 7-2
08034 Barcelona
Spain
gbodner@intercom.es

Guillermo Bodner  

Joan Coderch has for many years studied the conceptual aspects of the theory and practice of psychoanalysis. His works have appeared in articles and books, including an exhaustive volume on interpretation (see IJPA, 1997, 78: 615-17). In this new book, Coderch investigates the analytical link, conceived as an interpersonal space, in which the relationship unfolds through dialogue.

This is the perspective of a Kleinian-trained psychoanalyst with vast clinical and teaching experience, and Coderch has provided some important reflections on some of the trends in current psychoanalysis. He tackles controversial issues with clarity, notably the philosophical bases of these debates. The exposition is well set out, along with some slight nuances in the face of classical theory. The clinical vignettes at the close of each chapter clearly illustrate the author's theoretical criteria.

This work aims to offer a critical vision of the risk of dogmatism that schools of psychoanalysis run, and which may result in practices that obstruct the therapeutic function and creative spirit. The author comments that in recent decades dissatisfaction has arisen with the traditional model, in which the analyst is the healthy adult who recognises reality and the patient the sick person whose distortions need to be rectified by the analyst. As a consequence of this, many analysts prefer ‘a more egalitarian position, not to be confused with symmetrical’ (p. 22), in which the interpersonal relationship takes on greater importance.

1473

responseSet.title: From Knowledge to Competence: Reflections on Theoretical Work
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Knowledge to Competence

Reflections on Theoretical Work

Jurgen Reeder

Jurgen Reeder

Gammelgårdsvägen 15C, S-112 64, Stockholm, Sweden — jurgen.reeder@telia.com

Jurgen Reeder  

Psychoanalytic theory, theoretical work, metapsychology, experience.

In this paper, the author maintains that the language of the clinical situation is and should be that of common-sense psychology. The author argues that the languages of clinical theory and metapsychology are at a distinctive distance from clinical language. In fact, no theory could be produced, should this distance not be upheld. The function of clinical theory is to act as a corrective to the tendency of common-sense psychology to fall back into the banalities and prejudices of general ideology. In addition, the function of metapsychology is said to be that of scrutinising clinical theory and defining its range of operation. Due to the claimed distance between the common-sensical language of the clinical situation and the language of theory, the analyst must utilise specific ‘techniques’ to appropriate the theoretical material and make it part of his competence and capacity for psychoanalytic action. This aspect of theoretical work is given the name ‘assimilation’ and, in turn, relies upon a process where theoretical concepts are ‘deconstructed’ as to their knowledge content.

responseSet.title: On ‘The analyst at work’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On ‘The analyst at work’

Judith Chused MD

Judith Chused

1805 Randolph Street NW
Washington DC 20011-5339
USA
jchused@chused.com

Judith Chused  

Dear Sirs,

In the section ‘The Analyst at Work’ of the most recent issue of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, material is presented by Elias M. da Rocha Barros from the analysis of a woman who complained, rather bitterly, that it was her attractive, seductive, charming appearance and behaviour which caught her husband's, father's and analyst's attention, and that it was extremely painful that they and others failed to respond to the ‘inner person’. Both the analyst whose material it was, as well as the two discussants, did an excellent job of understanding the material in terms of her longing to be recognised as having intrinsic value and her distress over separation and sense of aloneness. However, all three authors appeared to accept her complaints that her seductive, attractive and charming behaviour was an external attribute that she felt compelled to adopt to be ‘special’, but which she inwardly despised. I wonder how much these analysts’ acceptance of her complaints about others’ response to her seductiveness was a collusion with her defensive denial of her pleasure in her capacity to seduce—a mixed pleasure, to be sure, but a pleasure none the less. My impression is that ‘the lady doth protest too much’, which suggests a defensive retreat from oedipal rivalry with the colourless mother which coexists with her pre-oedipal longings.

1188

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in China
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in China

Teresa Yuan

Teresa Yuan

M.T. de Alvear 2127 P. 6° A
1122 Buenos Aires

Teresa Yuan  

Dear Sirs,

There has been a widespread welcome and interest in psychoanalysis in China during the past decade. I would appreciate being able to communicate something of this to our international colleagues through your kind agency. I shall summarise some landmarks concerning the history of our science in China.

The first writings based on Freud's ideas entered China during the second decade of the last century through scholars who strove to employ the new cultural conceptions of science within the context of the social crisis prevailing in China at the time. The social reformer Zhang Shizhao, an intellectual who held important positions in the government both before and after the Revolution (1949), was the only Chinese scholar to correspond with Freud (there is a letter dated 27 May 1929).

The first translations into Chinese of Freud's works date back to about 1920. An Autobiographical Study (1925) was translated from German by Zhang Shizhao and published in 1930. Dr Bingham Dai, a psychiatrist who trained under Harry Stack Sullivan in the US, taught psychotherapy at the Beijing Union Medical University from 1935 to 1939, but had to go into exile after the Japanese army's invasion.

In the beginning, Freud's thought was used to bolster social reform ideas. Psychoanalysis's early impetus decreased prior to the 1949 Revolution and it was proscribed during the Cultural Revolution. In spite of this it remained alive if latent, emerging once again in recent years. Psychiatrist Professor Zhong Youping applied the technique based on the analysis of unconscious conflict, and described this in his book Psychoanalysis in China, written in Chinese and published in 1988.

Since the opening up of China in 1978, reports by psychoanalysts who visited China have been published in scientific magazines. However interesting and original these reports were, they provided a limited perspective based as they were on isolated impressions and opinions, and they did tend to reveal a lack of knowledge of Chinese thinking, society and ancestral culture. At the beginning of my own work in China in 1995 I was not surprised to find a different reality from that portrayed in the reports I had read. My relationship to Chinese culture and my experience in dealing with Chinese patients in Argentina had made me increasingly aware of the importance of bearing in mind factors such as cultural differences and the influence of social criteria in the understanding of psychic structure and individuals’ behaviour.

Florence Guignard

Square d'Orléans

75009 Paris
France
flogui2@club-internet.fr

Florence Guignard  

I

Jean Guillaumin is a member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society, professor emeritus at the University Lumière of Lyon, as well as the author of several books and numerous articles. He is a remarkable thinker in the field of contemporary psychoanalysis and has a special interest in the ways in which clinical practice and metapsychology are interrelated. With his elegant style of writing and a genuine concern to communicate with the reader, Guillaumin is skilful at relating a variety of psychoanalytic concepts in a rigorous and original way. In particular, he has linked up the retroactive effect of trauma with psychic temporality, the vicissitudes of the object with those of the negative in psychic reality, and the manifestations of transference and counter-transference with those of symbolisation in analytic treatment.

Within the dialectic specific to this author the present study, Adolescence et désenchantement, forms a counterpoint to an earlier study entitled ‘The archaeology of the father: between the anxiety of a presence and the metaphor of an absence, the “father of individual prehistory”’ (Guillaumin, 2001). While seeking to revise certain of his earlier works, which he succeeds here in enriching considerably, the author examines the vicissitudes involved in the intersection of two problematic themes specific to the adolescent. The first is the personal disenchantment linked to the process of the mourning of childhood; and the second is the social re-enchantment connected with an adherence to values, aims and collective dependencies—a movement aimed at avoiding depression.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Recrimination in the Analytic Situation

A Hypothesis about Its Influence on Psychoanalytical Groups

Manuel Gálvez and Jorge Maldonado

Jorge Maldonado

Dr Manuel José Gálvez, Av. Coronel Diaz 2760, 1° C, (1425) Buenos Aires, Argentina—
manueljg@interserver.com.ar

Dr Jorge Luis Maldonado, Juez Estrada 2725, (1425) Buenos Aires, Argentina—
jmaldonad@arnet.com.ar

Jorge Maldonado  

recrimination, ideal, superego, framework, symbolisation, interpretation, paradox, institutions, mourning, rule of abstinence.

This paper1 deals with certain distortions in communication generated by mutual recrimination that is the result of disturbances in the ideal agencies of both parties. Although the ideal ego, superego and ego ideal participate equally in reproach, it is the latter which is the most decisive. In clinical experience, recrimination may easily colour the analytic dialogue. In such cases, interpretation loses its sense of clarification and another type of dialogue replaces it. There, words are used to take possession of the other, for its autonomy is a threat to the static character of the pathology of mourning. The problem of recrimination has also tainted the development of psychoanalysis, to the point of disrupting the process of discovery itself. This paper deals with repercussions in the psychoanalytic movement and also in the elements that constitute its structure. Finally, different variations and disturbances in the psychoanalytic ideal are considered, as well as the involvement of the psychoanalytic institution in preserving or transforming the ideal. Here the importance of institutions and institutional ideals is emphasised. Finally, we suggest that ideals either encourage or hinder the working through of individual and collective mourning.

Comments on the Case of Nina

Clifford Yorke

Clifford Yorke

Fieldings
South Moreton
Nr Didcot
Oxon
UK
clifford.yorke@virgin.net

Clifford Yorke  

Preliminary

When, as part of this new series of psycho-analytic discussions, I agreed to comment on a colleague's case presentation, consisting of parts of the patient's history followed by accounts of two analytic sessions, I realised I would be taking on a very difficult task. I was pleased to know that nothing would be required by way of ‘supervision’: what was wanted was some indication of how one might have tackled the kind of analytic material or problems presented. But that is still a hazardous undertaking. The analyst has available years of detailed material inaccessible to the commentator, who will have to fall back, however unwillingly, on at least some speculation.

As a student, I always felt more than a little dissatisfied if, when asked by a seminar leader to present a case, I was expected to spend five minutes on the history and discuss two therapeutic sessions at length. And, of course, not just the seminar leader, but also the entire class, acted as supervisors and always knew far more about the case than I did. Yet so many of the interpretations that entered their heads could be discounted by information they did not possess at the moments they made them.

If ‘supervision’ is no part of the present plan, it does have one disadvantage that did not affect the clinical seminars of the kind I have recalled. There is no opportunity for participants to discuss the case with each other: the printed page asks no questions; is impervious to comment; neither confirms nor denies, refutes nor concurs; cannot be drawn into dispute; and fills in no gaps in the history.

Whatever the difficulties, Dr Giovanna Ambrosio tells a moving story and tells it well: one does have a picture of the plight into which a little girl was thrown. It is not difficult to imagine the confusion that must have blighted her early development, affected her psychological progress through life, and remained a burning and unresolved source of uncertainty about who she was and why that burden was visited upon her, with no answers available until she sought psychoanalytic help. If I read the material correctly, that is the kind of impression that the analyst's initial account leaves me with.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Gulliver's Virtual Travels’

A Review of the Browser's Delight: Pep Cd Rom Archive 1, Version 3*

Robert Tyson

Robert Tyson

5174 Chelsea Street
La Jolla
CA 92037-7908 USA
robert@tysonz.com

Robert Tyson  

I begin with the conclusion: the PEP CD ROM is a magnificent tool for anyone interested in learning who has published what, when and where in the journals of the psycho-analytic literature from 1920 to 1998. With this tool, one may also annotate, print out various sorts of selections, leave bookmarks and perform a variety of other tasks. The PEP CD ROM is here to stay, unlike earlier efforts at indexing the psychoanalytic literature, and its use has become progressively easier with each of the three iterations or versions, with updates to come. The user needs to accommodate to the demands of a software program (Folio Views) that is rather more complex and different from the usual word processing and email programs, but it can richly repay the explorer's efforts in accessing the PEP database, particularly with the aid of the improved help facilities that are provided. The price seems expensive at first, but may be a bargain when compared with the increasing costs of journal subscriptions and the considerable effort it would take to gain access to the scope of the psychoanalytic literature provided by this product just once, let alone whenever one wishes. The PEP CD ROM Archive 1 Version 3 is a central and essential part of every psychoanalyst's library, of which it may actually constitute the core.

Now to begin this review, based on experience with the Windows version, the Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing (PEP) company's advertising over the past few years has made its product familiar to many psychoanalysts. In journals, on the internet and in demonstrations at conferences around the world, it is promoted as ‘The perfect research tool!’ That may indeed be so, but I imagine that, for many psychoanalysts, a more apt description would be ‘The Browser's Delight’. It is, in fact, the electronic recreation of the stacks of a library devoted to psychoanalysis. In these virtual stacks, the user may zero in on a particular bibliographic target and then allow curiosity freedom to digress agreeably into adjacent areas, limited only by time and by one's associative powers and available skills to use the instrumental software. Those skills come with application in the same way that riding a bicycle or driving a car becomes second nature to most of us. It takes a little practice and use. I will focus on my experience of the initial contacts with PEP CD ROM, Version 3.

[[RunningHead]]

Hat och kärlek i psykoanalytiska institutioner: En professions dilemma [Hatred and love in psychoanalytic institutions: The dilemma of a profession].

Jurgen Reeder Stockholm: Brutus Östlings Bokförlag Symposion. 2001. Pp. 397.

Anders Zachrisson

Anders Zachrisson

Prof Dahls Gate 18
N-0353 Oslo
Norway

Anders Zachrisson  

This is an important book. It concerns how the psychoanalytic system of education affects psychoanalytic institutes and organisations, and, conversely, how the organisation functions as a conservative agent against amendments of the education system. Reeder's views are reminiscent of Kernberg's rough discussion of the institutional problems of psychoanalytic education in 1986, where he introduced four models of organisation (the art academy, the trade school, the religious retreat and the university college), and concluded that:

… a serious discrepancy exists between the prevalent, explicitly formulated or implicitly acknowledged aims of psychoanalytic education and the dominant organizational structures that characterize psychoanalytic institutes. In my view, while psychoanalytic educators think they are transmitting what is both an art and a science, they have structured their institutes so that they correspond most closely to a combination of the technical school and the theological seminary (Kernberg, 1986, p. 812).

Reeder's central thesis is that there are aspects of the analytic education that have detrimental effects on the candidates, the institutes, and on psychoanalysis as a whole. These effects, he claims, are antithetical to the spirit we aim at in analytical work. Furthermore, he believes that the tripartite educational system still prevalent in most institutes, consisting of training analysis, supervision and seminars, which has its roots in the Berlin Institute of the 1920s, is formed in a way that gives the candidates an inhibiting, professional superego. It also causes the development of what he calls an institutional superego system in analytic organisations. These phenomena interact in a dialectical way, forming a structure which he calls the psychoanalytic superego complex.

727

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

Balsamo, M. (ed.) (2000). Soggetti al delirio. Elaborazioni del dolore e percorsi della cura. [Subjects of Delusions. Elaborations on Pain and Paths of Treatment.] Milan: Angeli. Pp. 95.

The book deals with the theme of the multiple functions of delusion: the tearing but also the restitching of the psychic fabric, the moment of depersonalisation and at the same time of the acquisition of subjectivity, when a perspective of the self can begin to exist, the failure of as well as the search for representation. Through clinical cases and theoretical considerations much space is given to the role of the Other in the patient's delusions: his mind can be taken over by elements not belonging to the self, in the same way as it can be the subject of somebody else's delusional system.

Bolognini, S. (ed.) (2000). Il sogno cento anni dopo. [The Dream One Hundred Years On.] Turin: Boringhieri. Pp. 450.

One hundred years after the publication of Freud's The Interpretation of Dreams, this book discusses Freudian dream theory in the light of new ideas on psychoanalytic treatment and theory. The volume consists of numerous historical and clinical contributions. The issues relating to theory and technique, which add depth to the analysis of the structural and relational elements of the dream, are also presented in this volume within such contexts as the theory of the self, the neurosciences and the arts.

Crafoord, C. (2001). Införlivande. Om den udvecklande dialogens betingelser. [Embodiments. On the conditions for the evolving diaglogue.] Stockholm: Natur och Kultur. Pp. 285.

The author links together his model of psychic development with the recent neurophysiological observations and new theories about evolution. In this way he demonstrates that the old antagonism between a psychoanalytical and a biological viewpoint can be overcome. In the final chapter the author looks back on his many professional years as a psychoanalyst and a psychiatrist and reflects over the body, the dream, the language, ageing and the human need for religions. He also discusses how his idea of an ‘evolving dialogue’ can be applied in a pedagogical and therapeutic context.

responseSet.title: A Hypothetical Reconstruction Unveiling a Family Secret
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Hypothetical Reconstruction Unveiling a Family Secret

Viviana Minerbo

Viviana Minerbo

Address for correspondence:
No. 5, Ave. St. Honoré d'Eylau
75116, Paris
France
102565.100@compuserve.com

Viviana Minerbo  

In this commentary, I shall endeavour to make a hypothetical reconstruction of Nina's personal history as related in Giovanna Ambrosio's clinical material.

She begins by presenting Nina's most unusual family background. At her grand-parents' suggestion, and for family reasons, such as assuring equal inheritance to both sons, Nina was given up for adoption to her father's brother and his childless wife. At the time, she was the first-born and only child of her biological parents, although the mother was now pregnant again; three other daughters were born to her natural parents, whom they kept. They all lived together in one big house. The family, thus, consisted of two sets of parents whom Nina called ‘mummy’ and ‘daddy’, respectively, and four sisters. I wonder what family pathology made everybody so compliant to this unusual request. Nobody voiced any opposition or took a stand. Was this compliance due to cultural factors that demand obedience to one's parents or did it come out of a need to share the inheritance legally to avoid feelings of envy, guilt and voracity? This was the family secret—known consciously or unconsciously by all members of this family but not voiced, discussed or questioned. Years later, Nina stated that, in reality, she felt she was nobody's daughter.

In such a case of actual, objective trauma, one could be misled by the external circumstances, ignoring the role played by Nina's own psyche in collaborating to keep the family secret by not questioning this peculiar family set-up. Supposing amnesia had set in when she was given way for adoption at the age of 18 months, she could not objectively know much about her biological parents. But her history shows that she did have unconscious knowledge of their being part of her internal history. They made themselves present in her life by the confusion created in her mind, which was aggravated by living with both them and the adoptive parents; confusion held on to and which she was unable to unravel before her analysis took place.

responseSet.title: Changing Conceptions of Psychoanalysis. The Legacy of Merton Gill: Doris K. Silverman and David L. Wolitzky. The Analytic Press. 2000. Pp. 336
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changing Conceptions of Psychoanalysis. The Legacy of Merton Gill

Doris K. Silverman and David L. Wolitzky. The Analytic Press. 2000. Pp. 336

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

Cornell University
418 East 71st Street, Suite 41
New, York, NY 10021
USA

Robert Michels  

Merton Max Gill died on 13 November 1994 at the age of 80. His first publication was in 1941 on malingering; his last book, more than fifty years later, was entitled Psychoanalysis in Transition. Between these he co-authored Diagnostic Psychological Testing with David Rappaport and Roy Schafer; wrote on hypnosis with Margaret Brennan, on the initial interview with Newman, Redlich and Somers, on metapsychology with Rappaport; discussed psychoanalysis and psychotherapy with Bob Wallerstein and others; discussed metapsychology with Phil Holzman, Bob Holt, George Klein and others; described strategies for systematic empirical research in psychoanalysis; wrote his seminal monographs on the analysis of the transference with Irwin Hoffman, and was a major voice in every important psychoanalytic dialogue in North America. In this commemorative volume Doris K. Silverman and David L. Wolitzky have collected and lovingly edited papers by fourteen of Gill's colleagues. Although Gill himself is not a contributor and the subject matter ranges quite broadly, Gill emerges vividly both personally and intellectually, and the book is far more coherent than are most edited volumes. The authors are often continuing dialogues with Gill that in some cases have been ongoing for decades, and his views emerge quite clearly as the objects of their disagreements.

Gill loved dialogue. He developed his ideas in argument with others. His voice was powerful—literally as well as metaphorically. (I recall him in the far corner of a large conference hall speaking without a microphone and remember thinking that anyone who sounded that much like Moses had an unfair advantage in debate.) He wrote long, carefully reasoned letters to his friends and would respond regularly to those who could maintain a discourse with him. His thoughts and his words had great impact on the profession. He also had unusual capacity to change his mind; often the clearest and most effective statements on both sides of a debate in psychoanalysis were written by Merton Gill, although generally separated by a decade or two.

[[RunningHead]]

Otto Weininger: Sex, Science, and Self in Imperial Vienna

Chandak Sengoopta. Chicago: University of Chicago Press. 2000. Pp. 239.

Léon Wurmser

Léon Wurmser

904 Crestwick Road
Towson
MD 21286
USA

Léon Wurmser  

The task of the historian in writing about his subjects, says Tacitus, is to do this ‘sine ira et studio’—that is, without hatred and self-centredness. Dr Sengoopta has admirably fulfilled this obligation. He gives us a very interesting study of one of the most peculiar, even bizarre, authors of the turn of the last century—a philosopher who wrote one book, Sex and Character, which proved immensely influential, though pernicious in its effects. The book was begun when Weininger was still only a late adolescent—an immensely gifted and erudite young writer, but of a fiercely ideological bent and full of prejudice, who, though himself of Jewish origin, was so profoundly anti-Semitic that he was liked by the Nazis. He was deeply influenced by, and sympathetic to Freud and Breuer, but was the ostensible cause for the break between Fliess and Freud. He was respected by many early on, yet committed suicide at the age of 23, after having completed his book and converted to Protestantism, by shooting himself in a room he had rented in the house where Beethoven had died.

Dr Sengoopta is lecturer at the Wellcome Unit for the History of Medicine, University of Manchester, but began this work at Johns Hopkins University. His book is well written, thoroughly researched, critical and fascinating. It opens up for the present reader an illuminating view of the attitude prevailing at that time in Central Europe and exposed in the pernicious equation of the triple ‘dangers’ of sex, femininity and Judaism: Weininger's book is ‘a mélange of science, bigotry, philosophy, personal anxiety, and cultural politics … a disorganised, fragmented, and cacophonous text’, as Sengoopta describes it (p. 156).

responseSet.title: Response to Ahumada
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Ahumada

Bernard Cullen

Bernard Cullen

63 Maryville Park
Belfast BT9 6LQ
Northern Ireland
b.cullen@qub.ac.uk

Bernard Cullen  

Dear Sirs,

I was dismayed that Professor Ahumada felt it necessary to register a public complaint about my review of the internet discussion of his paper ‘The rebirth of the idols’. To take the most generous point of view, his concerns seem to be based on multiple misreadings of my text.

Professor Ahumada would appear to be unable to distinguish between ‘postmodern rebuttal’ and honest-to-goodness intellectual disagreement with his views, as expressed by the vast majority of the contributors to the discussion. My review is an even-handed digest of the wide-ranging views of the twenty-eight contributors to the online discussion of his paper (forty-five contributions in all, including three of his own). As Professor Ahumada should know, since he presumably read all the online contributions, most of what he complains about reflects the views of others, which I simply summarised and reported. It would appear that he has forgotten the ancient plea not to shoot the messenger. Although he is free to feel that some of those contributors either misunderstood or failed to grasp the full intellectual force of his paper, I completely refute the charge that my review contains either distortions or inaccuracies.

As soon as the paper appeared on the web, I solicited online responses to it by writing to a wide range of colleagues worldwide and expressing my personal view that it was an important paper, dealing with worrying issues for the future of psychoanalysis in both the academy and the consulting room. As someone who divides his professional life between the academy and the consulting room, I was very happy to do what I could to generate a vigorous discussion of the issues raised by the paper. However, that should not imply an endorsement of the arguments presented in the paper. And surely Professor Ahumada would not wish the reviewer to be totally excluded from the subsequent discussion. While for the most part I confined myself to reflecting and summarising the views of the contributors, on a few occasions I associated myself with some of the criticisms offered by the discussants, and I do not believe there is anything either unusual or improper about this. I did not offer any opinion on subjects with which I am unfamiliar (e.g. the work of Laplanche), but I did feel at liberty to comment on Professor Ahumada's interpretations of writers with whose works I like to think I have a good working familiarity: in the cases of Aristotle (on rhetoric), Nietzsche, Heidegger and Kojéve, I registered my considered view that his readings are ill-informed and fundamentally flawed. Although knowing nothing of my philosophical or theoretical views, Professor Ahumada has come to the unwarranted conclusion that this is a consequence of my ‘postmodern urges’.
