Pourquoi Les Hommes Ont Peur Des Femmes? [Why are Men Afraid of Women?]

Jean Cournut. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2001. Pp. 304.

J. Rabain and Andrew Weller

Andrew Weller

5 bis rue La Tasse
75116 Paris
France
j-f.rabain@wanadoo.fr

Andrew Weller  

Why are men afraid of women? asks Jean Cournut. And why does this fear seem to have as its corollary the domination of women by men? We are familiar with Zarathustra's warning: ‘You are going to see women? Don't forget the whip!’ The whip is needed to defend oneself against the figure of the shrew, who is not always tamed, or against the no less dangerous figure of Jocasta. Is not sadomasochism—said to be the best cement for couples—also an indirect consequence of this fear?

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Tomas Böhm's ‘Sara in her Fourth Analytic Year’

John Steiner

John Steiner

28 Park Drive
London
NW11 7SP
UK
johnsteiner@onetel.net.uk

John Steiner  

Dr Böhm's clear and detailed account of his session allows us to compare different approaches to analysis. I think that my style of work differs from his chiefly in the importance it gives to the analysis of transference: I also tend to pay close attention to the effect a patient's problems and defences have on the analytic atmosphere and particularly on the capacity of the analyst to work freely and productively.

Dr Böhm explains that the way he prefers to work is to wait ‘for cues from my or the analysand's own awareness before interpreting transference’. By contrast, my approach to a patient's material is to try to focus my attention on the transference and to use my perception of the mood of the session, the background events such as changes in the schedule, and my countertransference, to interpret the content of the session as far as it can be understood in transference terms.

responseSet.title: Response to Internet Discussion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Internet Discussion

Jorge Ahumada

Jorge Ahumada

Av. Las Heras 3898, 3° ‘H’
1425 Buenos Aires
Argentina

Jorge Ahumada  

Dear Sirs,

Having read Professor Bernard Cullen's Internet Discussion Review (2001) of my paper ‘The rebirth of the idols: the Freudian unconscious and the Nietzschean unconscious’ (Ahumada, 2001), I must say that I found there a postmodern rebuttal in lieu of a review.

Which is a good example of a moral issue. Given that, in his own words, Professor Cullen finds my paper offputting, I ask whether he was in a position to accept being its moderator/reviewer. Instead of accepting and acknowledging his bias, he chooses to play his own agenda and proceeds to put my paper down. I surmise he should in any case have satiated his postmodernist urges in a paper of his own. Ought not the role of the moderator/reviewer demand an equitable rather than a partisan stance?

Space does not allow me to detail the manifold distortions and inaccuracies in his summary, such as raising adversarial opinions into authorities, his putting in my mouth that Nietzsche was ‘proto-fascistic’ (words uttered by a participant rather than by me, in what to my mind amounts to a simplistic statement on a complex issue), or his volunteering that I add insult to injury in my consideration of Laplanche's Cartesian biases. Furthermore, he muddles the roles in his nimble jumping into the boots of editor and judges and putting into question my paper's pertinence for the International Journal—an opinion belied by the passion in the discussion.

To profit from a partisan posture under the cloak of moderator/reviewer is itself of a piece with postmodernism, where rhetoric substitutes for logic. I take it, however, that Professor Cullen can claim venerable enough antecedents: both Nietzsche and Foucault define themselves as philosophers with a mask.

responseSet.title: Response to Wylie
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Wylie

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua

Corazón de María 2
Madrid, Spain

Cecilio Paniagua  

Dear Sirs,

I thank Dr Wylie for his interest and comments on my article ‘The attraction of topographical technique’ (2001, p. 671). Dr Wylie was an old teacher of mine, and for me this represents a rare opportunity to continue reflecting on his observations after many years and many miles of distance.

In my paper I tried to spell in clear terms the powerful, though irrational, motives why topographical technique continues to be so untowardly attractive. I was sorry to learn that my arguments did not persuade Dr Wylie at all on the convenience of replacing the prestructural methodology with the structural one. Neither did Dr Wylie find convincing my explanation of why I decided against giving a patient an oedipal interpretation ‘insufficiently based on explicit material’ (p. 680). I wondered in puzzlement why he thought that I missed ‘an opportunity, at this point early in the analysis, to strengthen the working alliance’, for my feeling was quite the opposite: had I given an interpretation along that line, the patient would have sensed that I was too theory driven in my views and not sensitive enough to what he actually verbalised or what he could emotionally absorb. My question ‘Where do you think these demands are coming from?’ implied an encouragement to look into the past, as Dr Wylie remarked, but I thought that it also opened the possibility of other dimensions, such as enquiry into intrapsychic conflict and search for internal meaning. Did my question remove the patient from the clinical surface? I do not think so, at least not in my rendition of clinical surface (Paniagua, 1991).

I thoroughly agree with Wylie's—and Jacobs's—suggestion that we should use our inner experiences (among many others) to grasp certain unconscious communications transmitted by analysands. However, in this regard, I would like to stress two technical points: first, I would consider it essential to go in mente over the clinical material in order to try to discern what in it could account for my inner experience, i.e. trying to distinguish a reactive counter-response from counter-transference vera; and second, I would wonder how I could direct the patient's attention to observable processes that took place in front of our eyes, so to speak, with the aim of enlarging his/her awareness of how he/she may be influencing other human beings—analyst included. I am set against following intuitive drifts unreflectingly, by-passing close process analysis, let alone intervening without due consideration for the patient's ego state.

responseSet.title: Response to Ralph Roughton
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Ralph Roughton

Jean Bergeret

Jean Bergeret

47 rue de la Garde
69005 LYON
France

Jean Bergeret  

Dear Sirs,

I am surprised with the stance R. Roughton takes to develop a criticism of my article published in the International Journal. Indeed, in this article—as in all my works, and being convinced like Freud of the scientific status of psychoanalysis—I simply express hypotheses and offer to discuss their validity (or not) in a most open dialogue with my colleagues. I never intervene to defend a position that could involve moral judgements of a choice of life! Or even passions.

I simply offer to try to reconsider, in unison with others, the usual conception that psychoanalysts, as well as the public, have of what we call (singularly) ‘homosexuality’. In order to reach this aim, I intend to start from positions taken by Freud as well as by Ferenczi and by other later psychoanalysts, whether they belong to the old continent or to the ‘new world’ …

I do not intend to express a moral judgement on object choices made by anyone, whether by persons I meet in life or by the ones who come and consult me. For thirty years I have sought to practise the prudence with which psychoanalysts must consider the notion of ‘normality’, paying attention to its nuances.

I have tried to offer a differential decomposition of the excessively heterogeneous group of very different situations considered as ‘homosexual’.

952

responseSet.title: Freud in the Pampas: The Emergence and Development of a Psychoanalytic Culture in Argentina.: Mariano Ben Plotkin. Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press. 2001. Pp. 314.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud in the Pampas: The Emergence and Development of a Psychoanalytic Culture in Argentina.

Mariano Ben Plotkin. Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press. 2001. Pp. 314.

Maria Boulan

Maria Boulan

50W 96th Street
New York, NY 10025
USA

Maria Boulan  

This fascinating history on the origin and development of psychoanalysis in Argentina was inspired, Plotkin writes, by a conversation with his aunt, an Argentine psychoanalyst, when he was a foreign graduate student at Berkeley. His aunt's surprise that he and his American fellow students were not in therapy, in contrast to Argentine students, sparked a long-standing interest on Plotkin's part in understanding why psychoanalysis became so popular in Argentina, a country where ‘politicians, bank clerks, soap opera stars and cab drivers, seem to use psychoanalytic language to express the concerns of everyday life’ (p. 1), and where one in every 200 inhabitants of Buenos Aires is a psychologist. Plotkin, a historian, takes us on a scholarly historical journey through the whole of the last century in Argentina, and provides us with a meticulously detailed account of the political and social events that shaped the complex context in which psychoanalysis developed.

Plotkin argues that the popularity of psychoanalysis in Argentina is accounted for by two main factors: very passionate proponents and an especially receptive people—the latter resulting from unique historical and sociological factors. Plotkin states:

Argentina, a country of immigrants, had been a success story until the 1920s. By the 1930s the long period of stability came to an end. In the following decades Argentines suffered dictatorship, violence and war. In the 1970s a feeling of crisis and disappointment became widespread among intellectuals and was followed by one of the most repressive military governments of the continent. Given this history, a variety of factors explain the development of a psychoanalytic culture in Argentina. Among them we find the European background of most of the population and the crisis of identity provoked by its foreign roots; the presence of a large Jewish community; a permanent feeling of disappointment and the national tradition of the tango and the gauchos (p. 7).

This is a tradition rich in the subjects of pain and suffering over loss and abandonment.

Plotkin recognises that Argentine psychoanalysts have been very influential in the development of psychoanalysis in such Latin American countries as Uruguay, Brazil and Mexico, and also in Spain, where the ‘Argentine psychoanalyst’ has become a social stereotype in Madrid and Barcelona (p. 2). Argentine psychoanalysts have also made important theoretical contributions. However, he agrees with some sources that the lack of a strong Argentine School of psychoanalytic thought may have contributed towards a more flexible adaptation to several foreign psychoanalytic schools. This adaptation may have allowed Argentine psychoanalysis to remain valid through time and be adopted by many medical and non-medical professionals.

responseSet.title: Non-Discursive Expressive Elements and Their Role in the Construction of Meaning in the Analytical Situation: A Clinical Discussion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Non-Discursive Expressive Elements and Their Role in the Construction of Meaning in the Analytical Situation

A Clinical Discussion

Estevam Lima

Estevam Lima

9 de julho, 5017, Suite 6-A Jd.
Paulista São Paulo
SP Brazil CEP 01407-200

Estevam Lima  

The autdor discusses the role of non-discursive expressive elements in the construction of the analytical situation, using three examples to illustrate the problems with which he is concerned. His claim is that the issue in question necessarily involves the subject of affects, and he proceeds to discuss the difficulties associated with this subject. In addition, he considers the contributions of Green and Imbasciati, and Kleinian developments of this theme, including also the contributions of Bion—in particular the latter's theories concerning thought, in which emotion comes to assume an essential place in the origin of thinking. The author resumes the discussion by taking up his clinical examples, using them to put forward the view that non-discursive expressive elements may well play a decisive role in the construction of meaning in the analytical situation. He suggests also that the meaning of an emotional experience may be thought of as a construction contributed to by a number of symbolic forms which both interfere with and interact with the symbolic system of language. Following examination of his third example, the author reflects on ‘musicality’, a notion sometimes referred to informally in clinical data in connection with the ‘emotional climate’ of the session. He proposes that the complex problem of meaning in music be extended to cover the construction of meaning in the psychoanalytic setting, and in so doing returns to ideas put forward by Suzanne Langer. His underlying view here is that essential elements of the musical phenomenon and essential elements of particular forms of emotional life give rise to the same emotional matrices—perhaps to what Meltzer calls ‘musical deep grammar’. Finally, the author considers various symbolic forms that contribute to the particular configuration of analytical situations, suggesting that the mental condition of ‘free-floating attention’ requires the broad availability to the analyst's mind of a multiplicity of symbolic forms, his conversion of these into new expressions of meaning, and the possibility of their verbal communication by him to the patient.

responseSet.title: Response to Marco Rivalta
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Marco Rivalta

Richard Skues

Richard Skues

62-66 Highbury Grove
London
N5 2AD
UK

Richard Skues  

Dear Sirs,

Readers of my paper who value the customary conventions of scholarly rigour will no doubt sympathise with me in my task of responding to Dr Rivalta's letter in respect of the degree to which he misrepresents its argument and selectively avoids confronting the evidence it presents. But, curiously enough, his letter is not alone in the latter respect as there has recently appeared in print an article that cites my paper and proffers a competitive thesis as to the identity of the ‘young man’ of Freud's aliquis slip, but only does so with any semblance of plausibility in so far as it evades the facts established by my article. Hirschmüller's (2002) favoured candidate is Freud's brother, Alexander, and he lists a number of characteristics the ‘young man’ of the slip shares with Alexander, which are sufficient to persuade him that he has good grounds for claiming to have found the subject of Freud's analysis. The strategic and tactical similarities between Rivalta's letter and Hirschmüller's article are striking when it comes to disregarding uncomfortable evidence, albeit that each of them does so in the service of different conclusions.

Dr Rivalta maintains that if the ‘immediate context’ of the aliquis slip was not the news of Freud's failure to gain his professorship, but rather a newspaper article referring to St Januarius and a fear about a possible pregnancy of Freud's sister-in-law, Minna Bernays, then I ignore the consequence that Freud must have disguised the ‘psychological circumstances’ that preceded the slip's occurrence. It is not clear whether Rivalta means by ‘immediate context’ the specific circumstance that triggered the slip or that which led up to the conversation immediately preceding it, but in either case, this is a topic on which I am careful not to speculate in my article. For his part, all Freud maintains is that the slip occurred in the middle of a conversation about the social status of the race to which he and the ‘young man’ both belonged. He never attributes either the slip, or even the conversation leading up to it, to the reception of a piece of news of any kind in the way that Rivalta implies. In my article (as indeed in those of both Swales and Kuhn) the failed professorship is simply part of the contextual background that would explain why Freud himself might be so personally affected at that time by the general status of his race. To that extent my account is both more consistent with Freud's own than Rivalta implies, and less discrepant with Freud's version of events than Rivalta's own unsubstantiated supposition about what precisely occurred.

responseSet.title: Comments on Paniagua's ‘The attraction of the topographical technique’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comments on Paniagua's ‘The attraction of the topographical technique’

Harold Wylie MD

Harold Wylie

12 Seventh Street, N.E.
Washington D.C. 20002-6022
Drwylie@cs.com

Harold Wylie  

Dear Sirs,

It seems to me that in his article ‘The attraction of the topographical technique’ Dr Paniagua sets up a ‘straw man’ by suggesting that the topographical technique ‘is a practice that should be laid to rest’. Indeed, Dr Paniagua, in his first clinical vignette, avoids making an oedipal interpretation and does not heed his own advice, which is to focus on the clinical surface and thereby avoid ‘iatrogenic contamination’. Instead, thinking of his patient's competitiveness with his father, Dr Paniagua asks the patient, ‘Where do you think these demands [to be perfect] are coming from?’ I think Dr Paniagua misses an opportunity, at this point early in the analysis, to strengthen the working alliance. By asking the patient where his feeling of being critically examined by another comes from, Dr Paniagua implicitly encourages him to look into his past. Thus the patient moves to past memories, thereby avoiding the surface. On the other hand, judging from the intense affects the patient brought into the analysis at that point, Dr Paniagua's implicit suggestion (that the patient's difficulty lay in the enduring impact of his earlier experiences) was, in fact, more clinically useful. In the second vignette, Dr Paniagua heeds his own advice and brings the patient's attention to what she was saying, rather then directing her attention to Dr Paniagua's internal formulations about penis envy. As he quotes Busch, ‘the analyst should not indulge his own interests while the patients are inhibiting theirs’.

To follow Dr Paniagua's suggestion that analysts today should not use the topographical model would leave psychoanalysis much impoverished. We should have both models as points of reference. In 1923, the same year he developed the structural model, Freud wrote of the topographical model.

one should strive to catch the drift of the patient's unconscious with his own unconscious … in a state of easy and partial attention so as to avoid, so far as possible, reflection and the construction of conscious expectations, not try to fix anything that he had heard particularly in his memory, and by these means to catch the drift of the patient's unconscious with his own unconscious (p. 239).

responseSet.title: Untying the Knot: Working with Children and Parents.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Untying the Knot

Working with Children and Parents.

A. H. Brafman, London: Karnac. 2001. Pp. 166.

Athol Hughes

Athol Hughes

3 Antrim Grove
London NW3 4XP
UK

Athol Hughes  

Throughout this book Dr Brafman acknowledges his debt to the work of Winnicott as he shows how effective brief diagnostic therapeutic interventions can be in helping children with emotional difficulties. Brafman uses many of Winnicott's techniques, but with a difference. He focuses on the interaction of the parent and child in his consultations in ways that Winnicott did not. He takes into account the parental situation as well as that of the child in his assessments. He tries, often with considerable success, to untie the ‘knot’ that he sees binding child and parent—a ‘knot’ that is detrimental to the development of the child, and a factor that has led to the symptoms that have brought the patient—child pair to the consulting room in the first place. Brafman has found that in the cases where children can appreciate that someone listens and tries to understand their communications, and parents can change their approaches and attitudes to their children and their problems, the symptoms clear up.

1205

responseSet.title: The Importance of Fathers: A Psychoanalytic Re-Evaluation.: Edited by Judith Trowell and Alicia Etchegoyen. London: Routledge. The New Library of Psychoanalysis. 2002. Pp. 256.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Importance of Fathers: A Psychoanalytic Re-Evaluation.

Edited by Judith Trowell and Alicia Etchegoyen. London: Routledge. The New Library of Psychoanalysis. 2002. Pp. 256.

Mauro Morra

Mauro Morra

Via Biasioli 321
16167 Genova
Italy
morrafr@tin.it

Mauro Morra  

It seems to me that today the focus of psychoanalytical investigations in what concerns object relations is turned towards the parental couple, either as a unity or as the presence of two separated individuals.

This book, edited by Judith Trowell and Alicia Etchegoyen, is certainly set up along this line of thought and can be considered a response to the questions put by a number of psychoanalysts influenced by sociology: is father's role still so essential? There are two reasons for asking that question: the first is that many women, either by choice or by necessity, find themselves in the position of being the only parent; the second is that fathers and mothers no longer have such an extreme difference of functions as they had in Freud's times. It could be suggested that in the first case children can make do without a father, and in the second that they cannot differentiate, in the relevant respects, between the two parents.

This book, however, implicitly stresses that the father's importance has not been diminished at all, and all aspects of the existence of the paternal object in the child's fantasy (even where a real father may unfortunately not exist in the family) are examined in detail by the several authors, including by the two editors. It would be hard to do justice to all of these contributors and quote from each individually, so out of preference I will free associate, following the many inspirations I received from this very interesting book.

Psychoanalysis initially dealt mainly with fathers: Freud gave fathers enormous importance, as objects of admiration and rivalry for boys and libidinal attraction for girls. Of course it was the opposite for the mothers—attraction for boys and rivalry for girls—but it seems to me that the maternal image nevertheless tended to be seen as a predominantly sexual object.

1477

responseSet.title: Commentary on Barros's ‘First Interview and one Session with “Ana”’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Barros's ‘First Interview and one Session with “Ana”’

Vincenzo Bonaminio

Vincenzo Bonaminio

Via Nomentana 256
00162 Roma Italy

Vincenzo Bonaminio  

With remarkable clinical evidence, the author has provided for us a ‘model of functioning’ of the analyst at work: one that allows us to watch, as though through a magnifying glass showing details of the texture, the course of a process involving both parties of the relationship. It shows, on the one hand, projective identification of the patient, who tends to ‘force’ upon the mind of the analyst, and in the therapeutic scenario, an organisation of her inner world and her mental functioning not only in order to communicate it, but also to impose it; and on the other side, the analyst's reverie function as he gathers, elaborates and restores her material, now transformed, back to the patient.

There is a remarkable internal coherence in this clinical material between the theory of mental functioning, the theory of the psychoanalytical process and the theory of technique. What the patient is told (technique) derives from how the mental functioning is understood (fundamentally, projective identification); similarly, the mental functioning described in these terms ‘places’ the analyst at the receiving pole (reverie) of the projective identification. The analyst can either recognise and elaborate what the patient tends to ‘force’ on him, thus building up a reverie for the growth and broadening of the meaning of what is happening in the analytic scenario, or he can act by ‘colluding’ with the patient's request, which would turn out to be antigrowth as well as parasitic for the analytic process.

Of course, we need to take into consideration the degrees of these two extremes, which ought not to be regarded as absolute, but rather considered in terms of a continuous oscillation between the two poles. An oscillation, however, with transformative effects, if it inclines more to the side of ‘good reverie’ rather than ‘collusion’.

But, why must ‘Ana’ ‘make a projective identification’? Why must she ‘force’ the analyst to respond seductively to her seduction, only to feel that the analyst is as ‘weak’ and fragile as herself, if he agrees to her request for ‘inconvenient’ hours for her sessions?

responseSet.title: Reply to Jean Bergeret's ‘Response to Ralph Roughton’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Jean Bergeret's ‘Response to Ralph Roughton’

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

240 Halah Circle
Atlanta, GA 30328
USA

Ralph Roughton  

Dear Sirs,

Bergeret completely missed the point of my argument. I did not accuse him of being morally judgemental; rather, I raised specific questions about the scientific validity of his theoretical and clinical positions.

I referred to gay psychoanalytic candidates and training analysts not to open a political discussion, but to question Bergeret's theory as applied to homosexual individuals who manifest securely differentiated self and object relationships and who function at a high level of psychological maturity. By what evidence—not theory, but evidence—does he proclaim that their affective and relational objects can only be narcissistic ones?

Bergeret insists that he does not consider ‘all homophiles’ to be sick; but what is he saying when he asserts that ‘the homophile’ does not experience true object love and when his four categories of homoeroticism span the spectrum of pathology from ‘developmental block’ to ‘progressive deterioration of a psychotic structure’?

By ignoring such challenges to his theory, Bergeret leaves us to surmise that he finds it unthinkable that his views might be skewed by heterosexist bias or that there might be better explanations from a different psychoanalytic perspective.

953

responseSet.title: Working With Traumatised People
responseSet.authorMast: David J. De Levita and  Lewis A. Kirshner
responseSet.authorCitation: De Levita, D. J. & Kirshner, L. A.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working With Traumatised People

David De Levita and Lewis Kirshner MD

Lewis Kirshner

Amsterdam

306 Harvard St.
Cambridge MA 02139

Lewis Kirshner  

In a way, the title ‘Working with traumatised people’ could be said to encompass the whole of psychoanalysis. All the great theoretical visions within the psychoanalytic panoply have referred to unbearable conditions that placed excessive demands upon the subject and resulted in extreme and crippling responses. We read the effects of psychic trauma on the splits and scarrings of the ego, and our evolving technique has extended our therapeutic efforts over a widening scope of these calamities. The panel chaired by David J. de Levita was squarely in this tradition, addressing contemporary ways of responding to victims of trauma seen regularly in clinical practice, as well as directing psychoanalytic attention to phenomena at the interface between the private and social worlds, pushing the boundaries beyond the intrapsychic sphere.

The first paper, a careful clinical study by W. Bohleber, ‘Trauma and dissociation’, presented the treatment of a 30-year-old diabetic woman suffering from dissociative states and depersonalisation. In his admirably documented report, Dr Bohleber described his initial efforts to create an atmosphere of safety and ‘holding’ within which he became gradually a good object with whom his patient could begin to speak about the traumatic events of her life. His account is consistent with the well-known model that equates trauma with unsymbolished experience that returns as action or repetition, with the exception that he emphasised external intrusions as traumatic disruptions of inner space rather than as stimuli for over-excitation. In his reconstruction of the history, the invasive traumas of the patient's early eye surgery and the subsequent onset of her diabetes damaged her nascent selfobject structures, promoting the use of dissociation and splitting as predominant defensive operations. Within the safety of an empathic treatment relationship, she was able to address directly for the first time the traumatic experiences of her childhood and to begin to integrate a fragmented self. Of note in this formulation utilising an object-relations and self-psychology framework was the absence of attention to the role of sexuality in the trauma, a point that perhaps reflects an historical shift in conceptualisation (Baranger, Baranger & Mom, 1988). At the opposite pole, Dori Laub, the panel's discussant, wondered about possible effects of collective traumatisation in this German woman (for example the fear of carrying a biological ‘taint’), which might be psychically unrepresented.

[[RunningHead]]

First Interview and one Session with ‘Ana’

Elias Da Rocha Barros

Elias Da Rocha Barros

Rua Tupi 579 apto. 161
01233001-São Paulo
Brazil
E-mail: erbarro@terra.com.br

Elias Da Rocha Barros  

I would like to present here the material from the first interview of a patient and then a session that took place a few months later. The material of the session reflects a very important aspect of this patient's mental functioning, which was present, in general lines, in the relationship established with me during the first interview, although much less clearly. My objectives are to show how interpretations are constructed using the patient's material and what happens when I interpret.

This patient, whom I will refer to as Ana, started her analysis some years ago. One day I received a telephone call from a person who asked for an interview with me as soon as possible.

569

responseSet.title: ‘The Analyst at Work’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Analyst at Work’

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua
Corazón de María 2
28002 Madrid
Spain

Cecilio Paniagua  

Dear Sirs,

I accepted with appreciation the invitation to be the first participant in the new section of the Journal, ‘The Analyst at Work’, as soon as I understood what the underlying idea was: ‘to examine ordinary sessions with all the doubts, frayed edges and uncertainties that characterise our everyday practice’ (Gabbard & Williams, 2002, p. 2). I readily identified with the need to present material as unmanicured as possible, to be scrutinised by respected colleagues from different psychoanalytic ‘cultures’. This exercise entails some degree of narcissistic mortification—especially when describing difficult situations with parametric cases—but analysts seem champions at sublimating masochism, and the benefits of detailing sessions in this fashion seem obvious for our discipline: the promotion of non-idealised clinical communication and the countering of what Esman (1979) called our ‘Babel of tongues’.

I felt very grateful to Drs Britton and Michels for their commentaries on my presentation. I learned from both of them. I appreciated Dr Britton's understanding of my struggle to avoid ‘an abortive analysis’ on one hand and ‘an interminable analysis’ on the other (p. 189). Also, I welcomed Dr Michels's close, supervisory-like discussion of the clinical exchange. I was glad that he found my ‘openness … courageous and refreshing’ (p. 195). I thank him for graciously offering excellent alternative interpretations to my interventions, no doubt realising that interpretations analogous to the proposed ones could have been issued before or after session 1293.

As an epilogue, I shall add that last Christmas I heard from the patient, Dr B. He sent me some fine bottles of wine, and I called to thank him and find out how he was doing. He let me know that the first few weeks after termination were ‘a bit rough’, but he felt well adjusted thenceforward, dealing reasonably well with daily problems. Also, he openly expressed gratitude for the treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

De la guérison psychanalytique [On the Psychoanalytic Cure]

Nathalie Zaltzman Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1998.

Marie-Françoise Laval-Hygonenq and Andrew Weller

Andrew Weller

2 rue Rossini
75009 Paris, France

Andrew Weller  

Zaltzman has been a member of the ‘Fourth Group’ since its creation in 1969 by P. Aulagnier, F. Perrier and J. P. Valabrega, all three of whom were members of the executive body of the École Freudienne de Paris (EFP), founded by Lacan in 1964. The ‘Fourth Group’ emerged from a split resulting from disagreements over the training modalities of analysts as they were set out in the first two issues of the review Topique, founded in 1970 by Piera Aulagnier. Zaltzman was a member of the editorial committee of this journal from 1974 to 1998, and most of her works have been published in it. Two of her articles have also appeared in the Nouvelle Revue de Psychanalyse under the direction of J. Pontalis.

De la guérison psychanalytique, published in 1998, is a collection of texts dating back over a period of twenty years. The earliest texts are gathered together in the second part of the work under the general title ‘La mort dans la vie’. Here the author includes three chapters which reveal the originality of her elaboration on the economy of the death drives with specific reference to a clinical case and what she calls expériences-limites or ‘extreme experiences’ (‘La pulsion anarchiste’, 1979). At the same time, she offers a re-examination of the question of melancholia and its eventual elective affinity with the analytic function (‘Baiser la mort? Une sexualité mélancolique’, 1982, 1986). More generally, she enquires into the disinvestment and counter-disinvestment of reality in the life struggle; that is, how each one of us may or may not resist the ‘combined action of a destructive social reality and an internal adhesion to exclusion’ (‘Tomber hors du monde’, 1989).

716

responseSet.title: Gender and Psychoanalytic Method
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender and Psychoanalytic Method

Joan Raphael-Leff and Gabriele Junkers

Gabriele Junkers

London

COWAP representative for Germany
Konsul Mosle Weg 18
28359 Bremen
Germany

Gabriele Junkers  

The relation between anatomically fixed endogenous influences of sexuality, on the one hand, and gender in intrapsychic and interpersonal relations, on the other, were made central to the discussion in this panel, mainly attended by women. Four panellists and a discussant were chosen to explore some of the complex relationships between sex and gender in psychoanalytic method. All of them agreed that scientific investigation in this field is lacking, but also that the existing research is not being studied as it should be in order for its findings to be absorbed and utilised. All the panellists shared the view that it was likely that much psychoanalytic theory evolved as a countertransferential defence against threatening unconscious material.

The Chair, Joan Raphael-Leff, opened the panel by stating that women have always been ‘at the “navel” of psychoanalytic thinking as well as early clinical work’. However, she argued that over the years psychoanalytic theory has maintained a precarious balance between the idea of psychic bisexuality (quantitatively expressed in differing saturation of traits across the sexes) and the qualitatively different experiences of each sex. A tension that teeters between fluidity of symbolic interpretation of anatomical ‘bedrock’ and fixity of sex-specific endogenous influences—both of which structure (and are structured by) unconscious conceptualisation of the body and self in childhood sexual development and adult reproduction.

As Chair of the IPA Committee of Women and Psychoanalysis (COWAP), she illustrated the great interest in the work of this group, which, although it came into existence only three years ago, has already managed to organise twelve well-attended conferences in this field of research.

The first panellist, Irene Matthis from Sweden, COWAP Co-ordinator for Northern Europe, prepared the stage for the discussion with her paper ‘Does gender matter?’. For ease of communication she proposed to use the term ‘gender’ for the psychological self-image while reserving the term ‘sex’ for the biological body (Stoller, 1964). She sees ‘sex’ and ‘gender’ as two ‘names’ that we create to give a representation and understanding to ‘the intuitively given fact that there exist men and women’. In using the word ‘gender’, she wanted to draw our attention to the need to be aware of the ever-present unconscious ‘bias’ in thinking about issues of sex and gender arising not only within the patient but also within the analyst. It is exactly this phenomenon that she wished to place at the centre of research in this field.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tradition, Change, Creativity. Repercussions of the New Diaspora on Aspects of British Psychoanalysis.

Riccardo Steiner. London: Karnac Books. 2000. Pp. 362.

Graciela Abelin-Sas

Graciela Abelin-Sas

300 Central Park West
New York, NY10024
USA

Graciela Abelin-Sas  

The author now focuses on the impact of the arriving refugees upon the thinking and the training of the British Psychoanalytic Society. The New Diaspora has taken place and the Institution is shaken by many clashes: ideologies, personalities and different meanings attributed to the same concepts. Problems are also centred on modalities of clinical thinking: how much weight should intuition and inference be given over more logical, research-supported data (even though derivative-based), in interpretation and reconstructions?

For those of us who had the privilege to read through the Controversial Discussions (King & Steiner, 1992) it is fascinating to see the historian summarise as well as situate these in the socio-political context that he has already delineated in the book previously reviewed. With great clarity, Steiner underlines the main issues that separated Kleinians from Freudians. Through well-chosen passages, their technical, theoretical and clinical differences are lucidly sketched out. These most creative psychoanalytic thinkers had to find ways of clarifying, reconceptualising, exchanging, defending and finally restructuring their Institution.

Steiner dedicates one chapter, the introduction to Part Two of his book, to describing the importance of Joseph Sandler's thinking in the development of his own ideas. He reminds us that, very courageously (considering that some of the participants to these discussions were still alive and the wounds inflicted during the Scientific Discussions still unhealed), Sandler edited, in 1967, a limited edition of these documents for the internal use of the British Psychoanalytic Society. It is to Pearl King and Steiner himself that we owe the later, most valuable edition (1992) of the formidable Controversial Discussions. That book included: (1) the papers by Isaacs, Heimann and Klein presented from 1941-5 at the four Scientific Discussions; (2) the following discussions by the Society and (3) the comments that had been requested from the Training Commission (composed by Brierly, A. Freud, Glover, Klein, Payne and Strachey) on the effect of the theoretical disagreements on the training and the life of the British Psychoanalytic Society. Steiner is indebted to Sandler's interest in the extra-analytical factors contributing to the new developments in psychoanalysis. These included the historical, cultural and scientific moment in time when new ideas spring into view or old concepts are reviewed as to their significance. Sandler's emphasis on an open-minded, scientifically curious point of view provided a more respectful attitude towards new developments. Consequently, unconscious schemata and partial theories could be seen as possibly preceding further advances rather than being refuted as unconventional. In this respect, Steiner brings to mind several moments in the development of the Controversial Discussions. I shall quote here three that seem to be for ever relevant. In 1943, Brierly wrote to the Training Commission that ‘… we cannot crystallize psychoanalysis; we cannot keep it fix in any shape or form, however desirable these may appear at any given time. Analysis cannot live unless it grows, and it cannot grow unless undergoing modification’ (p. 37).

responseSet.title: Film Review Essay Blue Velvet: David Lynch's Primal Scene
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Film Review Essay Blue Velvet

David Lynch's Primal Scene

Danielle Knafo and Kenneth Feiner

Kenneth Feiner

20 Gilchrest Rd 2A
Great Neck, NY 11021
USA
dknafo@liu.edu

220 East 26th Street #LD
New York
NY 10010
USA

Kenneth Feiner  

‘The thing is about secrets.’

David Lynch has achieved a curious blend of notoriety and fame as one of the most eccentric yet innovative artists today. His films are evocative and known for their kinky, grotesque, violent and just plain weird themes. He has been called the ‘czar of the bizarre’ and a ‘pornographic fabulist’. Lynch's films are disturbing and disorientating as they revel in the portrayal of violence, perversion, deformity and insanity.

In the created world of David Lynch, things are never what they appear to be. He inverts the ‘American dream’—the idealised vision of a comfortable, orderly, secure and moral life—and shows that behind the thin veneer of normality lurks an underworld permeated by evil, corruption and disorder. In sum, Lynch undermines the illusion of goodness that bolsters one's security and comfort.

An interesting facet of Lynch's films concerns the ways in which the viewer becomes implicated in the corrupt underworld that he uncovers. The unfolding of the story and the use of cinematographic images and techniques, for example, shape the viewer's experience, participation in the film, fantasies and identification with its characters. Throughout his films, Lynch employs sounds and visual images to advance the story and to engage the viewer's voyeuristic and associative processes, making the experience of watching his films more interactive than merely passive. In this essay, we shall attempt to explain the appeal and power of Lynch's work and, in particular, his ability to simultaneously excite and unsettle the viewer, by focusing on what we consider to be his best and certainly his most evocative film, Blue Velvet.

Blue Velvet is a coming-of-age story that centres on the efforts of its central character, Jeffrey Beaumont, to solve the mystery of a crime he uncovers. Jeffrey is first seen after visiting his father who is hospitalised, having been incapacitated in a freak accident. While wandering aimlessly, Jeffrey discovers a decomposing severed ear, which he delivers to the police. Intrigued, Jeffrey resolves to unravel the mystery. As with Oedipus, whose need to penetrate the riddle of the sphinx led him to uncover his own crimes, Jeffrey's investigations into the dark ‘under—inner—world’ lead him to find the same darkness within himself. In the process of his sleuthing, Jeffrey becomes involved with two women: Sandy, the young, blonde-haired, virginal daughter of the detective to whom he had furnished the severed ear, and Dorothy Vallens, the ‘blue lady’, a mysterious woman whose husband and son have been kidnapped. This is the manifest crime that lies at the centre of the mystery Jeffrey seeks to penetrate.

responseSet.title: Borges, Immortality and the Circular Ruins
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Borges, Immortality and the Circular Ruins11

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

7 Mackeson Road, London NW3 2LU, UK—bronstein@compuserve.com

Catalina Bronstein  

J. L. Borges, death, death drive, immortality, loss, narcissism, psychic pain, temporality, transience.

The author explores ideas surrounding immortality and death focusing on the interplay between their development in two stories by Borges (‘The circular ruins’ and ‘The immortal’) and their manifestation in a patient. With the help of Borges's stories, the author addresses the desperate necessity experienced by some individuals to search for immortality. This is not just an expression of the universal wish to live forever but, at a deeper level, arises from the impossibility of bearing the mental pain of experiencing ordinary human vulnerability and loss—death being the ultimate expression of such vulnerability. It is suggested that the relentless pursuit of immortality in such individuals expresses an omnipotent phantasy of ridding the self of the emotional pain and fear that arises through being alive. It leads to a denial of the emotional significance of passage of time, of separation and sexual differences. In actuality, the individual's state of not feeling approximates to a complete loss of human identity and emotional death, with no place for any meaningful others. The individual him/herself becomes a ‘mere image’, living in a delusional world peopled by him/herself and his/her projections, and ending up trapped inside the circular ruins he/she has generated. The horror experienced at the stark awareness of the individual's emotional death and the wish to re-establish contact with the good internal objects that have been attacked sets in motion the long process of searching for the recovery of a sense of temporality (that would still include the wish for immortality) and, with it, a sense of identity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mad Men and Medusas. Reclaiming Hysteria and the Effects of Sibling relationships on the Human Condition

Juliet Mitchell. London: The Penguin Press. 2000. Pp. 381

Phil Mollon

Phil Mollon

Mental Health Unit
Lister Hospital
Stevenage, Herts
SG1 4AB UK

Phil Mollon  

‘Always provocative, rarely less than brilliant’ enthuses the cover note by Lisa Appignanesi and John Forrester. Certainly, every page of Mitchell's book presents a dazzling array of challenging clinical observations and thoughts—but brilliance is not the same as clarity. This is an exciting but often profoundly frustrating book. Mitchell might argue in her closing chapter, ‘There is no way in which hysteria cannot exist; it is a particular response to particular aspects of the human condition of life and death’ (p. 317), but nowhere does she state clearly what hysteria is. Since she appears to view almost everything—from Gulf War syndrome to autism, through eating disorders, dissociative states, witchcraft and borderline personality—as ‘hysteria’, the term can appear bewilderingly all encompassing or even meaningless. To add to the complexity of the book, the broad canvas treatment of hysteria is combined with a new theory of sibling rivalry, or ‘lateral relationships’. Indeed Mitchell sees hysteria as the fundamental way in which human beings express their sibling rivalry and the wish to be the special and uniquely loved child.

529

responseSet.title: The Analyst at Work: Sara in her Fourth Analytic Year
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst at Work

Sara in her Fourth Analytic Year

Tomas Böhm

Tomas Böhm

Rödabergsgatan 8
113 33 Stockholm
Sweden
tomas.bohm@swipnet.se

Tomas Böhm  

Sara is 45 and has been in an ongoing analysis with me, four times a week for the last three years. She lives with Bill, who is ten years younger than her. She has two grown-up children from her first marriage. She works as a counsellor and teacher for psycho-socially disturbed teenagers, but due to her psychic and somatic symptoms she has worked only part-time for the last few years.

She sought analysis because of her recurring and serious migraine and fatigue. She had sexual anxiety, and she felt obsessed by the fear of men abusing women. She was almost unable to touch or hug other people except her children and Bill, and was extremely phobic about snakes and worms, especially toy snakes. She was and still is wondering if her father and maternal grandfather abused her sexually. She knows that her father, being an episodic alcoholic, frightened her a lot when she was under 10 years old; he chased her in what he considered to be ‘playing’, imitating a gorilla and touching her in sexualised ways that felt dangerous. He could totally misjudge the relationship even when she was a grown-up woman on her own, and would squeeze her nipples in social situations.

On the other hand, the father was experienced as distant, locking himself into his work room, denigrating ugly women and comparing Sara and her mother to all the beautiful women he saw on TV. He has been a mystery to Sara, a man partly charming towards women, partly isolating himself, working as an executive, but also in very unqualified jobs. The father is an adopted child; he has never seen his biological mother and always felt tension towards his adoptive mother. Sara's mother has been loyal to her husband, maybe out of an awareness of his vulnerabilities. When Sara has tried to talk with her mother about her childhood and what her father was like, the mother has avoided the subject. She doesn't want to talk to her daughter about what it was like in the family; she hints at secrets that she doesn't tell, according to Sara, and the mother doesn't seem to have been emotionally available, as Sara remembers it.

responseSet.title: From the Theory of Seduction to Traumatic Seduction: Incest
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Theory of Seduction to Traumatic Seduction

Incest

Eloisa Castellano and Guillermo Bodner

Guillermo Bodner

Madrid

Josep Irla I Bosch 2, 7-2
08034 Barcelona
Spain
argui@intercom.es

Guillermo Bodner  

The topic of this panel, and the clinical cases presented, gave rise to an interesting debate on theoretical and technical questions regarding seduction and the psychological effects of incest. The panel moderator, Eloisa Castellano, pointed out at the beginning that actual incest is talked about very little, since it remains masked by the theory of seduction. The force of this avoidance shows that the topic of incest is taboo and implies unconscious complicities; it may be thought that incest, being a step towards the real act, is not a matter for psychoanalysis but rather a subject for forensic psychiatry.

The first panel member, Juan Eduardo Tesone, presented two cases of women who had suffered sexual abuse by their parents. One of them had been deflowered digitally by her father during puberty, a practising gynaecologist; she had not found any help from her mother and felt frozen when the act was repeated. For a long time her real first name was kept from the analyst, since in the transference she feared that through the possession of her name the analyst could ‘take over’ her body. The author considers that the incestuous trauma causes stupor in the person who suffers from it, and that the effects can reveal themselves for years, lodged within the psychic system. This kind of patient needs a type of listening on the part of the analyst that alternates between phantasy and reality, without judgment being passed on the perceived reality of the story.

The other of these two patients had been sexually abused by her father without explicit physical violence since she was 10 years old. The patient had reproached her father for ‘having deceived her’ owing to her need for tenderness. The death of her mother, which took place shortly after, left the patient doubly orphaned: on the part of the mother because of her death and on the part of the father because of the disappearance of the symbolic function. Bereft of parental figures, she felt herself filled with persecutory objects, the terrifying parental imago and the maternal one accused of having failed to protect her.

responseSet.title: The Clinical Value of The Ideas of Wilfred Bion
responseSet.authorMast: Arnaldo Chuster and  Rita V. Frankiel
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Value of The Ideas of Wilfred Bion

Arnaldo Chuster and Rita Frankiel PHD

Rita Frankiel

Rio de Janeiro

185 West End Avenue
New York 10023

Rita Frankiel  

The first of its kind at an IPA Congress, this clinical workshop centred on the contributions of Wilfred Bion. Organised by Drs Cairo-Chiarandini and Sosnik, it attracted more than fifty analysts, most of whom seemed to be actively using Bion's ideas in their clinical work. Dr Chuster, the moderator, emphasised Bion's contribution to the study within the analytic process of thinking in itself, and he added, ‘To observe and to work with the process of thinking means to work with creativeness in psychoanalysis.’

Dr Sosnik then referred to Bion's dictum that the analyst strives in the hour to be without memory or desire, that is, the analyst should not try to advance predetermined understandings. He formulated the analytic process as a meeting of two persons, the psychic apparatus of each available to influencing the other. The understanding of the mutuality of influence in the analytic hour makes it possible to arrive at a more accurate picture of clinical interaction and clinical influence.

The clinical material was presented by Dr Laura Etchegoyen (London), and was derived from the first year and a half of the analysis of a thirty-five-year-old professional woman. She presented material from a number of different times in the treatment—the initial interview, two later sessions, a week apart, which included several dreams; and material arising toward the end of the period of the analysis under study. In an addendum towards the close of the meeting, she provided additional amplifying clinical data along with a third and fourth dream. Central to the clinical situation was the patient's fear of breakdown and ambivalence about making a commitment to the analyst and the treatment. A major theme was finding a way to contain a patient so resistant to treatment. Efforts at understanding that phenomenology and its effects both on the patient and the analyst made the workshop a compelling professional experience.

The patient was described at the initial interview as seeming very determined. She characterised her parents as inconsistent, quarrelsome persons; a weak mother submitted to an erratic and authoritarian father. Resentfully, she recounted that her parents left her with a grandmother for long periods during the first year of her life. Later, she revealed that after the first trimester of her mother's pregnancy with her, a gynaecological problem emerged which resulted in her being placed on absolute bed-rest for the remainder of her pregnancy. The pregnancy immobilised and restricted her totally. The patient was convinced that, for this, she must have been hated by her mother. The mother's problem was remedied before the patient's younger sister was conceived, an event that added to the patient's rivalry with her sister, who she felt, came into the world too soon and in better circumstances.

Muhammad and the Rise of Islam: The Creation of Group Identity.

Subhash C. Inamdar. Madison, CT: Psychosocial Press. 2001. Pp. 226.

Vamik Volkan

Vamik Volkan

PO Box 800657
Charlottesville, VA 22908-0657

udu@tetra.mail.virginia.edu

Vamik Volkan  

The events of 11 September 2001 have transfixed a public otherwise indifferent to the Muslim world. We hear about the training of suicide bombers, are exposed to terms like

1207

jihad—which refers to a personal struggle to reach higher consciousness but is popularly used to mean a holy war—and we connect similar activities and concepts with Islam. Before 11 September, the conflict between Israelis and Palestinians was often generalised as a conflict between Jews and Muslims. After 11 September, other events in the Islamic world also captured the attention of the general public in the West, as well as the rest of the world, to an unprecedented degree, and the divisions that these events relate have shifted the focus from divisions between Islam and Judaism. These divisions are not new. When the 1978 revolution took place in Iran, it was perceived to a great extent as a division between Shi'i Islam and the ‘Great Satan’, representing in Iranians’ minds mostly the Christian West, especially the United States. Meanwhile, the basic origins and motives of the Iranian revolution remained obscure, as they are still misunderstood by many non-Muslims, if not by many Muslims as well (MacEoin, 1983).

Since the attacks, a broader public has read stories about the Taliban—stories that sometimes appear to many non-Muslims, as well as to some Muslims, to suggest that such events are taking place on another planet or in some far-distant past. Muslim extremism is often generalised, leading to stereotyping and confirming popular misconceptions and existing prejudices, in spite of unusual events connected with non-Muslim religious extremism such as the assassination of Yitzhak Rabin in Israel (Moses-Hrushovski, 2000) and the Waco incident in the United States. I do not mean, of course, that there are not many scholars, journalists and lay people in the West and elsewhere in the non-Muslim world who are well informed about Islam.

responseSet.title: The Work of the Negative: André Green. Trans. A. Weller. London: Free Association Books. 1999. Pp. 317.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Work of the Negative

André Green. Trans. A. Weller. London: Free Association Books. 1999. Pp. 317.

Antonino Ferro and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Via Cardano, 77
27 100 Pavia
Italy

MA (Cantab.), MITI

Philip Slotkin  

What struck me about this book—and I have found this throughout André Green's work—is the author's exceptional way of combining his knowledge of and loyalty to Freud's texts

974

with a felicitous creativity and intuitiveness that opens up new and original vistas. This familiarity with Freud's oeuvre never takes the form of a ‘religion’ or ‘ideology’, but constitutes a living field to be ploughed for hints, ideas and intuitions that afford access to the new.

There is also a kind of ‘modesty’ in Green, in the sense that others—even if they lacked his profound knowledge of Freud's work—would go their own way in blissful unawareness of how much implicit or indirect inspiration they derive from the ‘folds’ of Freud's text, whereas Green is very precise in this respect and can always specify exactly which Freudian ‘thread’ constitutes the starting point for his development. I am therefore impressed to see how Freud's theories do not hold back Green's thought but instead serve in effect as a runway along which he first taxies and from which he then takes off into the blue of his new ideas.

The book is admittedly not an easy read, for a number of reasons. As stated, there are constant references to the ‘folds’, both explicit and implicit, of Freud's thought, thorough familiarity with which cannot be presupposed in all readers. He also refers frequently to important French psychoanalytic authors, many of whom are not so well known in other cultures. And references to the cultural climate of France abound. However, in this book the author takes the reader by the hand, helping him through difficult passages and showing him their ‘secrets’, as well as demonstrating his method of thinking and research. Metapsychology becomes an instrument for reflection and a way of trying out new formulations and hypotheses. As used by Green, metapsychology puts me in mind of the work of a brilliant mathematician who succeeds in ‘predicting’ or anticipating what physicists will later discover ‘experimentally’ in the field.

responseSet.title: Changing Nature of Family Structure: Developmental Theories Under Challenge
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changing Nature of Family Structure

Developmental Theories Under Challenge

Ruth Stein and Lucy LaFarge

Lucy LaFarge

Jerusalem

239 Central Park West, #1BE
New York, NY 10024

Lucy LaFarge  

The impact of contemporary social forces upon family structure and personality development was addressed by the three presenters at this workshop, while the discussant focused upon the difficulty of confirming psychoanalytic hypotheses concerning this complex interface. In introducing the three presenters, Ruth Stein observed that all of them emphasised the effects of social factors upon individuation and autonomy. Kitty Schmidt explored these themes in relation to adolescence in a totalitarian society; Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel described a heightened and pathological self-sufficiency related to the breakdown of family structure; and Nancy Chodorow considered the difficulty that women encountered, in a society where many choices were open to them, in consolidating a generative maternal identity freed from impingement by early relationships with mother and siblings.

In her paper, ‘Some possible consequences of changes within families: a wave of disorders aimed at autarchy’, Chasseguet-Smirgel grouped together drug and alcohol addiction, eating disorders and certain kinds of sexual conduct. All these behavioural disorders, she said, shared a goal that she termed ‘autarchy’, the aim of becoming totally independent of human objects. In most cases the autarchic ambition involved doing without the mother. For women, this forced independence was associated with an attack upon the mother, in the form of an attack upon the woman's own body (which was identified with the mother's body). In men, this aspect was less evident.

Chasseguet-Smirgel described two male patients, both alcoholics, for whom dependency on a substance that could be controlled and replaced warded off passive longings for the nurturance they had not received in childhood. For two female patients, eating disorders reflected a refusal to enter the biological order of female development. For the anorexic woman, restriction represented a triumph over the need for the object. For the bulimic, binging and purging reflected the enactment of a self-sufficient cycle, in which ingested food was felt to represent the bulimic's own faeces. For all the patients she described, Chasseguet-Smirgel said, sexuality involved a dehumanisation of the object as a defence against intimacy, dependency and loss. All had multiple partners. One male patient insisted on the physical perfection of his partners, as a defence against the guilt that a flawed object would evoke.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Le vampirisme. De la Dame Blanche au Golem: Essai sur la pulsion de mort et sur l'irreprésentable [Vampirism. From the White Lady to the Golem: An Essay on the Death Drive and the Unrepresentable.]

Pérel Wilgowicz. 2nd, revised and expanded edition. Meyzieu: Césura. 2000. Pp. 354.

Dominique Bourdin and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

21 rue Guynemer,
93700 Drancy, France

MA Cantab. MITI

Philip Slotkin  

The vampire is a mythical being devoid of shadow or reflection. Neither living nor dead, he spends his days in the grave and his nights wandering and searching; he leaves his coffin in the moonlight and returns to it at cockcrow. In between he sucks the blood of his victims, drawing out their vital substance and making them languid and pallid so that they are themselves suspended in a state of non-life. The preferred victims are pathetic, defenceless young women, if possible close relatives of these beings who cannot die and who, unlike ghosts, return in the flesh to haunt the living and suck their blood so that they can go on with their nocturnal, destructive pseudo-lives. The vampire's innocent, fragile prey often become vampires themselves, giving rise to a monstrous line of beings—neither dead nor alive but subterranean bringers of death.

Pérel Wilgowicz, a member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, has been interested in the figure of the vampire for many years. She sees the vampire myth as highly relevant to contemporary clinical practice, which is alert to the most archaic aspects of the psyche and to the difficulties of separation, differentiation and identity. Following in the footsteps of Freud, who drew on the myths of Oedipus and Narcissus, she uses prominent figures from myth, legend, literature and art to cast light on psychoanalytic practice and theory. In keeping with present-day trends in French psychoanalysis, she is interested in narcissistic and borderline pathologies, though without on that account rejecting the notion of the Oedipus complex or detaching questions of identity from those of sexuality. By invoking the various forms and variants of the traditional culture of vampires and related figures, Wilgowicz draws our attention to forms of psychic existence displayed by patients who have never truly been born: these living-dead subjects hover or float, instead of having a proper sense of being inside their bodies; dream of frozen springs turned to glass or ice; and fail to recognise their image in the mirror—instead sometimes glimpsing in horror a reflection of their mother's face rather than their own.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reverie and Interpretation: Sensing Something Human

Thomas Ogden. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1997. Pp. 286

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th Street, #1G
New York, NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs  

After finishing the final pages of Thomas Ogden's new book, Reverie and Interpretation: Sensing Something Human, I put it down with the conviction that remarkable as he is as an analytic author and thinker—his is one of the most original and creative voices in psychoanalysis today—Ogden may have even greater gifts as a literary critic. For the last chapter of his book, devoted to an illuminating discussion of three poems by Robert Frost, constitutes as fine a piece of literary criticism as one is likely to encounter in contemporary writing.

This superb chapter, however, is only one of the gems to be discovered in this new collection of Ogden's. One will find here original thinking, imaginative ideas, a deep and sensitive understanding of language and the effects that it produces on readers and listeners, rich and richly illustrated discussions of case material and challenging reformulations of long-accepted analytic concepts and techniques.

Ogden's approach to analysis is well known to those who have followed his work over the years. For him the heart of the analytic situation lies in the ideas, fantasies, beliefs and shared assumptions that are jointly created by patient and analyst, a phenomenon that he has termed the analytic third (1994).

521

[[RunningHead]]

Cathecting Body and Mind in a New Relationship

Aspects of the Analytic Method in Work with Adolescents

Christel Airas , Donald Campbell and Björn Salomonsson

Björn Salomonsson

Helsinki

London

Gaveliusgatan 11
11641 Stockholm
Sweden
bjorn.salomonsson@chello.se

Björn Salomonsson  

The chairman reminded the audience that adolescence has been viewed as a specific developmental phase for only a comparatively short time. Many analysts have contributed to this shift. Freud (1905) highlighted the extensive psychological changes brought about by puberty. ‘The adolescent's response to and resolution of the conflicts engendered by these changes determine the final sexual organisation’, the chairman said. Pathology in adolescence is often due to a premature foreclosure of the conflicts. Through psychoanalysis the young can ‘buy time’ to work them through.

Psychoanalysis of adolescents has its specific difficulties. It is taxing for the analyst to adjust to the adolescent's mental lability. Parents can provide gratification of infantile sexuality, undermining their child's motivation to give up and mourn relationships with the primary objects. The adolescent tends to engage in love objects, which detracts from his or her investing in the transference. Nevertheless, adolescent psychoanalysis is the treatment of choice in many cases of adolescent disturbance. The co-ordinator, who was responsible for putting together the Panel, remarked that the young person is locked between an unreachable, insoluble past and a frightening future. She compared his or her situation to that of Dante at the beginning of his journey, whereas the analyst may function as a guiding Virgil.

Who am I?

The first case was presented by Anita Schmukler. She wished to examine ‘an adolescent's response to her developing body’, summarised in the recurrent question, ‘Who am I?’ She also wanted to examine the consequences of these bodily changes for analytic technique. The pubertal girl often reacts to these changes with conflict. This can be seen especially in two areas. One is when the oedipal solution is affected by castration fantasies. Another is when the girl's view of herself as an emerging sexual woman is affected by an early conflation of vaginal and anal spheres.

Apart from these zone-specific conflicts, the young girl presented also suffered from an injury to her infantile narcissism. This was demonstrated in her symptom of compulsively writing certain letters, interpreted as the girl's submission to her mother's ambitiously teaching her to write prematurely.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Mind of One's Own: A Kleinian View of Self and Object.

Robert CaperNew Library of Psychoanalysis 32. London: Routledge. 1999.

Richard Rusbridger

Richard Rusbridger

27 Normanby Road
London NW10 1BU

Richard Rusbridger  

This book is a series of papers on psychic development and psychoanalytic technique that take off from Robert Caper's previous book, Immaterial Facts (1998), in which he

719

described Freud's discovery of psychic reality and Klein's extension of Freud's discovery. The papers in this book are on a more disparate number of themes than the earlier book, but share some central concerns and are further unified by their close reference to Caper's theoretical framework, which is the work of Freud, Klein and Bion. He does a great deal to clarify, as well as to connect, the contributions of all three.

The papers can be broadly divided into two main groups, with a few additional papers that fall into neither group. In both the main groups of papers the author addresses the theoretical question of how analysis works, and derives implications for technique from the answers. In the first group he asks how analysis heals, and what distinguishes it from suggestion. This leads him to discuss the use of the transference in analysis.

He begins this selection of papers by exploring Freud's and Bion's work on groups. In analysis, as in a group, the patient projects his omnipotence into the analyst. This can be flattering, and can be concordant with the analyst's wish or need to be a powerful healer, but it leaves the analyst, and therefore the patient, at the mercy of the patient's delusional transference. This situation led Freud into studying the transference, and Caper has a special appreciation, which I share, of Strachey's 1934 paper on the subject. The value of the transference neurosis is that it is the current, active version of the unconscious processes that are troubling the patient. When an analysis is going well, the analyst crucially does not accept the invitation to play the part of the patient's archaic superego. Instead, he or she acts as an auxiliary ego (a characteristically perceptive reformulation of Strachey's ‘auxiliary superego’), and interprets the patient's pressure rather than responding to it. Caper, like Strachey, is clear that this is in itself an idealised version of what happens in practice in an analysis, as the act of transformation is extremely difficult for the analyst. In addition, even though the analyst may be acting as an analyst rather than as a transference object, the patient may nevertheless take the analyst's interpretations as suggestions. For both these reasons, suggestion occurs in all analyses, which, in Strachey's terms, are always on the verge of deteriorating into a ‘real’ relationship.

‘It is a New Kind Of Diaspora.’ Explorations in the Sociopolitical and Cultural Context of Psychoanalysis

Riccardo Steiner. London: Karnac Books. 2000. Pp. 212.

Graciela Abelin-Sas

Graciela Abelin-Sas

300 Central Park West
New York, NY10024
USA

Graciela Abelin-Sas  

This carefully researched and elegantly constructed book offers a treasure of information drawn from the correspondence (held in the Archives of the British Psychoanalytic Society) between Jones, Anna Freud, Eitingon, van Ophuijsen, Brill and many others during the Nazi persecution. Summarising and quoting from letters written between 1933 and 1938 from and to London, Vienna, Berlin, Amsterdam and New York, Steiner transports the reader to the angst and uncertainty of those dangerously evolving scenarios, which often required thoughtful and immediate action to save lives as well as protect the Psychoanalytic Institution.

About two hundred and twenty letters were exchanged between Anna Freud and Jones alone in the years 1933 to 1939. Steiner is well aware, as he states in the introduction, of the complexities involved in an epistolary-based reconstruction of history. How much of what was written served purposes of camouflage? Was it used as a means to document positions for posterity? Unconsciously censored? Knowingly and wisely avoided? For instance, in March 1933, after a visit to Berlin and specifically to Eitingon (Polish Jewish President of the Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute), van Ophuijsen writes to Jones that ‘mixed marriages will be considered illegal from now on … But if you write, you must be cautious, you have not been so in your last letter to E, it might have harmed him … as it is now absolutely certain … that his correspondence is under control’ (p. 37).

Along with an extraordinary mobilisation to rescue, which resulted in most persecuted psychoanalysts being saved from dying in concentration camps, we witness many a political misassessment. For instance, Jones's agreement in 1933 to name Aryan psychoanalysts (Boehm and Muller-Braunschweig) as heads of the German Psychoanalytic Society in order to keep that Institution alive and disconfirm the Nazi belief that Psychoanalysis had a Jewish ideology was proven to be a naïve move. Five years later, in 1938, the Viennese Society, ‘mother of them all’, was dissolved by the Nazis and put under the control of the German Psychoanalytic Institute, directed by Muller-Braunschweig, acting as a Nazi puppet. In 1933, when many a Jewish colleague was being expelled from the Society and forbidden to practise, Jones went so far as to advise the voluntary resignation of Jewish members: ‘For it would surely not be sensible to make the gesture (which will impress nobody) of dissolving the whole Society on the ground that the government makes a foolish racial distinction …’

Dominique Scarfone  

Dear Sirs,

In reply to Dr Fingert Chused's interesting question. I can only agree that the pleasure of being attractive and seductive in the case of the patient presented by Dr Barros is quite obvious. I would even add, if you pardon the ‘Witz’, that ‘it goes without saying’. The question indeed is whether such a general trait of the patient's attitude should be the target of the analyst's interpretive activity. In other words, what use can the analyst make of the remark that, indeed, ‘the lady doth protest too much’?

My way of dealing with the problem would be, very simply, to ask myself if this is the main—and workable—aspect of the transference, the one I must address at the present phase of the analysis lest we run into a dead end. Obviously, in the actual case, Dr Barros is best able to provide arguments in favour of or against any decision on this matter. I only wish to stress that, generally speaking, the analyst may well be aware of the pleasure highlighted by Dr Chused and nevertheless choose to make no reference to it, unless it becomes the main aspect of resistance. What if, for instance, it turned out that the seductiveness displayed is the last resort of an otherwise fragile narcissistic structure? I suggest it should then be left in the background, at least as long as it serves the basic positive transference without which no analysis can occur.

1188

Book Notices

Bonfiglio, B. (2000). Uno psicoanalista al “Servizio”. Psicoanalisi nei Centri di Salute Mentale [A Psychoanalyst at the “Service”. Psychoanalysis in Mental Health Services]. Roma: Borla. Pp. 223.

Written in non-technical language, this book is based on the author's supervisory experience in public Mental Health Services. His psychoanalytic knowledge is put ‘at the service’ of those challenging aspects of mental suffering which patients cannot express through words. Ample evidence supports his view that the clinical understanding of the patient, of the staff's psychological dynamics, and of the functioning of the psychiatric institution itself is crucial to the success of the therapeutic intervention.

Botella, C. & Botella, S. (2001). La Figurabilite psychique [Psychic Representation]. Paris: Delachaux et Niestlé. Pp. 261.

The authors report their analytic experiences with borderline and neurotic patients, as well as with children. They attempt to demonstrate the inadequacy of an analytic theory based entirely on a model of representation and they develop a broadened concept which has at its base their own understanding of hallucinations and of the work of representation as a means of access to unrepresentable areas specific to early trauma.

Cupelloni, P. (ed.) (2002). La ferita dello sguardo. Una ricerca psicoanalitica sulla melanconia [The Gaze's Wound. A Psychoanalytical Research on Melancholy]. Milan: Angeli. Pp. 187.

This volume brings together the writings of ten Italian psychoanalysts involved in the study of melancholy. They differentiate it from depressive pathology, relating it instead to those primary phases of existence which are characterised by indifference, formlessness and confusion. Treatment should focus on the identity process and on the analysis of blind-spots not yet representable by the patient, going back to the original incompleteness of the mind.

Denis, P. (2001). Eloge de la bẽtise [In Praise of Stupidity]. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. Pp. 175.
