responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Nature of the Problems of Psychoanalysis with So-Called ‘Difficult’ Patients

Jorge Badaracco and Rudi Vermote

Rudi Vermote

Buenos Aires

Stationlei 31
1800 Vilvoorde
Belgium
Rudi.vermote@skynet.be

Rudi Vermote  

This report covers the panel on 24 July, when all presentations were given, and the panel on 25 July, when the discussion was held after an introductory summary by Jorge García Badaracco, chair.

The panels were characterised by the basic assumption that psychotic symptoms have an interactional meaning. There was a feeling that the discussion functioned as a large container with the participants sharing their experiences. The intensity of this clinical reality seemed to override theoretical differences.

The Psychotic Dilemma

The psychotic dilemma proved to be a strong concept and was the theme running through the two days. Although this concept is not really new (e.g. Bateson, 1956; Bion, 1957; Lidz & Lidz, 1949; García Badaracco, 1986), the presentation by Stavros Mentzos (Frankfurt), ‘The dilemma of the schizophrenic and the therapeutic relationship as an alternative experience’, made it fresh and fruitful. Mentzos put psychotic dilemmas and neurotic conflicts in opposition. In his model most psychotic symptoms are not the result of defective ego-functions but the expression of active defences against dilemmas producing unbearable intrapsychic tensions. The prominent dilemma in psychotic pathology is the dilemma between having no object and fusion with an object: in other words, ‘loss of object’ versus ‘loss of self’. This idea is supported by the fact that psychoses are often triggered by the distance of the object, experienced as too great, or the proximity of the object, experienced as too close. In fact, first psychotic episodes are often seen in relation to a separation from the family or in a first love: hence the capital importance of offering the patient a new experience of relationships in order for them to obtain a balance between proximity and distance, between self-orientation and object-orientation. This differs from the model of M. Klein, which is based on aggression against the object.

It was the view of Juan Tubert-Oklander (Mexico) in his presentation ‘Premises for a psychoanalytic approach to psychoses’ that the psychotic dilemma is not merely a contradiction but a paradox. A patient may demand explicitly that the analyst behave as a good object, while implicitly requiring that the analyst confirm his belief that no object is ever to be trusted. Usually only one of the demands is communicated explicitly.

J. L. Donnet presents us here with a collection of his principal contributions, made over the last twenty years, to the essential questions of psychoanalytic practice. For the non-native French reader it must be emphasised that from the outset of his thinking Donnet has been intent on establishing a theory of the psychoanalytic setting and its characteristics, and that what he puts forward is an epistemology and a treatment of the psychoanalytic setting itself.

[[RunningHead]]

A 9-Year-Old with Conversion Disorder, Successfully Treated with Psychoanalysis

Barbara Milrod

Barbara Milrod

525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021, USA—bmilrod@med.cornell.edu

Barbara Milrod  

latency, conversion disorder, unconscious pregnancy fantasies, somatiform disorder, annunciation fantasy, ear pain.

This paper outlines the successful psychoanalytic treatment of a latency-aged boy with severe conversion disorder. Details of clinical treatment are presented. As no tested treatment has yet been found that reliably treats chronic conversion in children, this case is of importance in demonstrating the usefulness of psychoanalysis as treatment for severe psychopathology. The patient's unconscious fantasy constellations that gave rise to this syndrome are highlighted.

As to the Paper of Richard Skues

(‘On the Dating of the Aliquis Slip’, Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 2001, Pp. 1185-1204)

Marco Rivalta

Marco Rivalta

Via Bava 26/B
10124 Torino, Italy
marco.rivalta@libero.it

Marco Rivalta  

Dear Sirs,

I agree with the author on his first point: the almost complete silence of the psychoanalytic historians on Peter Swales's 1982 paper (‘Freud, Minna Bernays and the conquest of Rome’) and particularly on his hypothesis that the perpetrator of the aliquis slip was Freud himself ‘is particularly disappointing’. Besides that, what disappoints me is the fact that the body of Skues's paper is a relentless indictment against the one exception to that silence: Philip Kuhn's article (‘A professor through the looking glass’, Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 1999, pp. 943-959). Skues, so to speak, represents Swales versus Kuhn in the court of the IJP; his paper is devoted to a systematic confrontation between Kuhn's allegations (vs. Swales) and the original documents: namely Freud's letters of September 1900 to his former friend Fliess and to his wife Martha, the Viennese newspapers of that period, plus Skues's conjectures about Martha's and Minna's menstrual periods. (In Skues's view, of course, the ‘allegations’ are always false while the ‘original documents’ always tell the truth.) No wonder then, if one can find among the many ‘original documents’ presented by Skues in his twenty-page harangue for Swales vs. Kuhn just two or three details of Freud's text on the aliquis slip (Freud, 1901b, pp. 8-14; Psychopathology of Everyday Life, chapter II).

Skues points out that in Virgil's line (Aeneid, IV, 625) the son-avenger (‘aliquis … ultor’) conjured up by the Punic queen Dido against the ‘Roman’ Aeneas was none other than the Punic hero Hannibal, and this is something that Freud could not ignore, even if he omitted it, but Skues does not notice this likely omission of Freud's (cf. Skues, 2001, p. 1200).

The phrase the anonymous ‘young man’ (alleged perpetrator of the slip) uses in his anxious expectation of a possible pregnancy, namely that ‘he might easily hear [“bekommen”] a piece of news [… from an] Italian lady’ (Freud, 1901b, p. 11) ‘has clear connotations of a physical separation from the person concerned’ (Skues, 2001, p. 1201).

Arnold Werner  

Over the course of several days in May 1938, Edmund Engelman, a young, talented and resourceful Viennese photographer, immortalised the home and offices of Sigmund Freud in a series of pictures that represent the fullest visual documentation of the setting in which Freud lived and worked throughout almost his entire career. He also photographed Freud, his daughter and his wife. These photographs are best known through a book that also contains a memoir of Engelman (Engelman, 1976). The photographs have been recognised primarily for their documentary value and not appreciated for their artistic merit. Through a series of interviews with Engelman and members of his family, biographical data about the artist and information about the photographs has been gathered that heretofore has not been published. This historical note elaborates on Engelman's life before and after taking the photographs, and places the photographs in the context of the life of the photographer.

[[RunningHead]]

The Neurodynamics of Personality.

Jim Grigsby and David Stevens New York, NY: Guilford Press. 2000.

Lissa Weinstein and Larry Siever MD

Larry Siever

North Academic Campus Room 8/105
137th St and Convent Ave
New York, NY 10031
USA

Box 1230
1 Gustave L. Levy Place
New York, NY 10029
USA
larry.siever@med.ua.gov

Larry Siever  

This ambitious book, coauthored by a research neuropsychologist and a psychoanalyst, attempts to build a comprehensive model of personality compatible with current research on brain function. Personality is so defined as to include the typical patterns of behaviour and attitudes commonly called character, individually expressive motivational and emotional states, and the person's relatively enduring representations both of the world and of themselves. The author's premise, which is explicated in detail throughout the first half of the book, is:

Personality reflects the emergent properties of a dynamic, hierarchically ordered modular distributed self organizing functional system, the primary objective of which is the successful adaptation of the individual to his or her physical and social environment (p. 19).

In this definition, ‘self organizing’ does not refer to the ‘self’ in psychological terms, but is used to refer to a system that sets and resets itself according to law-based processes.

The authors, influenced by the work of A. R. Luria and Janos Szentagothai, seek to present a psychological model grounded in the more basic level of analysis offered by biology, which will supplement existing theories by offering a ‘metapsychological theoretical framework’. Recognising that terms such as representation and self are used variously by different disciplines, they have chosen to rely primarily on the language of neuroscience and discuss concepts in functional terms. While the authors never claim that personality is isomorphic with brain function, they do believe that there are lawbased probabilistic relationships between what takes place at the molecular and neural levels and the associated psychological activity. The biological processes underlying personality are in a constant state of fluid adaptation, but the time-scale over which observations about personality are made causes the processes to appear relatively invariant. Thus, personality is not a structure but a set of emergent properties of complex biological systems and personality functioning, based on brain function: ‘If the brain has a modular architecture, and if function mirrors structure, and if perceptual, motor and cognitive functions all seem to have a modular organization, then the same probably holds true for personality’ (p. 4).

responseSet.title: Response to ‘Psychoanalysis without Descartes’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to ‘Psychoanalysis without Descartes’

Giovanna Goretti

Giovanna Goretti

Via Panama 114
00198 Roma

Giovanna Goretti  

Dear Sirs,

In reply to the letter ‘Psychoanalysis without Descartes’ (Stolorow, Orange & Atwood, 2001, p. 1263), a response to my paper ‘The myth and history of some psychoanalytic concepts: Thoughts inspired by a reading of Orange et al., Working Intersubjectively’ (2001, p. 1205), I should like to draw attention to the following points.

The authors write, ‘She [Goretti] even declares that it is Freud who should properly be decreed the originator of the intersubjective perspective in psychoanalysis’ (p. 1263). Actually, I was quoting a passage from a paper of Freud's (1937) where, among the factors influencing the perspectives of a psychoanalytic treatment that could make it hard going, he focuses on the individuality of the analyst. What I wrote was that Freud's words might well have laid the foundations of an intersubjective current, had the authors been interested in undertaking an operation of the kind. My discourse contained no pragmatic injunction, merely a reference to a specific mental attitude of the authors, who appear to be unaware of the significant similarities to Freud's thinking (in my text I quoted many other passages from Freud where the inevitable influence of the analyst on the course of the cure is likewise evaluated), and also to the fact that they had failed to recognise this continuity with his thought and to grant him priority where certain subjects were concerned.

The authors state that Freudian doctrine is saturated in the Cartesian conception of the isolated mind, and that this conception has resulted in profound clinical consequences. I mentioned in my paper that Freudian thought is beset by contradictions. According to Holt (1965) the capacity for tolerating contradictions is characteristic of the cognitive style of Freud himself. I need only refer to a disconcerting example of this type of contradiction in Freud's classic text ‘Formulations on the two principles of mental functioning’ (1911). After giving us a description of how the newborn infant functions according to the pleasure principle—a type of process that certainly concerns an isolated organism—Freud directs the reader's attention to a note where he states that no organism could live according to such a principle. He then adds, ‘the employment of fiction like this is, however, justified when one considers that the infant—provided one includes in it the care it receives from its mother—does almost realise a psychical system of this kind’ (1911, p. 220). What can one say to this? ‘There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio/Than are dreamt of in your philosophy …’ But we might also wonder whether this rather startling note will allow the authors to state—in the last part of letter—that the innovative content of their work is the ‘effort … to reframe psychoanalysis as a contextual psychology that recognises the constitutive role of relatedness …’ (p. 1265).

Melting the Darkness: The Dyad in Clinical Practice

Warren Poland. New York: Aronson. 1996. Pp. 310

Irene Cairo

Irene Cairo

5 West 86th Street, Apt. 6A
New York, NY 10024
USA
Irecail@cs.com

Irene Cairo  

American psychoanalysis has several prominent figures whose ideas have been extensively published in various forms, from textbooks for beginners to more elaborate selections of theoretical and clinical essays. The purpose of such books is to highlight the ideas of their authors, specially if the authors are the founders of a school of thought. Generally these books are a kind of master class.

Rarely are such books a live history of their authors' psychoanalytic experience, an invitation to dialogue, a conversation with the reader. Warren Poland's Melting the Darkness is, however, one of those rare books. Poland belongs to the same talented psychoanalytic generation as Boesky, Jacobs, Schafer, Ted Shapiro, psychoanalysts trained in the most strictly classical tradition, who, all original thinkers, so to speak broke ranks with the absolutism of their teachers and institutes and yet stopped short of claiming to have started a revolution.

1463

responseSet.title: A final response to Dr Flink
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A final response to Dr Flink

Johan Norman

Johan Norman

Utflyktsv. 13
S-168 41 Bromma Sweden

Johan Norman  

Dear Sirs,

I have found it fruitful to have this exchange of comments that Dr Flink initiated by his reading of my paper ‘The psychoanalyst and the baby’ (IJPA, 82: 83-100) from the vertex of modern linguistic philosophy, mainly Marcia Cavell (1993). In Dr Flink's letter to the Editors and the following exchange of responses (Flink, 82: 805-807; Norman, 82: 807-810; Flink, 82: 990-991; Norman, 82: 1257-1258; Thomson-Salo, 82: 1259-1261; and in this issue, Flink and this final response) some points that I find extremely interesting seem to have emerged. I will pick up just a few points in this final comment.

On the infant's ‘mental’ life, ‘understanding’, ‘meaning’

Dr Flink argued in his first letter that infants ‘are not yet able to understand any kind of meaning’ as they have not yet developed language. I have pointed to the fact that we are using the concepts differently and there seems to be no disagreement about that. But the more important consequence of Dr Flink's use in his first letter of a narrow understanding of what we mean by language excluded, for him, the notion of the infant's mental life and understanding, and consequently also nursed a doubt in my understanding and description of the idea that the relationship between the psychoanalyst and the baby could be a dynamic force for the working through in the relationship between the baby and the mother. In Dr Flink's last response I found that our views have become closer to each other as he is not questioning the meaningfulness of the communication. He only wants to point out ‘that what is meaningful is not the same as having a discourse with language-meaning’. However, in my view the next step is not far away. With a wider notion of language we can include the non-lexical aspects of language and verbal utterances, that is the colouring with tune of voice, gestures, body language conveying emotional states and relationship, and the song-and-dance of the language that is understood by infants.

Trauma: A Genealogy

Ruth Leys London: University of Chicago Press, 2000. Pp. 312.

David Lindy MD and Stanley Bone MD

Stanley Bone

15 West 81st Street
New York, NY 10024

1155 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Stanley Bone  

Trauma is everywhere today. We wake up to it every morning with the news. In the popular Hollywood movie, Analyze This, we watch Robert Deniro, Mafia chieftain with panic attacks, discover the connection between his anxieties and witnessing his father's gangland-style execution as a boy. ‘It was my fault!’ he cathartically confesses to his psychiatrist, Dr Billy Crystal, who shares the mobster's trauma in the form of his own narcissistic, traumatising father. At this juncture in cultural history, we all understand the traumatic implications of such events, even without analytic training. Though we may wonder about ‘false memory syndrome’ or the secondary gain of self-proclaimed victims of trauma, our current sensibility inclines us to believe that there must be some merit to the victim's accusation. At the same time, we are left discomforted by the idea that the victim is automatically relieved, by virtue of status as victim, of all moral responsibility regarding his/her own actions and experience.

290

responseSet.title: Film Review Essay, Sous le Sable [Under the Sand], (2001): Directed by: Francois Ozon; Distributed by: Winstar Cinema
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Film Review Essay, Sous le Sable [Under the Sand], (2001)

Directed by: Francois Ozon; Distributed by: Winstar Cinema

Rita Frankiel PHD

Rita Frankiel

11 West 73rd Street
New York, NY 10023

Rita Frankiel  

This film analysis was written in the summer of 2001, long before the catastrophic terrorist attacks on the USA on 11 September. Now, sadly, it becomes particularly timely because it deals with the devastating psychic processes that can be set in motion by loss. Some who suffer loss are, for example, forced to deny reality altogether. My essay on the film Under the Sand explores this and other forms of pathological mourning and grief, many of which we are now forced to confront nationally and internationally. Gabbard's (1997) encouragement of analysts to use film for the exploration of psychic reality takes on an added urgency at a time of thousands of suddenly dead bodies, and of body parts and bodies that cannot be found.

Under the Sand evokes the tormented emotional life of a woman whose husband has disappeared. As the film opens, a middle-aged couple go on holiday. They are a curiously silent and seemingly ill-matched couple; she, lean, active and cheery, takes most of the initiative; he is overweight and phlegmatic. She ignores his unresponsiveness. Is he depressed? Is he unhappy in the marriage? Is it both? They go to the beach. He says that he will go for a swim. She dozes in the sun. When she awakens he is gone. What has become of him?

Francois Ozon, the director of this powerful film is a master of ambiguity and restraint. There is not a wasted shot, not a lingering directorial self-indulgence. Dialogue is pared to the absolute minimum. Concentrating with unwavering attention and growing power on emotions expressed in the face and body of the actress Charlotte Rampling, we witness insistent denial that a death has occurred in the face of accumulating evidence that the husband has probably drowned. She cannot believe he is gone. As the film develops we see that, for her, whether or not he is gone becomes a matter of control and will and perhaps ultimately be a question of life or death for her, too.

Water is a recurrent visual metaphor in this film. It opens with shots of the Seine as the couple drives out of Paris, and it ends at the sea on the beach from which the man disappeared. Midway through the movie, we see the heroine and her would-be lover in a restaurant decorated with a fish tank. Indeed, we first see them together through the fish tank with fish swimming by. Drowning versus swimming emerges as the basic metaphor or question. Significantly, one topic of conversation at that dinner is Virginia Woolf's suicide note, written as she set out to drown herself with a heavy stone in her pocket. The heroine seems to be sublimely indifferent to, or more likely consciously unaware of, the significance of that association. She mentions it only in the context of her having lectured on Woolf's The Waves that day.

[[RunningHead]]

Costruzioni e campo analitico. Storia, scene e destino [Constructions and the analytic field. History, scenes and fate]

Domenico Chianese. Rome: Borla. 1997. Pp. 267

Stefano Bolognini and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Via Dell' Abbadia 6
40122 Bologna,
Italy

MA (Cantab.), MITI

Philip Slotkin  

Domenico Chianese, who was born in Naples, is a psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who lives and works in Rome; a training analyst, he is currently President of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society. Trained in the classical cultural tradition, he has concerned himself with epistemology and psychoanalytic methodology, with particular reference to historical problems; his clinical interests have focused on the study of constructions, models of relationship and of the analytic field.

His essays are presented in a new context and further developed in this unusual, curious, cultivated, original and intriguing book.

The volume, whose title means ‘Constructions and the analytic field’, is a long and thoroughly considered ‘construction’ of Dr Chianese's personal idea of psychoanalysis as both science and practice, which emerges on page after page from a broadly based and at the same time cogently argued dialogue between the author and his chosen interlocutors—namely, first of all, Freud, whose oeuvre is revisited in a questing spirit leading to the discovery of novel connections, which have hitherto received little attention; philosophers and epistemologists, precious neighbours who can enrich our notions of psychoanalytic thought by bringing a different perspective to it; selected analytic masters and colleagues, some close, akin and congenial to the author and others very different and remote from him, who together form a kind of ‘not-self’ that provides stimulation and opportunities for comparison throughout the length of the book; and finally the reader, who is called upon to share in this interesting and unconventional critical tour—with the implicit promise (which I believe the author keeps) of overall cultural and conceptual enrichment provided that he/she entrusts him/herself to Dr Chianese's inspired if avowedly highly personal choice of route.

The book comprises two main parts and a conclusion. The first deals with the concept of construction and includes sections on material reality, historical reality, psychic reality and narratology. The second, devoted to the analytic field, has sections on virtual space, scenic space, the countertransference and the chiasma. The concluding chapter, ‘Leaving the stage’, discusses the boundary that every analyst must eventually cross, in both thought and profession.

[[RunningHead]]

Research

Presentations from Research Advisory Board Funded Projects

Robert Tyson , Nicola Abel-Hirsch and Maggie Mills

Maggie Mills

La Jolla, CA

65c Lofting Road
London N1 1JA

2 Meard Street
London W1V 3HR

Maggie Mills  

Three research projects won the Biannual Psychoanalytic Research Exceptional Contribution Awards: ‘Verbal and non-verbal indicators in psychotherapies with mothers and babies’, Marina Altmann de Litvan and Sylvia Gril; ‘A contingency-based approach to the etiology of “disorganised” attachment: the “flickering switch” hypothesis’, Gyorgy Gergely and Orsolya Koos; and ‘When more is less: an exploration of psychoanalytically-orientated hospital-based treatment for severe personality disorder’, Marco Chiesa.

The work of Litvan and Gril is a correlation study in which they sought to demonstrate that increases in non-verbal behaviours between a parent and a baby would correlate with increased thoughtfulness between a parent and a therapist. They did not find a ‘direct and lineal’ relationship between the two, but do suggest the importance of stimulating ‘the mother to develop non-verbal communications with the baby’. With its 10 subjects, 50 variables and no control group, the study, as discussant Phyllis Tyson noted, is very much a pilot project. Tyson further commented that the study is part of a much larger study in Uruguay investigating change in psychotherapy, with the intention ‘of finding therapeutic strategies to more effectively deliver psychotherapeutic services to at risk populations’.

Gergely and Koos, in their paper, give a clear description of the experiment at the heart of their study. A sample of 100 3- and 5-month-old babies were seated in front of two monitors, in such a way that they could freely move their legs. One monitor presented a live image of the babies’ moving legs, thus providing a visual stimulus perfectly contingent with the infants’ responses. The other monitor presented a previously recorded and therefore non-contingent image of the infant's moving legs. They found that by 5 months untraumatised babies prefer, or can manage, imperfect contingency, whereas traumatised babies continue to look at perfectly correlated images of themselves. The authors suggest that with the traumatised babies, when the mother has been unavailable the baby turns back to autoerotic feedback. This formulation is offered as a refutation of those who argue that it is only frightening parents who turn infants into non-processing babies.

USA

Barnet Malin  

There is at least one spot in every dream at which it is unplumbable—a navel, as it were, that is its point of contact with the unknown (Freud, 1900).

Gardez-vous de comprendre [beware of understanding] (Lacan, 1956).

The only point of importance in any session is the unknown (Bion, 1967).

The poetics of metapsychology are ephemeral, and we easily mistake metapsychology for fact in order to serve omnipotent wishes to know what we cannot know. Almost none of our metapsychology theorises beyond the knowable, although the work of Bion and Lacan comprises two notable exceptions. Each writes of emotional experience beyond representation (for Bion, the concept of O, and for Lacan, the register of the Real), meaning that each is faced with the impossible task of writing about, or representing, the unrepresentable.

James S. Grotstein joins Bion and Lacan with this book, a synthesis and reworking of papers published over the past twenty years. He begins with a story. One morning, Grotstein arose after ‘witnessing’ an extraordinary dream. He realised that he could not possibly have been the one who created, or dreamed his dream, because he had been asleep at the time. Who dreamed it? Who was the Dreamer who dreamed his dream? As Grotstein asks, Who is the unconscious? And, therefore, What is the nature of the self?

Grotstein organises his book around answering these questions. It is an audacious, inspiring, at times wild and wildly successful effort, that, for me, often evoked a sense of truth and wisdom about human beingness. At its best, Grotstein's book reads with the depth and scope of epic poetry. He draws not only on psychoanalytic thinking (primarily Freud, Bion, Klein, Lacan, Matte-Blanco, Fairbairn, Winnicott, and Ogden) but also on theology and philosophy to evoke visions of the indescribable, unknowable, infinite and ineffable unconscious and self. I also found it wildly frustrating during times when it reads like a first draft. Core proposals and themes become diffused by lists of synonyms, adjectives, allusions, and tangents; it is as if Grotstein tries again and again to get to the essence of his untellable story by writing it in any and all ways he could imagine. But ultimately I found that reading his book was worth the effort, and it has made an impact on how I listen to my patients and to myself. This is the highest praise I can offer a psychoanalytic work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Lacanian Look at English Elegance

Some Reflections on Ian Mcewan's Enduring Love

Olga Cameron

Olga Cameron

19 Belgrave Square, Monkstown, Co. Dublin, Ireland

Olga Cameron  

o-object, anxiety, pre-subjectivity, desire.

This paper makes use of certain thought-provoking ideas from Lacan's seminars in the years between 1959 and 1962 in order to engage with the very subtly conveyed inner drama that subtends the plot of Ian McEwan's novel Enduring Love. Freud, in his 1914 paper ‘On narcissism’, had referred to theories on the earliest moments of subjectivity as ‘nebulous, scarcely imagined concepts’. In 1959 Lacan created a concept that he called the o-object to make incursions into theories on the lasting impact of the anxiety-ridden passage of the small pre-subject into subjective existence. In particular, the seminar on Anxiety (1962-3), for all the convolutions of syntax and sense that render it so difficult to read, opens up reflections on this topic that are not dissimilar to those stimulated by McEwan's accomplished prose. Both Lacan and McEwan explore the problematic dissolution of the everyday distinction between subjectivity and objectivity attendant on the upsurge of anxiety driven by unconscious forces.

responseSet.title: On Abraham's ‘The psychodynamics of orgasm’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Abraham's ‘The psychodynamics of orgasm’

Tamara Feldman

Tamara Feldman

604 S. Washington Sq., #2611
Philadelphia, PA 19106
USA
feldmant@att.net

Tamara Feldman  

Dear Sirs,

Dr Abraham's thoughtful and thorough exploration of the psychodynamics of orgasm (2002) was a delight to read from start to finish. At its inception, psychoanalysis was intrepid in its approach to all matters sexual and erotic in nature. Since the advent of sexology, as Dr Abraham points out, psychoanalysis has shied away from the bold theorising in which it had once engaged. Particularly with respect to orgasm, psychoanalytic investigations have been in a state of virtual quiescence since Masters and Johnson's studies first appeared. I hope that Dr Abraham's paper will engender further dialogue within the psychoanalytic community about this important topic.

Several of Dr Abraham's points in particular struck me as worthy of such a dialogue. First was his exploration of the female orgasm and his observation that it is ‘free’ in so far as it does not participate in reproduction. Hence, by its essence, the female orgasm is wholly erotic. To take this idea one step further, it seems that the female orgasm is also free in so far as it does not ‘cost’ the woman anything, unlike the male orgasm, which he must pay for with his semen. Furthermore, the man's orgasm comes at the cost of another orgasm in so far as most men require a relatively long refractory period between orgasms, as compared to women, who can have them in immediate succession. In this sense, as well, the female orgasm comes at no expense. Perhaps this explains why Freud and other early analysts felt the need to pathologise women's orgastic experience—their attempts at pathologising may have been an expression of their envy of the ‘free’ nature of the female orgasm. Why should he pay if she doesn't have to? Karen Horney likened women's penis envy to men's womb envy (1967). Perhaps orgastic envy might have been a more apt analogue.

A second point worthy of further elaboration stems from Dr. Abraham's clinical example of his female patient. As Dr Abraham explains, the patient remained consistently orgastically responsive despite her profound conflicts around orgasm. In other words, her physiologic response remained intact despite being surrounded by a great deal of psychological turmoil. Might then the converse be true? If a woman has difficulty achieving orgasm, could it be the result of a high stimulus threshold rather than necessarily indicative of intrapsychic conflict? Helen Kaplan argued cogently that women's sexual response could be best represented by a bell curve (1974). On one end of the spectrum are women who could attain orgasm merely through fantasy; on the other end are women who need intensive clitoral stimulation over a prolonged period of time. Neither end represents pathological responsiveness, simply the variability between women in their sexual responsiveness. Furthermore, neuroanatomical studies have shown that women vary greatly with respect to the quantity and distribution of sensory nerves throughout their genitalia (Krantz, 1958). The clitoris seems to display the most variability from woman to woman in terms of the number of nerve endings it contains and its sensitivity to stimulation (Krantz, 1958; Weijmar Schultz et al., 1989).

responseSet.title: Response to Dr Neville Symington's letter on the article on ‘Tolstoy's depression’ (2002, 83: 407-18)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr Neville Symington's letter on the article on ‘Tolstoy's depression’ (2002, 83: 407-18)

Annie Anargyros-Klinger

Annie Anargyros-Klinger

56 Boulevard Arago
75013 Paris
France

Annie Anargyros-Klinger  

Dear Sirs,

I welcome Dr Symington's interesting comments on my article, as I think that the criticisms may have the benefit of stimulating a discussion.

Although I do not consider the aggressive tone of the letter (which concludes by saying ‘this is not psychoanalysis but a fake replica’) favourable to a scientific exchange, I shall nevertheless make some comments.

I regret that my admiration for Tolstoy, a writer of genius, has not been apparent from my article. This is why I am surprised that my comments should have been interpreted as pejorative. I am equally surprised that Dr Symington is not more tolerant of a viewpoint different from his own. He reproaches me for seeing Tolstoy in terms of the Freudian model. I should explain that, for me, Freud is the primary reference point, although I am very interested in the theoretical and clinical contributions of his successors, for whom he paved the way.

It happens that I found, in my research on Tolstoy, an echo of Freud's work on Leonardo da Vinci. Indeed, these two unusual artists both encountered blocks in their creative work, which in no way detracts from their high quality. In fact, Freud has responded in advance to Dr Symington's criticisms at the beginning of his work ‘Leonardo da Vinci and a memory of his childhood’:

When psychiatric research, normally content to draw on frailer men for its material, approaches one who is among the greatest of the human race, it is not doing so for the reason so frequently ascribed to it by laymen. ‘To blacken the radiant and drag the sublime into the dust’ is no part of its purpose, and there is no satisfaction for it in narrowing the gulf which separates the perfection of the great from the inadequacy of the objects that are its usual concern. But it cannot help finding worthy of understanding everything that can be recognized in these illustrious models, and it believes that there is no one so great as to be disgraced by being subject to the laws which govern both normal and pathological activity with equal cogency (1910, p. 151).

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Allied Therapies: What Does A Psychoanalyst Do When He/She Thinks or Says he/she is Doing Psychotherapy?
responseSet.authorMast: Paul Israël,  Lewis A. Kirshner and  Abby Adams-Silvan
responseSet.authorCitation: Israël, P., Kirshner, L. A. & Adams-Silvan, A.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Allied Therapies

What Does A Psychoanalyst Do When He/She Thinks or Says he/she is Doing Psychotherapy?

Paul Israël , Lewis Kirshner and Abby Adams-Silvan

Abby Adams-Silvan

Paris

Cambridge, MA, USA

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Abby Adams-Silvan  

This well-attended meeting opened in an atmosphere of active collegial involvement and sophisticated consideration, an atmosphere maintained throughout the presentations and audience discussion. The question posed in the title is, of course, of considerable general interest. Answers offered by the discussants, Elizabeth Lima da Rocha Barros and Rene Roussillon, were based on a frank, detailed clinical presentation by an experienced and skilled practitioner, Bien C. Filet.

Chairman Paul Israel welcomed the group, presenting a brief history of ‘COPAAT’ (Committee on Psychoanalysis and Allied Therapies), which was formed three years ago under the aegis of the IPA to explore ideas and questions about the relationship of psychoanalysis and the unique nature of psychoanalytic training to the practice and theory of psychotherapy. It is upon the work of that committee, of which Dr Israel is Chair, that this panel is based, focusing specifically on how the psychoanalyst conducts him/herself technically when defining the work as psychotherapy and how this difference is conceptualised.

Dr Lewis Kirshner, Moderator, then addressed some issues germane to the implicit necessity for differentiating between the two praxes, psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. Based on surveys done by the committee, Dr Kirshner cited two commonly held broad positions. The first holds that psychoanalysts are practising psychoanalysis irrespective of the setting or frequency of sessions when their work centres on transference, defences, unconscious function and other fundamental analytic concepts, to whatever extent is possible with any given patient. According to this model, variations and differences are of minor theoretical significance. Kirshner spoke of a ‘hard version’ of this position that advocates a ‘classical’ analytic approach to be applied across all diagnostic lines, and a ‘soft version’ that speaks in terms of a continuum of techniques across the clinical spectrum.

The second broad position cited by Kirshner with respect to the differentiation of psychoanalysis from psychotherapy holds that the former is characterised by a specific setting (including frequency) and process that are suitable only for patients with a neurotic structure.

responseSet.title: Melanie Klein: Her Work in Context: Meira Likierman. London: Continuum. 2001. Pp. 202.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein: Her Work in Context

Meira Likierman. London: Continuum. 2001. Pp. 202.

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Spillius

20 Achilles Road
London NW6 1EA

Elizabeth Spillius  

Meira Likierman has written a detailed textual analysis of the thinking of Melanie Klein. Unlike Hanna Segal in her classic book, Introduction to the Work of Melanie Klein (1964, second edition 1973), Likierman does not rely on illustrative clinical material to convey Klein's ideas. Nor does she attempt, like Hinshelwood (1991) and Kristeva (2000), to show the links between Klein's ideas and other schools of thought, and between Klein and what has come to be called ‘post-Kleinian’ psychoanalysis—the work of Bion, Rosenfeld, Segal, Joseph and others. Like Petot (1990, 1991), Likierman aims for a definitive exegesis of Klein's texts. She wants to go right into the heart of the conceptual difficulties of Klein's thought in an attempt to reach ‘an elucidation of her theory in its own terms and from her own perspective as manifest in the texts’ (Likierman, p. 5).

On the whole I think Likierman's achievement is impressive. At times her detailed scrutiny is a little quirky. At times, too, there are interesting issues to which I think she pays too little attention: Klein's developing use of the concept of death instinct, for example, and her views on the relation between superego and internal objects.

297

responseSet.title: Saving the International Review
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Saving the International Review

Neil Maizels

Neil Maizels

38 Urquhart Street
Hawthorn, Vic. 3122
Australia

Neil Maizels  

Dear Sirs,

After some consideration, I want to make some comments on both Krin Gabbard's Film Review Essay ‘Saving Private Ryan's Surplus Repression’ (2000) and, subsequently, the 1993 decision to ‘merge’ and ‘incorporate’ the International Review into the Journal.

The ‘review’ of Spielberg's film disturbed me—not because of Gabbard's extreme dislike for the movie but because the grounds for the attack were so idiosyncratic and ‘un-analytic’.

We are not guided through thoughtful impressions of the emotional and poetic impact of Spielberg's imagery, narrative, scene juxtaposition and character sculpting—elements which might comprise a director's imaginative-emotional receptivity and transformative capability, or ‘alpha-function’ (Bion). Instead, we are flatly told in the opening sentence that the film is ‘conventional and predictable’ and that Spielberg has ‘once again’ tricked gullible millions into ‘so warm a reception’ by touching their ‘especially vulnerable’ oedipal spots, unconsciously.

Gabbard's belligerence towards war as ‘senseless slaughter’ is admirable. But to suggest that Spielberg has sentimentalised and idealised it, and its emotional impact on all humans, seems ludicrously unsupported, and possibly evasive of the full emotional impact of the film.

In stressing Spielberg's (and his generation's) oedipal rivalry with the father as the ‘real’ meaning of war, and of the film, Gabbard neglects the crucial narrative element that saves Private Ryan. It is his link with his mother. It is the thought of a mother's intolerable loss of all of her sons that moves—for better or for worse—that drives the ‘oedipal’ generals to some compassion.

But Spielberg's compassion seems deeper. He lets us feel enough fear to understand even the paralysis of the ‘coward’ who cannot bear any more—and who thus betrays the hero and causes his death. This is a more complex morality than the oedipal rivalry of Gabbard's ‘good’ and ‘bad’ Spielberg.

To reduce the film to the argument that ‘… the Good War was a necessary, life-defining experience that baby boomers and generation X-ers will never know’ and to reduce Spielberg's craft to that of a baby boomer who has ‘struck it rich’ (‘once again’) is to paint him as a ‘hollow man standing in the shadows’ of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Some Thoughts on Psychoanalysis and Ethics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts on Psychoanalysis and Ethics

Jaime Szpilka

Jaime Szpilka

C/Espronceda 38, 3°B Escalera Derecha, 28003 Madrid, Spain—jszpilka@inicia.es

Jaime Szpilka  

ethics, natural ethics, human ethics, categorical and hypothetical imperatives, Good, Bad, ethical and logical paradoxes, Oedipus complex.

The author attempts to establish a framework for understanding the contribution of psychoanalysis to ethics through examining the work of certain philosophers, especially Kant. After reviewing the development of Freudian thought and going beyond the ‘psychoanalysis and/or psychiatry?’ question, he asserts that the space of the psychoanalytical cure revolves around an ethical problem. Thus, the limits of analysis should be determined by the subject's capacity for developing a structure of belief in the unconscious, with the concomitant capacity to go beyond equivocation in respect of an ethical conflict that underlies all cases where psychical suffering is manifested. Indeed, only human beings are called upon to deal with an ethical paradox—equally a logical one—which could be stated thus: there is Good in Evil and Evil in Good. This ethical paradox is the consequence of human subjection to the constituent laws of the Oedipus complex, which distances the human being, in his/her dealings with Evil and with Good, from any naturalist stance. In respect of the cure, then, we must take into account that Evil does not proceed from any particular drive-based characteristic, but is rather the expression of specific subjection to an unconscious Other, towards which it directs its affects. Finally, the author proposes a principle that emerges from the preceding discussion: let us not impute to or place in the Other our own subjective splitting or pain at existing.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analisis terminable: Estudio de la terminación del proceso analítico.

Antonio Pérez-Sánchez. Valencia: Editorial Promolibro. 1997. Pp. 264.

Jose Quiros

Jose Quiros

Císcar, 55, 10a
46005 Valencia
Spain

Jose Quiros  

In this text the author comes to grips with his central theme by means of a method that is canonical in psychoanalytical literature, characterised here by the very detailed account of a single case—observed on this occasion from the viewpoint of the termination of the analysis—and also by the division of the content into two clearly differentiated parts (which are, however, closely related in a general sense)—the one part clinical, the other part theoretical.

Antonio Pérez-Sánchez, a teaching member of the Sociedad Española de Psicoanálisis, is a psychoanalyst with longstanding clinical and teaching experience, well aware, as he assures us in his book's introduction, of the risks implied by the use of this method for transmitting the analytical experience. Because of this, he felt the need to add an appendix (Capítulo XI) to justify the choice of method and defend it a priori from possible criticisms regarding the material's veracity and possible bias. Thus, this last chapter in fact becomes the book's real introduction, because it also contains a brief history of its genesis. The book's origin appears to be a detailed transcription of the initial sessions of an analysis, made for supervision purposes. A later impasse led the author to revisit this detailed record of the sessions, and finally Pérez-Sánchez resolved to write an internal scientific paper, not for publication, about the termination of the analysis, based on this material.

If I have focused attention in this way on the text's genesis, it is because this makes it possible to have a better understanding of the limitations that come to light in the course of reading it. One of these is the notable disproportion between the clinical part, to which the author devotes eight chapters, and the theoretical, which has only two chapters. In addition, the volume of the clinical material communicated is quite excessive—full of details which allow us to see the analytical process and the author's way of working very adequately but do not seem necessary to illustrate the book's monographic topic. This may make the reader wonder at times whether the purpose of the book is to provide a detailed description of a complete analytical process rather than to meet its stated aim of contributing to the study of specific problems involved in terminating an analysis.

responseSet.authorCitation: Grossman, W. I. & Silverman, D. K.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

History of the Vicissitudes in the Application of Psychoanalytic Method During the Last Fifty Years

William Grossman and Doris Silverman

Doris Silverman

Tenafly, NJ

315 Central Park West
New York, NY 10025

Doris Silverman  

‘The fact is that all writers create their precursors. Their work modifies our conception of the past, just as it is bound to modify the future.’ The Chair William Grossman quoted the writer Borges on assessing history. Using the psychoanalytic concept of Nachtraglichkeit, Grossman commented that ‘If a century of psychoanalysis has taught us anything it is that the writing of history expresses both personal and social Nachtraglichkeit as we both express the influences of the past and rewrite the past’. Thus, the very act of writing alters history.

Each of the panellists was assigned the task of describing the influence of the last fifty years on the psychoanalytic thinking and technique in his/her country. Naturally it is difficult to do justice to the inherent complexities in the proliferation of psychoanalytic thought and treatment during such a lengthy period. Yet our speakers detailed major themes of continuity along with powerful disruptions.

Gilda Sabsay Foks who was supposed to address this issue for Argentina and Latin America was unable to attend. Mariam Alizade graciously stepped in to provide an historical overview. She relied on Foks's written material while adding her own personal, lively and quite charming approach.

Latin American psychoanalysis, and particularly that of Buenos Aires (its major centre), was late in initiating formal psychoanalysis. It began in the early 1940s when Garma and Carcamo, having become acquainted in France, arrived in Buenos Aires determined to develop psychoanalytic work. They soon gathered around them a small coterie of like-minded people. It had the quality of Freud's initial circle, with members analysing and supervising one another. Nevertheless, because the early founders had sought analytic training in Europe, their theory and technique was consistent with the dominant Freudian perspective of that time.

Although Freudian thinking continued to be important, by the mid-fifties Klein's influence began to pervade their psychoanalytic thinking. Alizade emphasised that a shift towards recognition of the significance of the internal world at the expense of the external one occurred. She also mentioned the increased weight given to ‘non-verbal language’. Here I assume she is addressing the concept of projective identification, which became so central in Kleinian treatment.

The current volume is the eighth published by Dr Hedges with Jason Aronson. As with previous volumes, a specific type of transference (labelled as ‘organizing transference’) forms the theoretical core. This transference originates in the mother-child relationship four months before and four months after delivery. If this relationship is unsatisfying, trying, painful etc. to the infant, the ‘organizing transference’ is replicated in the adult's therapy and requires special understanding and special therapeutic techniques.

responseSet.title: The Analyst at Work: Nina
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst at Work

Nina

Giovanna Ambrosio

Giovanna Ambrosio

via Tevere 48
00198 Rome
Italy

Giovanna Ambrosio  

Nina was in analysis with me for eight years—from age 22 to 30—four times a week. She was born in a small mountain village and came to Rome to study Psychology at the University of Rome. She decided to come into analysis not out of a specific and conscious need, but because she intended to become a psychoanalyst herself after getting her university degree.

Nina has a very strange family background: at the age of one-and-a-half, despite having natural parents, she was given up for adoption to her uncle and aunt (her father's brother and his wife), who were themselves unable to have children. Such a solution was thought up by the grandparents in order to make up for the absence of direct descendants on the side of one of their sons, and for reasons of family inheritance. The decision was taken when the natural mother became pregnant for a second time and Nina's first sister was born, to be followed in the years to come by two more daughters.

The whole family, consisting of the two sets of parents and the four sisters, has always lived together in a big house in their home town. Here, they have all the essential amenities (a bakery, a petrol station, a bar that also sells newspapers…) and this, therefore, represents for them some kind of ‘bubble’ structured in an autarchic way.

Nina, then, found herself in the complex situation of being the eldest of four daughters from her natural parents who had ‘given her up’, whilst at the same time being the only child of the two parents who had adopted her. It appears that she made no distinction between the four adult figures, using the names ‘mummy’ and ‘daddy’ for both mothers and fathers, respectively. However, it emerges that, for many years of her life, Nina attributed the position of authority to her natural father and to her adoptive mother, whilst generally saving her affection for her adoptive father. The natural mother (‘who doesn't say anything but knows everything that goes on’) was alternately perceived either as a woman suffering in silence or otherwise as a traitor expecting to exercise some form of authority on her daughter.

responseSet.title: Child Psychoanalysis: Cathecting and Verbalising Affects in a New Relationship Aspects of the Analytic Method in Work With Children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Child Psychoanalysis

Cathecting and Verbalising Affects in a New Relationship Aspects of the Analytic Method in Work With Children

Paul Brinich and Karen Gilmore

Karen Gilmore

Chapel Hill, NC

305 East 18th Street
New York, NY 10003
USA
kgilmore@psychoanalysis.net

Karen Gilmore  

Paul Brinich, the panel moderator, oriented the panel presentations in regard to four key questions raised by the title: (1) What does it mean to cathect an affect? (2) What is involved in verbalising an affect? (3) How does the relationship between the analyst and the child analysand contribute to these two processes? (4) How does our understanding of these two processes contribute to the ongoing development of technique in child analysis.

(1) What does in mean to cathect an affect? Freud believed it was the association of a quota of affect with an idea that created a cathexis. So how can you speak of cathecting an affect? Can an affect be present and not cathected, since by definition cathexis includes an affect? When a red balloon floats by, we can identify its colour as long as a source of light is present. Does the balloon ‘have’ a colour? Only when illuminated. Does analytic work illuminate affect that is present but not realised and in that sense achieve a true cathexis?

(2) What is involved in verbalising an affect? We do this every day. We often ‘lend’ our affects to our patients whose defences lead to distancing from their affects. We may wordlessly exclaim in response to a patient, thereby showing our understanding and experiencing of the affect.

(3) and (4) Finally, how does the relation-ship between patient and analysand contribute to these processes of cathecting and verbalising? And in what ways are we new objects and how does that facilitate these processes? How does our understanding of these processes contribute to the evolution of theory and technique in child analysis?

Two cases were presented as contexts for the discussion. The first presenter, Viviane Chetrit-Vatine from Jerusalem, described the first year of a four-year analysis of a girl, Chen, who was brought for treatment at 7 years and 6 months. This child, who was Hebrew speaking, had used the masculine gender to refer to herself ever since she began to talk.

Her history disclosed that she was a fourth child born of an acrimonious marriage; both parents were described as having suffered throughout their own childhoods. Their third child, a boy, was retarded developmentally and required extra care. A subsequent pregnancy had been aborted 7 months prior to Chen's conception and was explicitly viewed as a ‘mistake’.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Studies in Neuro-Psychoanalysis: An Introduction to a Depth Neuropsychology.

Karen Kaplan Solms and Mark Solms London: Karnac Books. Pp. 320.

Morton Reiser MD

Morton Reiser

200 Todd St
Hamden CT 06518
USA
reiser@biomed.med.yale.edu

Morton Reiser  

Karen Kaplan Solms and Mark Solms tackle the implicit challenge posed in 1895 when Sigmund Freud abandoned his ‘Project for a Scientific Psychology’. The challenge has been there waiting for qualified researchers to take it up whenever the neurobiology of brain came to encompass data and concepts comparable to, and compatible with, those of the psychoanalytic psychology of mind. Clinical Studies in Neuro-Psychoanalysis: Introduction to a Depth Neuropsychology stands as evidence that the right time has come! This pair of investigators has been both courageous enough and wise enough to rise to the challenge. And they are particularly well qualified to do so with respect to both poles of the mind/brain axis.

731

responseSet.title: Homosexuality or Homoeroticism?: ‘Narcissistic Eroticism’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Homosexuality or Homoeroticism?

‘Narcissistic Eroticism’

Jean Bergeret

Jean Bergeret

47 Rue de la Garde
69005 Lyon
France

Jean Bergeret  

It may be dangerous for a psychoanalyst to let him/herself be influenced by the social and media pressure that proposes the use of the term ‘homosexual’ to describe the affective functioning of a homophile, for this assumes—from the outset—that it is of a truly sexual nature. However, following certain Freudian writings and the works of Férenczi in 1911, we know the different mechanisms of the narcissistic register that come into play in the quite particular relational behaviour that should more relevantly be called ‘homoeroticism’. On the other hand, our diagnostic and therapeutic approach will benefit from not considering, at the outset, all the economically and psychogenetically very different varieties of homoeroticism, male or female, latent or manifest, in too global a fashion, with reference also to the Freudian concept of psychic bisexuality. The French psychoanalytical research team working with the author has focused on these important issues for a number of years now. This article aims to give a fairly brief account of its work, with the objective of opening up a discussion amongst psychoanalysts, within the framework of Freudian thought.

responseSet.title: History of Psychoanalysis: Freud and Dream Contributing to and Celebrating the Centenary of the Interpretation of Dreams as The Origin of the Psychoanalytic Method
responseSet.authorMast: Harold Blum and  John S. Kafka
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

History of Psychoanalysis

Freud and Dream Contributing to and Celebrating the Centenary of the Interpretation of Dreams as The Origin of the Psychoanalytic Method

Harold Blum and John Kafka

John Kafka

Roslyn Estates, NY

5323 Connecticut Ave., N.W.
Washington, DC 20015

John Kafka  

The presenters and discussants of this panel had been asked to contribute to the celebration of the birth, more than a century ago, of The Interpretation of Dreams. Freud, apparently half-joking, had wondered ‘if there would ever be a marble tablet with the inscription “Here the secret of dreams was revealed to Dr. Sigmund Freud on July 24, 1895”’. All panellists, as celebrants, emphasising different aspects of Freud's work, said essentially: ‘No joking necessary. Here is the plaque that honours your achievement’.

Harold Blum described and celebrated Freud's achievement by summarising major themes of the book and showing why it was a ‘landmark in the history of thought, a revolution in the understanding of the human psyche’. Then, working as an archival detective, Blum speculated on the probable peregrinations of a manuscript dealing with Freud's own dreams and the topic of typical dreams. Blum spoke of Freud's use of dreams in his self-analysis and Freud's early belief that dream self-analysis might be sufficient for some other ‘not-too-sick’ future analysts. Freud, as Blum pointed out, was aware of his wishes both to reveal and to hide content and interpretation of his own dreams. The fact that Freud's manuscript dealing with his own and with typical dreams was in the hands of Alfred Adler was the starting point of some of Blum's research and speculations not only about phases in the relationship between Freud and Adler but also about the intersecting historical tracks of scientific, educational, promotional and political ambitions of Freud and his entourage. Blum, in his presentation ‘The interpretation of dreams’, thus connected the development of psychoanalytic ideas with the fate of the psychoanalytic movement.

The language in the title of the panel, ‘The Interpretation of Dreams as the origin of the psychoanalytic method’, provided the focus for Grubrich-Simitis's carefully documented study of the sources of, and the development of, psychoanalytic method and technique. The scope of this study is such that it can obviously not be summarised here. Her central thesis, however, was that ‘If we are talking about origins at all, then the title should read: “The psychoanalytic (clinical) method as the origin of The Interpretation of Dreams”’.. This approach permitted her, for instance, to show how the patient's preparation for dream analysis corresponds to preparation for analysis generally; how the focus on details of the dream (not the whole dream)

responseSet.authorMast: Claudio L. Eizirik and  Shmuel Erlich
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Method and its Borders

The Interface with the Outside World

Claudio Eizirik and Shmuel Erlich

Shmuel Erlich

Porto Alegre

42 Midbar Sinai Street
Jerusalem 97805
Israel
shmuel.erlich@huji.ac.il

Shmuel Erlich  

This panel addressed issues faced by psychoanalysis, and by psychoanalysts representing it, across different interfaces. This decidedly important area has not always received its fair share of attention in psychoanalytic discourse. In his opening remarks, Claudio Eizirik attributed this to the developmental decades in which psychoanalysis favoured internal growth and clinical refinement at the expense of dialogue with other areas. This has shifted recently towards greater exchange and contact with ‘the outside world’. There is growing dialogue with a number of neighbouring disciplines and with the culture at large. There is even room for hope with regard to medicine and psychiatry, promoted by the renewed interest in neuroscience. The IPA's Committee on Psychoanalysis and Society (CPS), established in 1997, has studied, consulted and contributed to interfaces with other areas. Members of this panel, all former CPS co-chairs in their respective continents, are actively involved and currently knowledgeable.

Ricardo Bernardi tackled the relationship between a ‘core’ and its ‘border’. The outer world is always present with the patient on the couch, and in the analytic sensitivity to the relationship between inner and outer worlds. These relationships can be elaborated along several dynamic borders. The first is the professional, in which psychoanalysis faces dilemmas vis-à-vis other professions. We need to define our academic and professional identity, which impacts on what separates as well as connects us with other academic and professional communities.

The second dynamic boundary is culture. The dissemination of psychoanalytic ideas has been accompanied by their trivialisation. Psychoanalytic ideas have been embedded in many intellectual fields. Bernardi advocates dialogue with the intellectual community that stems from dialogue with our own philosophical and ideological underpinnings. Unacknowledged assumptions of this nature are at the root of many clinical and theoretical debates and disagreements. He suggested that dealing with these ‘inner boundaries’ will enhance dialogue at the external boundaries where such philosophical and ideological issues are encountered. Such internal debate will also foster the growth and maturity of our field. We must come to grips with the extent of the applicability of extraneously derived models (for example, Nietzschean thought, pragmatism, postmodernism, semiotics) within our own discipline. This difficult dual confrontation between clinical facts and philosophical assumptions will be much easier if, instead of isolation, we manage to evolve a culture of open debate, of greater heuristic value and ideational freedom.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Atom and Archetype: The Pauli/Jung Letters 1932-1958

C. A. Meier. Preface by Beverley Zabriskie. Trans. David Roscoe. London: Routledge. 2001. Pp. 250.

David Black

David Black

30 Cholmley Gardens
London NW6 1AG
UK

David Black  

At the age of 31, the brilliant quantum physicist Wolfgang Pauli, a close colleague of Bohr and Heisenberg and later the discoverer of the neutrino, approached Carl Jung for an analysis. Pauli's mother had recently committed suicide after discovering that his father was having an affair. Pauli's marriage had broken up, and he had started to drink heavily. He suffered from paranoid thoughts and had begun to pick fights with strangers.

Jung, however, had ideas of his own. He had recently received a copy of a Chinese alchemical text, ‘The Secret of the Golden Flower’, and had been inspired by it to embark on research into Western alchemy. Influenced by the Chinese text, he was coming to think of time as a continuum that could give rise to parallel events that were not connected causally. (This was the phenomenon he would later call ‘synchronicity’, or meaningful coincidence.)

Moreover, since his break with Freud twenty years earlier, Jung had elaborated his notion of an undifferentiated psychic energy not derived from the drives. He hoped that the exciting developments in quantum physics would provide many parallels to his ideas, and the arrival of Pauli may well have seemed a ‘synchronicitous event’ in itself. He decided therefore to stand back from the psychic events taking place in Pauli, and ‘to avoid all personal influence’ he asked one of his students, Erna Rosenbaum, to undertake ‘the observation of the process’ (Jung, Psychology and Alchemy, CW vol. 12, p. 42). ‘I was absolutely sure she would not tamper’, he said.

In the course of five months with Rosenbaum, and then during three months on his own, the energetic Pauli wrote down some four hundred dreams. He then spent two years in analysis with Jung himself, apparently on a once-weekly basis.

990

responseSet.title: Making Psychoanalytic Scholarship Accessible: A Review of the Pep Cd Rom (Archive 1, Versions 2 and 3)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making Psychoanalytic Scholarship Accessible

A Review of the Pep Cd Rom (Archive 1, Versions 2 and 3)

Mary Target

Mary Target

26 King Henry's Road
London NW3 3RP
m.target@ucl.ac.uk

Mary Target  

The PEP CD is an extraordinary achievement. Although I had heard about its growing pains for some years, I had had little access to it until asked to do this review, and had not fully appreciated the extent to which it made psychoanalytic scholarship more available. For a start, it is hard to imagine that this little plastic disc houses the full, searchable text of the key psychoanalytic English-language journals from their first issues: as well as this journal and the former International Review of Psycho-Analysis, the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Quarterly and the Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. Version 3 has added Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Psychoanalytic Inquiry. However, the disc is much more than the electronic equivalent of a psychoanalytic journal library. The search engine is what really makes the disc a tremendous resource for students, writers and anybody who needs to research any subject in psychoanalysis.

It may be most helpful to take the reader through the experience of using the disc, as a beginner. I am still learning, and sometimes struggling, but I can describe my experience and that of university students of psychoanalytic theory.1 I particularly wanted the disc for them, I thought it should be ideal for students, in their reading for class, essay writing and dissertation research. So, is it?

To start at the beginning, the installation is relatively simple. The Archive (Version 3) can now be fully downloaded on to the user's hard drive, provided he/she has enough space (about 700 MB). This means the user does not have to keep the disc in the CD drive and performance is faster. It also means that if, like me, you do most of your work on a laptop, you are not frustrated by having left the disc somewhere else when you need it. For users with too little hard disc space, a ‘compact install’ is available. The program will calculate how much memory the computer has free, and hence which option to use. The only complicated aspect is the registration of the disc. You have to get the registration number, without which the disc will ‘time out’ after the fifteen-day trial period during which the user can decide whether they want to buy the licence. To get this, you need to email or phone customer service in the US or UK. This can be fine, as the customer service person can talk you through installation and licensing. Generally, though, it would be preferable to be able to register on the website. The disc is registered for one machine. Were I to be using a laptop as well as a desktop, or desktops at home and office, this would be a real inconvenience.

responseSet.title: Foundations for Conceptual Research in Psychoanalysis: Anna Ursula Dreher, London: Karnac. 2000. Pp. 208
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Foundations for Conceptual Research in Psychoanalysis

Anna Ursula Dreher, London: Karnac. 2000. Pp. 208

Mary Target PHD

Mary Target

London WC1E 6BT
m.target@ucl.ac.uk

Mary Target  

This book is very timely. Psychoanalytic conceptual research might be said to be experiencing a vogue, should psychoanalytic research permit itself such frivolities. The increased interest in conceptual studies is illustrated by the fact that the new IPA President Daniel Widlöcher has divided the IPA Research Committee into two equal halves, dealing with empirical and conceptual research. Each has its own co-chair and all members are

712

identified as being aligned with one or the other approach to research. This extension to a committee that had previously consisted largely of empirical researchers may reflect two much broader changes. The first is a change in the climate of psychoanalytic research and of psychological research generally, to include more qualitative and conceptual approaches, and the second is recognition that continental Europe has deep and rich things to offer psychoanalysis from its hermeneutic and philosophical traditions. The Anglo-American, empirical approach to systematic research, dominating the English-language journals, is belatedly finding more room for French and German work, which—even in the field of conventional large-scale outcome studies—was previously too little known because of the language barrier. Continental conceptual research was less known still, formidable cultural and educational barriers being added to the linguistic problem. Dreher's book does us a great service in lucidly representing this approach.

Dreher's own work in this area was carried out largely at the Sigmund Freud Institut in Frankfurt, in collaboration with Professor Joseph Sandler; it is a great loss to us all that he did not live to collaborate also on this book. His presence lies behind much of what is covered. For example, Chapter IV is about the Hampstead Index Project (Sandler, Kennedy et al., 1980; Sandler, 1987) (begun by Dorothy Burlingham, then led by Sandler from 1956 until the nineteen seventies), and the chapter is extensively drawn from publications by those involved. Dreher adds an interesting discussion of the methodology of that project, which leads well into the following chapter, on the trauma project at the Sigmund Freud Institut, a project started by Sandler (Sandler, Dreher et al., 1991). Dreher is evidently a very worthy close collaborator in this work: her presentation and defence of it is spirited and scholarly, deserving wide attention.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Art, Creativity, Living.

Lesley Caldwell. London: Karnac. 2001. Pp. 160

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns

88 Nightingale Lane
London SW12 8NR
UK
kab33@dial.pipex.com

Jennifer Johns  

This book is a psychoanalytic Festschrift—a collection of essays specially offered by the authors, and selected by the editor to celebrate an honoured colleague. Each essay is offered or chosen as an individual gem, to be placed amongst its fellows in a carefully made setting, and if the choice is successful and the setting well crafted a Festschrift can be an ornament to the person to whom the book is dedicated—a very particular kind of book.

This book is dedicated to a very particular psychoanalyst, Marion Milner. Singular, inventive, provocative, and much loved by those who knew, worked with, or were analysed and taught by her, Marion Milner possessed a mind, as illuminated in her writing, as clear as one would expect from someone whose brother was a Nobel laureate in physics, and who herself rigorously tested her hypotheses in her clinical practice. Apart from her psychoanalytic writings, it is for the honest self-exploration revealed in the books published under the name Joanna Field—A Life of One's Own and On Not Being Able to Paint—and her interest in and relationship to art and artists that she is perhaps best known.

Lesley Caldwell, at present Director of the Squiggle Foundation in London, an organisation devoted mainly to the teaching and spread of the ideas of Donald Winnicott, and of which Marion was an enthusiastic member, has selected and presented these papers by different authors to give honour to her memory.

Framed—and the relationship between frame and picture was an important part of Milner's thinking—by Lesley Caldwell's useful and interesting introduction and a final chapter of appreciations of Marion Milner by colleagues, friends and neighbours, the individual papers naturally address art and creativity as well as clinical work, and include W. H. Auden's enthusiastic 1934 review of A Life of One's Own, and Winnicott's 1951 review of On Not Being Able to Paint. Milner was a close associate of Winnicott's, so that it is not surprising to find that the earliest chapters address creativity, quoting Winnicott rather more than Milner.

[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Alliance

Steven T. Levy M.D. Madison, CT: Int. Univ. Press. 2000. Pp. 137.

George Allison

George Allison

2271 NE 51st Street
Seattle, WA 98105

George Allison  

This slim volume comprising four papers and two discussions on the therapeutic alliance is the published version of a workshop sponsored by the American Psychoanalytic Association and originally presented in Atlanta in 1993 and Denver in 1994. It elicits surprising excitement on this time-worn subject through the rigorous presentation of divergent views by the six authors, whose ideas often disagree while they still overlap in many instances. It evokes a satisfying but maddening dilemma in the reader's rethinking not only of the therapeutic alliance concept but also of related concepts about the analytic relationship, such as transference, countertransference, interpretation, insight and suggestion.

Along with earlier monographs in the American's Workshop Series, this one is equally engaging. One becomes quickly immersed in a gripping debate about the consequences of Freud's efforts to make psychoanalytic technique free of suggestion. He did this by conceptualising ‘the unobjectionable positive transference’ to deal with the patient's co-operation in the analytic endeavour. This is skilfully presented by the editor, Dr Steven Levy, who sets the stage with a scintillating comprehensive overview in his brief introduction. This alone is worth reading. He also introduces each paper with a useful summary of what is to follow.

309

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Art: A Psychoanalytic View of the Life and Work of Cézanne
responseSet.authorMast: Saul K. Peña and  Ana-María Rizzuto
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Art

A Psychoanalytic View of the Life and Work of Cézanne

Saul Peña and Ana-María Rizzuto

Ana-María Rizzuto

Lima

75 Gardner Road
Brookline, MA 02445
USA

Ana-María Rizzuto  

Paul Cézanne, the father of modern painting, was the most original artistic innovator of his time, constantly reflecting on, and searching for solutions to problems of pictorial representation. He was also an unstable man—shy, misanthropic, occasionally paranoid, and ill at ease in the social world. Each presenter applied psychoanalytic insights to particular facets of the man and his art.

Melgar focused on what she considered Cézanne's main preoccupation: the genesis of a painting as a construction of the mind that has captured through the senses ‘the emerging order of a spontaneous organisation’ and has to express it on a flat surface. Cézanne considered that both the actual object and the pictorial object have a visible and an invisible structure, constructed by the artist through the painting itself. Visible natural objects and the artist's senses must interpenetrate each other in the ‘realisation’ of a work of art. Cézanne illustrated what he had to achieve as a painter by clasping together his hands, fingers spread, in a gesture of unity. For him a ‘landscape is thought from within’. He would spend hours and days looking at an object or a landscape in order to consider different angles of vision and the transformation of the object through them. In his canvases he included multiple perspectives of the objects, a technique which introduced dynamic tensions in the painting and enlarged the ordinary view of the objects. The structure of the technical construction of his paintings forces the viewing eye to shift the gaze in the composition of the image, causing it to reach out for what is not visible—for what Cézanne called ‘the negative space’. It is at that point that the aesthetic dialogue begins between the represented and the invisible in the viewer's experience. Melgar noted the similarity between Cézanne's constructions and the analytic work of constructing the analytic experience.

Cézanne's commitment to his methodological approach led him to avoid setting goals for himself before starting the work. Instead, he submitted himself to the generative process emerging from the dynamic forces between the interpenetration of the visible object in multiple perspectives and the invisible forces present beyond it. In his painting The Bathers (1900-1905) the generative geometry of forces conveys via the nude bodies and the landscape the aesthetic power of an inexpressible sexuality.
