responseSet.title: The Language of Schizophrenia and the World of Delusion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Language of Schizophrenia and the World of Delusion

Michael Robbins

Michael Robbins

56 Kestrel Lane
Amherst, MA 01002
mdrobbinsmd@attbi.com

Michael Robbins  

The bizarre language of schizophrenics is nowadays generally believed to be the meaningless breakdown product of a neurobiological disorder. The psychological significance of hallucinations and delusions also remains mysterious. Verbatim vignettes are presented from an advanced stage of a psychoanalytically informed treatment to illustrate that schizophrenics speak a meaningful language that is syntactically and morphologically similar to ordinary language, but is qualitatively different in terms of semantics and pragmatics. From a semantic perspective the ordinary distinctions between words, things, body states and actions are absent, creating words and sentences that are not representational or symbolic. It is a language of equivalence, immediacy and action as contrasted with a language of self-awareness, thoughtfulness, reflection and communication. From a pragmatic standpoint, because the language of schizophrenia makes no distinction between mind and world, it enables and supports delusional thinking. In order for the psychoanalyst to comprehend what the patient means, and couch interpretations in such a way as to convey the meaning he/she intends, analyst and patient must become aware of their inability to comprehend one another's languages and find a way to bridge the communication barrier.

responseSet.title: Presidential Address
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Presidential Address

Daniel Widlöcher

Daniel Widlöcher

248 boulevard Raspail
75014 Paris, France

Daniel Widlöcher  

A person who was a complete stranger to psychoanalysis (not a frequent case) was invited to our Congress. He was astonished by everything he heard.

‘How can so much be said in such a short time? And how can there be so many new ideas just two years after the last Congress?’

One of our female colleagues, rich in experience, was not slow to reply:

‘That's nothing. Bear in mind that in the meantime all the people you have heard have taken part in numerous colloquia and that they meet colleagues and trainees in their Societies every week for seminars, workshops and lectures.’

‘All that is, no doubt, what has been talked about during these last few days. They have been able to submit their work to the judgement of their foreign colleagues, and find out about the work of those colleagues.’

‘I am afraid that in many cases their main concern has been to be listened to rather than to listen to others.’

That is hardly a misrepresentation. For example, we devoted the Congress at Santiago in 1999 to the question of affect. Reports, presentations and summaries of the ‘panels’ were published. If our naive visitor had asked us how much that was new had been said on such an essential question we should have had some difficulty in giving a clear reply. Yet that would not have done us justice, as the Congress did give rise to very rich and topical reflections. The question I would like to ask is therefore the following: how and why do psychoanalysts have so much difficulty in debating among themselves, in clearly expressing their different points of view, the reasons for these differences and the conclusions we might draw for our practice?

In an article titled ‘Towards an international dialogue: North American reflections on the Santiago Congress’ Henry Smith, who was one of the co-presidents of the Programme Committee, records his surprise when, expecting to share a common language with the other participants, he discovered their language to be ‘an endless succession of tribal dialects’ (Smith, 2000, p. 307). This gave rise to a linguistic confusion which gravely compromised the quality of the exchanges. Nevertheless, Smith believes in, or hopes to believe in, a ‘common ground’, namely the same clinical experience, and considers that the diversity of languages may enable us to arrive at a common language which integrates certain contributions from these cultural dialects.

responseSet.title: New Perspectives from an Overview of the Congress
responseSet.authorMast: Otto F. Kernberg and  M. Nasir Ilahi
responseSet.authorCitation: Kernberg, O. F. & Ilahi, M. N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Perspectives from an Overview of the Congress

Otto Kernberg and M. Ilahi

M. Ilahi

New York

41 Central Park West,
Suite 1C New York, NY 10023

M. Ilahi  

The Chair, Otto Kernberg, noted that the subject of the Congress—‘Psychoanalysis: Method and Applications’—concerned the application of the psychoanalytic method to the derived techniques of psychoanalytic psychotherapy so as to reach a broad category of patients that could not be treated by ‘standard’ psychoanalysis. Such application implied some sort of modification, giving rise to two questions. First, what were the boundaries of psychoanalysis and when is treatment no longer psychoanalytic? Second, to what extent were these boundaries gradual or was there a qualitative change in crossing a boundary?

He felt the overall discussion was made more difficult as there existed two major approaches to the method: a so-called Anglo-Saxon one, dominant in countries where the Eitingon training model applied, and the French approach, applicable in the Francophone areas. While both approaches agreed on the importance of free association, transference and countertransference analysis, and interpretation (even though the various schools in these regions differed in terms of what content areas were important), the main differences arose in the way interpretations were thought about. For him, the various Anglo-Saxon approaches utilised ‘systematic interpretations, i.e. relatively rapid interpretations throughout the session’, versus ‘tactical interpretation’ in the French approach. The latter were more ‘unsaturated’ and attempted to go to all levels at the same time in order to stimulate unconscious change, which is then evaluated by the changes in free associations. They thus placed an important stress on the après coup (Freud's Nachtraglichkeit) where the meaning of the changes, or the lack thereof, can only be understood post factum. By contrast, the Anglo-Saxon approaches tended to move from surface to depth. He felt this applied even to the Kleinian approach, which attended to what was most dominant in the here and now in contrast to the deepest level of meaning.

Further, he observed that Jean Luc Donnet had drawn attention to other subtle aspects of the French approach, such as the need to pay attention not simply to the analyses of transference and countertransference but also to the fostering of a capacity for self-reflection—a capacity for thirdness, as it were. In the French approach, the ‘unsaturated’ interpretations of the analyst gradually enabled the patient to develop such a capacity via identification with the analyst's self-reflective function. For Kernberg, such subtleties created an uneasiness in certain analysts on the Anglo-Saxon side who wondered how the overall French approach could be sufficiently precisely specified for the purposes of carrying out controlled research on the applications of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Colour and Music: Voices of the Unconscious
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Colour and Music

Voices of the Unconscious

Ekkehard Gattig and Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

Bremen

38 Berkeley Road,
London N8 8RU
a.sabbadini@macunlimited.net

Andrea Sabbadini  

After welcoming presenters and discussants, Chairman Ekkehard Gattig briefly introduced the topic of this Special Panel, the last event in the Scientific Programme of the Congress. The original inspiration for its theme, he informed us, was the city of Nice itself, with its colours and sounds, its natural beauties and rich cultural tradition in both painting and music. Gattig described how for a long time the psychoanalytical study of art has connected individual works to the biography and individual psychopathology of the artist. For Gattig, however, this approach is inadequate to account for the effect that a work of art has on its viewer. The psychoanalytical study of art is now entering a new context of discussion as creativity consists of interactive, inter-subjective processes occurring among artists, the expressions of their psychic existences, and their audience.

In his important and thought-provoking presentation, José E. Fischbein reminded us how the psychoanalytical contributions to the study of the subject's singularity at the beginning of the twentieth century coincided with the pictorial movements which broke with realism and placed their emphasis on the artist's own internal experiences. These movements, which would lead up to abstraction and bring artistic figurability nearer to an increased subjectivity, include Klee's experimentations with lines and colours, Kandinsky's ‘art without an object’, Picasso's fragmentations and deconstructions of figures and Matisse's successful efforts to bring into harmony contrasting elements of colour, form and light.

The development of abstract painting in the work of Paul Klee, described as ‘a master in the art of making the invisible visible’, is the subject of Joachim F. Danckwardt's erudite presentation, which he illustrated with numerous slides. The emergence in 1914 of Klee's ‘magic squares’, where form is established with colour alone rather than lines (which only consist of dark/light contrasts and colour hues), amounts to the creation of ‘a new reality’. In the beginning, Danckwardt explained, Klee's colours were neurotic solutions associated to childhood experiences. The colour black, for instance, represented his mother's depressive mood, which, bound as she was by her paralysis to a wheelchair, she always showed when threatened by object loss (for example, when Paul left home). The colours white and red were instead unconsciously associated with Klee's early feelings of envy of girls and shame for being a boy.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Case Formulation: Nancy McWilliams NY: The Guilford Press. 2000. Pp. 240.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Case Formulation

Nancy McWilliams NY: The Guilford Press. 2000. Pp. 240.

Jerome Winer MD

Jerome Winer

912 South Wood Street
Chicago, IL 60612
jwiner@psych.uic.edu

Jerome Winer  

These are times when we speak of psychoanalytic theories rather than psychoanalytic theory, and psychoanalytic techniques rather than technique. We also have empirical research findings to illuminate our understanding or perhaps to confuse us. An individual clinician must select and integrate the relevant factors in each case he or she sees. No wonder case formulation can be daunting to even the most experienced psychoanalytic psychotherapist. Thanks to Nancy McWilliams we have a readable text to teach students psychodynamic case formulation, one well sprinkled with clinical ‘pearls’ gained from the author's own extensive experience doing psychotherapy and psychoanalysis.

McWilliams aims to teach what is usually passed on through supervision. She writes to instruct the reader how to sail through the narrow strait between the Scylla of DSM diagnosis called for by third-party payers and the Charybdis of an irresponsible subjectivity that claims case formulation is unnecessarily academic. A professor of psychology, McWilliams also hopes to bring psychoanalytic ideas into classrooms where they have not been welcome previously. She is mindful of the limitations of assessment and treatment. She takes the position that part of a dynamic formulation is recognising those patients who cannot be treated with even the most encompassing psychodynamic approaches.

McWilliams believes that ‘it is more important for the clinician to understand people than to master specific treatment techniques’ (p. 9). One might think that she is thereby placing an empathic bartender on a par with a well-trained analyst. Her target is actually ‘empirically validated treatments’, the manualised treatments designed for specific diagnoses that take no heed of a patient's individual and highly personal concerns. Consequently McWilliams sees treatment objectives for a given patient as enhancing his or her sense of agency, solidifying a sense of identity, increasing realistically based self-esteem, developing the ability to love, work and appropriately depend on others, and facilitating one's experience of pleasure and serenity. She cites research demonstrating that such changes lead to better physical health and greater resistance to stress. McWilliams frequently makes such connections to work published in journals other than psychoanalytic ones, and that is one of the strengths of this volume. In the opinion of this reviewer, if psychoanalysis is to be a science, it must note the findings of other scientists.

responseSet.title: Cracking Up. The Work of the Unconscious Experience: Christopher Bollas. London: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 264.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cracking Up. The Work of the Unconscious Experience

Christopher Bollas. London: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 264.

Vincenzo Bonaminio and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Via Nomentana 256,
00162 Rome, Italy

MA (Cantab.), MITI

Philip Slotkin  

Cracking Up, published in 1995, is the fourth in Christopher Bollas's series of rich and penetrating books, which has since continued with the addition of two more recent volumes, The Mystery of Things (1999) and Hysteria (2000).

As an enthusiastic reader of the author's original treatment of the clinical phenomena he explores, embodied in a unique and personal style that eschews all the conventions of psychoanalytic writing, and partly because of his two-year periodicity of publication, I was expecting this book to be a further development of what I saw as a unitary—albeit internally linked—trilogy comprising The Shadow of the Object (1987), Forces of Destiny (1989) and Being a Character (1992).

However, when I saw what intense concentration was called for in the (in fact highly rewarding) reading and re-reading of Cracking Up owing to its complex and closely argued subject-matter, I realised that this volume constitutes not only a further step in the evolution of Bollas's thought but also a qualitative leap in his theoretical and conceptual progression.

This change of level proves to be determined by the author's chosen theme, which is fundamental to psychoanalysis—namely, as he puts it,

the place of unconscious work in the psychic context of self experience, a venture I began on Being a Character, where I argued that Freud's vision of the total dream process is a model of all unconscious experiencing. That process, which starts with a sequence of psychically intense experiences during the day, continues with the dream event, followed by cracking up the dream contents through free associations (p. 3).

Bollas had, in fact, already foreshadowed this line of thought—one might almost say programmatically—in a paper dating from the same period1 and now included in his more recent The Mystery of Things. Here he maintained that it

is no accident that Freud took the dream as the cornerstone of psychoanalysis, where the person

1‘Origins of the therapeutic alliance’, presented at the London Weekend Conference for English-Speaking Societies in 1990.

responseSet.title: The Thread of Depression Throughout the Life and Works of Leo Tolstoy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Thread of Depression Throughout the Life and Works of Leo Tolstoy

Annie Anargyros-Klinger

Annie Anargyros-Klinger

2002
56 Boulevard Arago
75013 Paris
France

Annie Anargyros-Klinger  

Tolstoy, the author of two masterpieces, War and Peace and Anna Karenina, remains a writer of genius. Yet, after writing War and Peace, his existence had been torn apart by a serious depression. This depression, which was melancholic in character, almost destroyed him and, once he had finished Anna Karenina, led him to want to renounce not only sexuality but also literary creation and material possessions. Through examining Tolstoy's life and work, the author tries to uncover the underground paths of this depression, which emerged brutally in the middle of his life, and to understand why his creative genius dried up. Like Leonardo da Vinci, Tolstoy turned away from his artistic work, declaring that ‘art is not only useless but even harmful’, and thereafter devoted himself to philosophical, political and religious writings. These new sublimations would help him to recover his health.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ethics of Psychoanalysis

Confidentiality

Jerome Winer and Ruth Lax

Ruth Lax

Chicago

1185 Park Avenue, #1G
New York, NY 10128

Ruth Lax  

Jerome Winer, Chair of the IPA Committee on Ethics, opened the panel with the statement: ‘Respect for the confidentiality of the patient's information and documents is the bedrock principle of the psychoanalytic profession’.

The first IPA Principles of Ethics were adopted 3 August 1998 by the Executive Council. These contained the following provision: psychoanalysts shall respect the confidentiality of their patients' information and documents, within the contours of applicable legal and professional standards (my italics).

In 2000, the IPA's Executive Council struck this limiting clause. The limiting provision and its deletion raise many issues which were presented and heatedly discussed during the panel's proceedings.

Romulo Lander was the only presenter who discussed the intrapsychic factors which may cause a ‘well-trained analyst to break the promise of confidentiality’ made to the patient. According to Lander, when the analyst is exposed to the effects of ‘chain signifiers’1 and ‘routinely’ identifies with the patient's material, he/she thereby places him/herself in ‘symmetry with the analysand’. This increases the analyst's anxiety, activates his/her ‘ideals and value judgment’ determining whether what he/she has introjected is ‘good or bad’. Such factors interfere with the correct flow of the analytic process.

According to Lacan (1962), by whom Lander is greatly influenced, ‘symmetry’ exists in the analytic dyad. Only an extensive, indepth, thorough analysis can help to prevent it. It will also enable the analyst to work primarily in ‘asymmetry’. The analyst in this preferable mode can maintain free-floating attention, avoid identification with the analysand, have a non-judgemental stance and make appropriate analytic interventions.

Freud's concept of the Mystic Writing Pad (1925) is utilised by Lander as a metaphor to describe the analyst's capacity to ‘automatically erase’ the material of a previous session from his/her consciousness, keeping it in the ‘forgotten preconscious system’ and thus entering the succeeding session unencumbered.

responseSet.title: Plenary Panel on Postmodern Psychoanalysis

responseSet.authorCitation: Mehler, J. A. & Robertson, B. M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Plenary Panel on Postmodern Psychoanalysis

Jacqueline Mehler and Brian Robertson

Brian Robertson

Rome

4999 Ste Catherine Street West
#201
Westmount, Quebec H3Z 1N2
Canada
mcbr@musica.mcgill.ca

Brian Robertson  

Jacqueline Amati Mehler, as Chair, welcomed the members of the audience and introduced the two speakers: Arnold Goldberg, the author of the plenary session paper entitled ‘Postmodern Psychoanalysis’, and Irma Brenman Pick, the discussant. Amati Mehler commented on the variable definition of the term ‘postmodern’ in contemporary culture. The modern world has cultivated the myth of progress, extolled the quantitative experimental method of science and accepted the power of universal reason; postmodernists challenge these beliefs. In contrast, they privilege diversity, pluralism, extreme relativism and the fragmentation of political groups. Amati Mehler further noted that although postmodernism suggested a unifying phenomenon that brought together philosophers, architects, novelists, painters, scientists and ethnic minorities, the reality was different. She referred to two types of postmodernism: one invented by artists, architects and then adopted in literature; the other invented by philosophers. The differences between these authors are many, at least in their relation to the past. Amati Mehler noted that the relation to the past is of particular interest to contemporary psychoanalysts, confronted as they are with theory and practice that derives both from tradition and from conceptual development.

The work of such philosophers as Lyotard and Derrida casts doubt on the whole inheritance of previous thinking, even though one may detect notions of refusal that at the same time represent a kind of re-appropriation. Things are different when postmodernism refers to fields other than the philosophical, such as the arts and literature. Here, post-modernism turns from the extreme abstractions of the modern artist, and the fragmentation of literature into experimental non-narratives, to a renewed relationship with the past expressions of these art forms, albeit a relationship of irony and self-examination, In conclusion, Amati Mehler wondered, what relevance does postmodernism have for psychoanalysis? Contemporary psychoanalysis is confronted with different theoretical models. Do these models imply different methods? How does this pluralism in theory and technique fit with, or oppose, the idea of a progressive conceptual and methodological development?

responseSet.title: Contrasting Aims in Transference Interpretation
responseSet.authorMast: Jaqueline Godfrind-Haber and  Helen K. Gediman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contrasting Aims in Transference Interpretation

Jaqueline Godfrind-Haber and Helen Gediman

Helen Gediman

Brussels

55 East 87th Street, #1B
New York, NY 10128
USA

Helen Gediman  

This panel left little doubt that there are contrasting aims and methods of transference interpretation among North American, European and South American psychoanalysts today. Cecilio Paniagua, speaking from the floor during the discussion period, claimed support for his 1995 position that contemporary psychoanalysis is characterised not only by diverse theories but also by diverse methodologies. He then questioned Wallerstein's (1988, 1990) view that methodologies, including common ways of analysing transference and resistance, if not theories, constituted our common ground. The varied approaches to psychoanalytic methodology of transference interpretation espoused in this panel do seem to support a position in which the common ground is a growing acceptance among analysts from disparate parts of the world that pluralism and diversity are the order of the day. What was most apparent to me was that any one of a variety of theories and methodologies may be applied to some patients more than others at any given moment, and that one methodology may be more valuable than another for a given patient.

Each contributor to this panel has addressed the issue of interpreting the here-and-now transference resistance observable in the analyst's consulting room at one or another point along a surface-to-depth continuum, from observable blocks in the flow of free associations to underlying, often unconscious dynamics presumably motivating that resistance. The controversy that emerged boiled down to choosing what to make explicit in the style and content of interpretation along three hypothetical points: (1) one analyses primarily the resistance, trusting that the patient will find the way, independently, towards understanding the symbolic meanings of underlying conflict; (2) one analyses primarily the contents of the underlying dynamic conflicts that are being resisted; (3) one analyses giving more or less equal weight to both the resistances and the underlying dynamic conflicts. In this panel, I found Busch to represent the first position, Diatkine the second, and Temple and León de Bernardi the third.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Method From an Epistemological Viewpoint
responseSet.authorMast: Sander M. Abend and  Nancy Kulish
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Method From an Epistemological Viewpoint

Sander Abend and Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

New York

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 48009
nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

This panel addressed the following questions: What do we psychoanalysts know and how do we acquire that knowledge? What are the limits of this knowledge and how is this question related to our methods of obtaining it? How do we discuss these issues and how do we validate our knowledge and our methodology?

Juan Pablo Jimenez began by advocating a sceptical attitude as the most suitable in exploring the foundations of the psychoanalytic method. Jimenez noted that a broad consensus supports Freud's idea that psychoanalytical knowledge emerges from the psychoanalytical clinical situation. In Freud's view, psychoanalysis refers to the procedure for the investigation of mental process, a method for the treatment of neurotic disorders, and a collection of psychological information obtained in these ways, which gradually becomes accumulated into a scientific discipline. For Freud the distinction between method of discovery and method of demonstration of the validity of what was discovered was not particularly relevant. For Jimenez, however, the relationships among interpretive method, theory and science are much more complex than Freud envisaged. Both therapeutic change as well as construction of theory spring from sources other than those acknowledged by the three-part model described above. Since Freud, the psychoanalytical method has undergone a process of idealisation, which has been replaced more recently by perplexity and plurality of approaches.

Turning to the problem of validity of the psychoanalytic method, Jimenez differentiated between the validity of knowledge and the origin of such knowledge. It is not enough to state that a given theory or technical approach originated in clinical experience to accept them as valid. Jimenez questioned Freud's assertion that the abundance of reliable observations makes them independent of the need for verification. Moreover, Freud did not foresee the fragmentation and pluralism in psychoanalysis today. There is no longer a belief that training analyses will render analysts impartial, so that there cannot be a collective consensus. Monism can only be sustained by absolute trust or total submission. Since monism is not sustainable, dogmatism should give way to a moderate and healthy scepticism.

responseSet.title: Negotiating the Therapeutic Alliance: A Relational Treatment Guide: Jeremy D. Safran and J. Christopher Muran. New York: The Guilford Press. 2000. Pp. 260
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Negotiating the Therapeutic Alliance: A Relational Treatment Guide

Jeremy D. Safran and J. Christopher Muran. New York: The Guilford Press. 2000. Pp. 260

Fredric Busch

Fredric Busch

10 East 78th Street, #5A
New York, NY 10021

Fredric Busch  

Over the last several years the concepts of a two-person psychology and intersubjectivity, along with associated clinical techniques, have become increasingly prominent in the psychoanalytic and psychotherapeutic literature. However, many psychoanalysts do not yet have a clear notion of what these ideas signify. In addition, many psychoanalysts still do not accept the critiques of the concepts of neutrality, anonymity and abstinence and cringe at the self-revelation suggested by many clinicians who employ these approaches. Safran and Muran brilliantly explicate these approaches and argue for their utility in a clear and cohesive fashion, focusing on the handling of disruptions in the therapeutic alliance. They weave together multiple concepts in constructing their approach, drawing from the work of Ferenczi, Sullivan, Mitchell, Greenberg, Luborsky and Buddhist philosophers.

The book, however, suffers from a significant flaw: it does not address the potential problems with these approaches. This is of particular importance because the authors offer their book as a manual for training therapists in approaches to resolving disruptions in the therapeutic alliance. Individuals in training and beginning analysts who use some of the self-revelatory techniques suggested in this book would be more likely to need advice on dealing with the potential ‘side effects’ of these interventions. These side effects, which could stall the therapeutic process, include patients experiencing therapists as sensitive or rejecting, or experiencing limitations of fantasy about therapists, stemming from therapists' self-revelations. It is not until the last case in the book that mention is made of a patient being hurt by one of the therapist's self-revelations, despite the fact that recurrent references are made to therapists commenting on feeling distant from patients or feeling unable to help patients.

In spite of this problem, the authors provide a complex interweaving of ideas supporting the value of their approaches.

responseSet.title: The Dove that Returns, The Dove that Vanishes: Paradox and Creativity in Psychoanalysis: Michael Parsons. London and Philadelphia: Routledge. 2000. Pp. 226
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dove that Returns, The Dove that Vanishes: Paradox and Creativity in Psychoanalysis

Michael Parsons. London and Philadelphia: Routledge. 2000. Pp. 226

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

833 Chestnut East, Suite 210-C
Philadelphia, PA 19107

Salman Akhtar  

Few books that I know are as panoramic, kaleidoscopic, dialectically based and aphoristic as Michael Parsons's The Dove that Returns, The Dove that Vanishes. Excitement begins with the book's name, which Parsons has drawn from Genesis, which describes Noah sending a dove out of his ark to see if the flood has abated. The dove returns conveying

534

that the earth's face is still wet. The next time it comes back with an olive leaf in its beak, thus informing Noah that the flood has receded somewhat. Upon being sent out a third time, the dove vanishes. The dove that returns brings knowledge to Noah. The dove that vanishes brings nothing. Or so it seems. Think again and you will realise that in using this allegory Parsons has cleverly laid the ground for the themes to come: home and sojourn, tradition and innovation, safety and risk-taking, belonging and freedom, psycho-analysis and beyond psychoanalysis. However, I must not get ahead of myself. So let me return to the four adjectives I have chosen to describe the book.

Panoramic as a compliment to a book tends to bring a huge multi-volume tome to mind. However, it is not the length of manuscript but the genuine contact with a diversity of topics and a fundamentally broad vision of the issues at hand that, to my mind, earns the designation. Parsons's book is devoted to the central contradiction of our clinical enterprise, i.e. psychoanalysis is based upon systematic principles of technique and yet depends upon a deeply personal engagement between two unique individuals. His book, however, goes far beyond the clinical situation and seeks to explicate issues not only of psychoanalytic setting and technique but also of psychoanalytic training and psychoanalytic identity in the context of their inherent contradictions and paradoxes. The scope of discussion is thus widened dramatically but never at the cost of serious attention and depth.

The adjective kaleidoscopic refers to the active manipulation, alteration and juxtaposition of perspectives in this book. One moment you see the material from this angle, next moment from a slightly different one. Parsons is skilful at this art. Read the following two sentences in succession and you will sense my meaning: ‘There is the deeply personal, subjective and intuitive aspect of psycho-analysis, and there is the need for objectivity, theory building, and discipline’ (p. 1); and, in connection with karate: ‘Speed and power means that attacks must also be delivered with discipline and control’ (p. 9). Or take note of these two ideas: ‘All development, not just in the work of analysis, poses that same challenge of leaving known and familiar satisfactions behind, for something perhaps more evolved, but unknown and uncertain’ (p. 81) and ‘Aggression has a fundamental, positive, role in establishing to begin with the space in which creativity can occur’ (p. 164). One moment Freud, Ferenczi and Fairbairn are cited to highlight a point. The next moment light is shed on that very issue by explicating the practices of Zen Buddhism, karate, archery and even Islam. Milner, Klauber, Bion and Matte-Blanco stroll through the pages of this book as unquestioned as Lao Tsu, Tagore and A. A. Milne. The result is aesthetically sumptuous and heuristically corroborative.

responseSet.title: ‘The Meaning of Dreams in the Psychotic State: Theoretical Considerations and Clinical Applications’: Paola Capozzi and Franco de Masi
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Meaning of Dreams in the Psychotic State: Theoretical Considerations and Clinical Applications’

Paola Capozzi and Franco de Masi

Andrzej Werbart

Andrzej Werbart

Högbergsgatan 71 D
SE-118 54 Stockholm, Sweden
andrzej@werbart.se

Andrzej Werbart  

Paola Capozzi and Franco De Masi's paper (82: 933-952) was discussed between 29 October 2001 and 14 January 2002. Full details of the discussion can be downloaded under the heading ‘Archives’ at the IJPA homepage (http://www.ijpa.org). A previous paper by one of the authors, De Masi's‘The unconscious and psychosis: some considerations on the psychoanalytic theory of psychosis’ (81: 1-20), was discussed in the spring of 2000 and the internet discussion was reviewed by Denise Cullington Roberts (81: 625-35).

The Paper

The authors consider that the Freudian theory of dreams is not directly applicable to psychotic and borderline patients with their constantly varying states of mental integration. These patients' dreams lack associations and have nothing to do with the metaphorical language of the dreamwork. Such dreams express the concreteness of the hallucinatory construction, but may also be a source of valuable information, which cannot be obtained from other material, on the manner of construction of the delusional system.1 The main thesis of this paper is that exploration of the dreams of psychotic patients may prove useful for understanding their psychosis and contribute to the analytic work of modifying the delusional state. The paper includes some clinical examples and a discussion of a longer case report.

Imprisonment in psychosis

According to clinical experience the relationship between the healthy and psychotic parts of the personality resembles a precarious compromise, which can easily break down.2 Persons with psychosis often describe themselves as imprisoned in an all-present system of delusions that can be activated at any time. To preserve their psychic equilibrium, patients often exert themselves not to experience or to keep their emotions ‘flat’.3 Such patients can see themselves as prisoners who can feel relatively safe provided that they

1This point was previously made by one of the authors, De Masi (1997; 2000).2Frosch (1983) has previously described the psychotic core of the personality, a locus of an ever-present potential for regression. The maturer ego mechanisms can surround the infantile psychotic self, which persists in an encapsulated fashion (D. Rosenfeld, 1992; H. Rosenfeld, 1965; Volkan, 1976; 1995).

responseSet.authorMast: Jennifer Johns and  Jorge De La Torre
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Method Working with Mental and Physical Impairment

Jennifer Johns and Jorge Torre

Jorge Torre

5300 San Jacinto 140
Houston, TX 77004
USA
drdelat@aol.com

Jorge Torre  

The panel offered a unique opportunity to look at a topic that has been largely foreign to psychoanalysis. In her introductory remarks, the moderator, Jennifer Johns, prepared the audience for what proved to be a very challenging and enlightening set of presentations. Some of the cases raised E. Ticho's ‘heroic analysis’ to a new height.

In the first presentation Valerie Sinason, of London, described a very disturbed and disturbing patient and her courageous, original treatment. The patient, an adult woman who functioned physically and also emotionally at a very primitive level, was incontinent and paralysed and had been institutionalised most of her life. She had been treated in spite of the fact that her potential in life was defined by the maintenance of a very limited biological existence. The analyst's genuine empathy and commitment allowed for the investigation of the possible meanings of the patient's behaviour, rather than this behaviour being seen as the consequence of neurological deficit. At one point, which coincided with the anniversary of the patient's abandonment by her family, the patient tested the limits of the analyst's capacity to accept her very regressed and mainly angry and aggressive behaviour. It was possible to interpret such a reaction in a very meaningful, compassionate and most useful manner. The analyst's countertransference, in the ‘totalistic sense’, guided her to more effective interventions.

James Rose, also of London, approached the problem of physical impairment from the perspective of secondary gain, as a struggle between ‘the healthy’ and ‘the ill’ aspects of the personality. Rose used Main's The Ailment (1989) as a way of organising his hypotheses about the patient's illness. According to Rose, patients with physical impairments relate to their analysts through the illness, assuming that this is the only aspect in which the analyst is interested. Together with the sense of ‘specialness’ that they experience, the stage gets set for the development of very intense countertransference. The analyst feels pulled by opposite forces, on the one hand not wanting to be perceived as not understanding the patient—as the patient felt misunderstood all his life—and on the other hand trying to avoid being overprotective in response to the patient's resistances. The patient's hostility towards his helpers is ambivalently held in place by his real need of these same helpers, while all the time he resents being seen ‘solely as an ill patient’.

[[RunningHead]]

The Problems of Technique with Severely Ill Patients

David Rosenfeld and Viviana Minerbo

Viviana Minerbo

Buenos Aires

Rua Sarandi, No. 78
01414-010 São Paulo
S. P. Brazil
102565.100@compuserve.com

Viviana Minerbo  

The chairman opened the Panel by saying that even with acute psychotics we have to think as analysts and fight against the patient's resistance and our own countertransference. Freud said that there is a healthy corner in any patient's mind.

The first panellist, Barbara Stimmel, presented her paper ‘Analysis ends’, in which she said that the dividing line between necessary reality-based interventions and psychoanalytic ones is not so clear and that both were used in the treatment of the difficult case she was going to describe. When is the analyst acting out, and when is she setting the boundaries to help the patient respect and come to grips with reality?

The patient was described as presenting overt assaults and aggressive behaviour in the sessions—screaming, ripping her hair and slamming doors and menacing the analyst's equilibrium. Constantly ridding herself of unbearable aspects by making use of massive projective identification into the figure of the analyst, the patient felt threatened by that same figure. More than once the analyst refused to continue an impossible session. In so doing, the analyst was putting the patient in a quiet corner, holding her responsible for her actions and simultaneously defending the patient and her own self from disguised retaliatory responses. The analyst hoped with this attitude to bind the patient's uncontained fury, and also to highlight the line separating them. Only when it was clear that their minds were separated could analytic work be resumed, and some sense be made of the symbolic meaning of such disruptive behaviour.

The main point of the paper was that the creation of structure and boundaries are necessary for analysis to take place. The patient was able, at moments, to gain sufficient distance to reaffirm her trust in the analyst's helpfulness, while recognising at the same time her intense need to both reject and ruin what the analyst had to offer, in an almost perpetual see-saw movement.

The analyst became aware that the reality-based nature of some interventions was necessary to preserve the analysis but was also a precursor of its ending. If the analyst allowed it, the patient would stay in analysis forever, even though the sessions were no longer productive. There was no true working through of the nature of the patient's attachment, to allow her to move on, and to mourn the loss. No amount of interpretation or insight could pierce the protective shield she had placed around their dyad.

The Musical Edge of Therapeutic Dialogue.

Steven H. Knoblauch Hillsdale, NJ and London: The Analytic Press. 2000. Pp. 184.

Ana-María Rizzuto MD

Ana-María Rizzuto

75 Gardner Road
Brookline, MA 02445 4523
USA

Ana-María Rizzuto  

Knoblauch is a supervising analyst from the Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity in New York City and an experienced jazz musician. His book applies the experiences and insights he has obtained in practising improvisation and accompaniment as a jazz player and listener to analytic listening, to the understanding of the analytic process, and to therapeutic technique. Knoblauch asserts that there is music in therapy and that the musical rhythms, tones, tempo, volume, gestures and accent present in the patient's verbalisations convey emerging meanings and desires. The analyst needs to immerse himself together with his patient in their shared musical ‘performance’ of dyadic improvisations and accompaniment in order to facilitate the emergence in the analysand of unformulated experiences and to establish mutual regulation between them and mutative therapeutic transformation of patterns of interaction and expectations.

Susan Kolodny, the author of The Captive Muse: On Creativity and its Inhibition, is a psychotherapist in practice in Oakland, California who holds a Doctorate in Mental Health from the University of California at San Francisco, and an MFA in poetry from the Program for Writers at Warren Wilson College. She has published a volume of poetry, Shoreline, and her work has appeared in the New England Review and River Styx as well as several anthologies. I begin by mentioning her credentials because in The Captive Muse she draws on her own experiences both as therapist and as writer.

This clinical material emphasises an obstacle, which can be considered either as an inconvenience or an opportunity to discover some new unconscious aspect of the material. If it is treated as an inconvenience, the intention would be to eliminate it in order to facilitate associations to a memory. As an opportunity, it will either hint at differences between patient and analyst in this situation, or pose a theoretical challenge.

responseSet.title: Discussion of Dr Bolognini's Case Presentation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of Dr Bolognini's Case Presentation

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, Apt. 1BB
New York
NY 10024
USA

Jay Greenberg  

The opening moments of Dr Bolognini's first session capture, for me, something crucial about what it feels like to do the work of analysis, and also what happens when the treatment is going well. There are two dimensions to this, both involving benign splits, one in the analysand, the other in the analyst.

First, the analysand. Dr Bolognini notes that he could consider ‘the adventure’ of the patient because—as represented in the dream—she is able to trust him to care for the 11-month-old baby while setting off to explore ‘other, more disconcerting portions of the transference’. This split, well represented in the dream, is promising. Alba has reached a stage in her analysis where she can at least entertain the possibility that she is safe enough to take risks, although it is likely that this is still quite conflictual for her.

As subsequent developments show, a crucial aspect of the risk that Alba needs to take is the risk of encountering Dr Bolognini himself, not simply as a dependable caretaker, but as the captain of a mythical, rather ominous enterprise. And, moreover, the caretaking Dr Bolognini must be entrusted not only with Alba's baby/self but also with his own. That is, she must believe that he will be able to contain his own negative feelings for Alba, feelings that we meet in Dr Bolognini's report almost before we meet Alba herself.

This gets me to the split in the analyst, which I believe is a ubiquitous feature of our work, but one that is rarely addressed in our literature. Dr Bolognini openly and immediately acknowledges it, telling us that ‘I become aware of a kind of functional scission within me …’ As I conceptualise it, the split is between a pull to analyse Alba—to get to work on the dream as he typically does—and a pull to react more personally, giving in to his emotional reactions to her way of reporting it.

762

responseSet.title: Authority in the Analytic Relationship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Authority in the Analytic Relationship

Gemma Jappe and Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Bonn

15 Arden Road
London N3 3AB
UK
Williams@dial.pipex.com

Paul Williams  

Gemma Jappe introduced the meeting by stating that psychoanalysis has undergone vast changes in the past century, but its core concern with individual freedom and attention to subjectivity has remained constant. Questions concerning authority are therefore crucial, and this panel planned to explore the theme through three presentations.

Ellen Rees then presented her paper, ‘The analyst's authority: an epistemological perspective’. In this she asked: Is the analyst's authority to interpret grounded in a body of knowledge that can be critically assessed, validated or potentially tested? In other words, does the content of an interpretation describe something real in the psyche or is it part of a narrative construction? Further, how do we decide if one hypothesis is better than another and on what basis do we change our minds about an idea or model we use? Psychoanalysis has used two traditions for the provision of methodologies for epistemological efforts: the philosophical (latterly herme-neutic) and the empirical. The former is characterised by attention to coherence and consistency, the latter by attention to truth and falsity. In addition, both traditions have had to face the problematic nature of psychoanalytic data—the inferences we make about unconscious content and processes. Edelson, for example, has argued that the procedures and methods of reasoning that characterise scientific activity are independent of subject matter. We can study mental phenomena in an interpersonal context provided we define rigorously the domains and entities that concern us. We need certain minimal standards for making case studies, for example, clear hypotheses, an alternative hypothesis, use of our hypothesis to account for observations, a noting of contradictions affecting the hypothesis, an account of the influence of subjective factors affecting observations and the provision of a method of generalisation to other cases. These are the tools of enquiry that give the analyst authority. Ricoeur, by contrast, has tried to extend the hermeneutic method to the human sciences through the study of the relationship between understanding and explanation. He hypothesised that the methodology of text interpretation could be used as a paradigm for interpretation in the human sciences. Forms of discourse and their meaning become the focus of attention. Ricoeur's criteria for evaluating the adequacy of an explanation are coherence with theory, intratextual consistency and narrative intelligibility. Explanations also need to succeed in economic terms. The role of falsification is played by the conflict between competing interpretations.

responseSet.title: Response to Bergeret's ‘Homosexuality or Homoeroticism: “Narcissistic Eroticism”’: (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 83:351-362)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Bergeret's ‘Homosexuality or Homoeroticism: “Narcissistic Eroticism”’

(Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 83:351-362)

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

240 Halah Circle
Atlanta, GA 30328
USA

Ralph Roughton  

Dear Sirs,

Jean Bergeret (2002) discards homosexuality as incorrect both conceptually and linguistically. He replaces it with his conceptualisation of homoeroticism and narcissistic eroticism and invites a discussion among psychoanalysts. Unfortunately, Bergeret's article amounts to a giant leap backward into old beliefs that psychoanalysts in the United States long ago recognised as based on seriously flawed assumptions. Repackaging them in French psychoanalytic thinking does not confer any greater validity.

Due to space limitations, I shall only comment here on a few major differences, leaving it to others to carry the discussion further. True sexuality is by definition heterosexual, according to Bergeret, who ‘emphatically insist[s] on the narcissistic nature of the erotic satisfaction sought by the homophile’. The libido of ‘homophiles’ has not evolved beyond the narcissistic-phallic stage; and their objects are therefore narcissistic, not sexual.

949

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On ‘The analyst at work’

Antonio Pacheco E Silva Filho

Antonio Pacheco E Silva Filho

Rua Padre Jaco Saliba n9
Sao Paulo, S.P.
CEPPCEP 01444-010
Brazil

Antonio Pacheco E Silva Filho  

Dear Sirs,

I want to congratulate the Journal for publication of ‘The Analyst at Work’. In my opinion it is the best thing a journal of psychoanalysis can offer.

Regarding the case presented by da Rocha Barros and discussed by Scarfone and Bonaminio, I agree with them about the lack of maternal care felt by the patient and the patient's flight towards an identification with the father figure, represented also by the analyst. In her dream about the blue vase, the case of the blue dress, the blue vases and the blue ceiling of the consulting room, we can clearly see her urge for a mother-environment.

The fact that in the patient's dream the other people are entranced by the vase in an almost religious way (a search for the idealised maternal object) could be, in my view, the fragmentation of a projective identification of her own craving for a maternal object to be adored. With this she felt empty and, as a result, created a pseudo-self, as Bonaminio put it, paraphrasing Winnicott.

I think her dilemma was between living ‘too inside’ (come back to the uterus, so to speak) or ‘too outside’, performing important deeds as identified with her father. This is a dilemma common to all human beings, well studied by Mauricio Abadi, an Argentinean psychoanalyst. Ana, the patient of da Rocha Barros, has it to a greater degree than most.

[[RunningHead]]

Psykoanalyyttisia tutkielmia [Psychoanalytic essays].

Pentti Ikonen. Helsinki: University Press. 2000. Pp. 193.

Turo Reenkola

Turo Reenkola

Ritokalliontie 8 E
00330 Helsinki
Finland
Turo.reenkola@pp.inet.fi

Turo Reenkola  

This compendium of essays written by Pentti Ikonen, one of Finland's psychoanalytic pioneers, deals comprehensively with the problems of psychoanalytic practice and theory. The most crucial issues taken up are the basic tools of the psychoanalytic method; the manifestations of the oedipal complex; phallic defences against the feelings of oedipal insufficiency; the metapsychological concepts of psychic binding and instinctual dualism, and idealisations behind ideologies.

Ikonen's conception of the psychoanalytic method takes as its starting-point Freud's observation that an analysand will grasp where his symptoms derive from if allowed to study his mind in peace.

1469

responseSet.title: Indeterminacy & Compromise Formation: Implications for a psychoanalytic Theory of Mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Indeterminacy & Compromise Formation

Implications for a psychoanalytic Theory of Mind

Bradley Peterson

Bradley Peterson

1051 Riverside Drive, Unit 74, New York,
NY 10032, USA—PetersoB@child.cpmc.columbia.edu

Bradley Peterson  

indeterminacy, compromise formation, meaning, language, philosophy, conflict, interpretation, translation, representation, psychoanalysis.

The meanings of communications between analyst and analysand are replete with alternative interpretations and ambiguity. Interpersonal meaning is inherently indeterminate, and so too is intrapsychic meaning. Indeterminacy of meaning, in fact, is an intrinsic feature of all psychic experience. In the clinical domain, indeterminate meaning is the psychic basis for forming and sustaining neurotic patterns of behaviour. This recognition has important implications for effective psychoanalytic intervention. Despite the inherent indeterminacy of meaning, it is not true that ‘anything goes’ when trying to make sense of the data that comprise psychic experience. Wittingly or not, analysts employ specific operational constraints to decide which among numerous alternative, competing interpretive schemes is the one that we accept as the ‘accurate’ and ‘true’ understanding of our experience. These constraints are employed by all rational systems and are important components of scientific theories and other explanatory domains. Neurotic symptoms and compromise formations do not recognise these constraints. They make freer use of indeterminate meaning to satisfy simultaneously the multiple competing demands of mental existence. Indeterminacy underlies the intense gratification afforded by neurotic symptoms and other transitional phenomena, in that it allows us to gratify simultaneously and as fully as possible our potentially contradictory desires that conflict with social and moral proscriptions. This simultaneous gratification afforded by compromise formation is why neurotic symptoms are so intractable to treatment. If conflict is one defining feature of the mind, then indeterminacy and compromise formation are the others.

responseSet.title: Pathways of Pleasure: The Channel of Primary Anality1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pathways of Pleasure

The Channel of Primary Anality1

Anna Potamianou

Anna Potamianou

4, Karneadou Street, 10675 Athens, Greece—helpsyco@otenet.gr

Anna Potamianou  

primary anality, narcissistic nidification, ego restriction, pleasure, defence, engulfment, mastery, excitation, mobility.

This article describes a mode of transference relationship in which the analysand has to contend with a psychic mobilisation that results from exchanges with the analyst-object and is related to the activation of pleasure in his/her own mental functioning. The patient's ego feels the internal and external excitations that stem from this mobilisation to be dangerous because of the anxieties about threatening intrusions that they raise. These anxieties arise along the contours of narcissistic flaws that give resonance to early traumatic experiences. The ego protects itself from the danger by organising narcissistic defences that oppose the impulses towards the analyst-object and foster a stagnation of the psychic work. The author puts forward the hypothesis that the patient's ego, not tolerating exposure to drive-related dangers, retracts into a mode of primary anality that is imprisoning and restrictive, setting up a fantasy of narcissistic nidification in which a part of the ego merges with an omnipotent primary object. The purpose of this strategy is to neutralise the excitations—as far as possible—while nevertheless maintaining the element of the drive excitation that forms the basis of the fantasy. The dynamics and the economy of the defensive organisation are examined in detail, as are the questions that these raise. A clinical case illustration is presented.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reporting a Dream Accompanying an Enactment in the Transference Situation

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Abteilung für Psychosomatische Medizin, Robert-Bosch-Krankenhaus, Postfach 50 11 20, 70341 Stuttgart, Germany—Heinz.Weiss@rbk.de

Heinz Weiss  

borderline-pathology, countertransference, dream, enactment, evacuation, interpretation, projective identification, psychosis, symbol-formation.

Patients with pathological organisations of the personality present the analyst with considerable technical difficulties. One of these problems arises from the fact that, in such patients, dreams frequently are not being used for communication of unconscious meaning, but instead for purposes of manipulation of the transference situation. They then represent attempts to identify the analyst with a part of the patient's self or with a particular internal object in order to draw him/her into collusive enactments. Following the work of Bion and Segal the paper presents a two-dimensional model in order to clarify the structure and use of dreams in this situation. The model may serve as a background orientation for the analyst in the clinical situation, as is subsequently illustrated in a detailed clinical sequence with a borderline patient. To conclude, the author suggests that whenever tendencies towards acting in are predominant, the interpretation of the enactment should generally be given preference over the interpretation of the dream content. The possible advantages and disadvantages of both strategies of interpretation are discussed. Finally, the author highlights consequences that arise when dealing with countertransference.

responseSet.title: Considerations on Depressive Conflict and its Different Levels of Intensity: Implications for Technique
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Considerations on Depressive Conflict and its Different Levels of Intensity

Implications for Technique

Francisco Espasa

Francisco Espasa

41 Chemin Crêts-de-Champel, 1206 Geneva, Switzerland — Elke.David@hcuge.org

Francisco Espasa  

depressive conflict, para-psychotic DP, para-depressive DP, para-neurotic DC, levels of intensity, technique implications, interpretation of guilt feelings.

This article considers the problem of depressive conflict (DC) and the difficulties that arise in integrating aggression towards the object of libidinal cathexis. This conflict results, in particular, from the destructive omnipotence that infantile fantasies attribute to the aggression. In order to better clarify Klein's very broad concept of the depressive position, three levels of severity of depressive conflict are proposed, depending on the type of fantasies concerning the loss of the object that predominate in the individual. These are: fantasies of catastrophic and irreparable destruction of the object (‘para-psychotic’ DC): fantasies of death and serious damage of the object (‘para-depressive’ DC); and fantasies of rejection and loss of the love of the object (‘para-neurotic’ DC). Having looked at these theoretical hypotheses, their implications for technique will then be considered, particularly the importance of focusing the interpretation on guilt feelings rather than on drives in the transferential relationship. A clinical example drawn from the work of Klein and a clinical vignette of a particularly difficult analysis illustrate this technical hypothesis.

responseSet.title: The Analyst at Work: Editors' Introduction
responseSet.authorMast: Glen O. Gabbard and  Paul Williams
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst at Work

Editors' Introduction

Glen Gabbard and Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams  

‘The Analyst at Work’ is now a regular feature of the Journal. Originally suggested by Henry F. Smith, when he was a member of the North American Editorial Board, this format allows for respectful and constructive dialogue regarding different analytic perspectives on clinical data. It also raises complicated methodological issues. The clinical material provided is not a compilation of factual observations. It is a construction or ‘text’ composed by the analyst that attempts to capture the experience of two persons in a consulting room on a particular day or days. Our discussants then attempt to interpret the text according to their respective psychoanalytic cultures and their own unique subjective understandings. Their task is challenging because they must create their interpretations from written words on a page, not from immersion in vivo in the ebb and flow of an analytic session. The patient's transference is to the analyst, not the discussant. The patient's tone of voice, subtle body movements, change in breathing patterns, sighs and whispers all escape the discussant's scrutiny. Our two commentators, then, are writing about a ‘patient’ who is somewhat different for them than for the analyst. They must enter an arena of imaginative play within which they can picture what might be going on in the session and speculate on different ways of looking at the content and the process. They are not supervising the analyst, but merely engaging in a form of creative reflection by placing themselves in the analyst's chair and allowing their associations to lead them in one direction or another. We hope that this exercise is instructive to our readers and that it paves the way for greater explorations in the largely uncharted territory of comparative psychoanalysis.

569

responseSet.title: Wishing for the Impossible: An Essay in Comparative Psychoanalytic Practice1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wishing for the Impossible

An Essay in Comparative Psychoanalytic Practice1

Dominique Scarfone

Dominique Scarfone

2983, avenue de Soissons
Montréal, Québec
Canada
H3S 1W1
Dominique.Scarfone@umontreal.ca

Dominique Scarfone  

I usually pay a great deal of attention to the very first moments between patient and analyst. The way the patient enters the analytic ‘stage’, including the first phone call, carries a number of implicit messages. These are not to be interpreted as such, but provide useful hints about the form of transference that is being introduced. Here, right from the start, Ana is asking for special treatment on account of her professional commitments. The analyst responds by accommodating her while making himself uncomfortable with the time schedule. However, this does not seem to have been addressed at the time as something significant in Ana's beginning analysis. So I am not surprised that the session the analyst chose to present is one where, a few months later, he is once again asked for special arrangements, to the point of feeling that he has ‘no way out’.

This makes me put the focus not so much on the patient's conscious dilemma between her feeling worthless and her need to seduce, only to feel thereafter disdain for the seduced. I am rather drawn to asking myself: to whom is she actually addressing her requests for ‘special arrangements’, to whom is she speaking about her dilemma, and what is this communication aimed at obtaining? In other words, just like Elias Barros, although from a different angle, I try to stay focused on the transference as the axis along which to follow the unfolding of the material. In so doing I read all of the patient's gentle assaults on the analytic setting as queries addressed to a father, himself ‘too committed’ to his social and financial business and, I surmise, too busy with a quest of his own for narcissistic perfection, a quest he ‘delegated to his children’. The theme of ‘availability’ is thus running through the material. The patient is at the same time enacting her own unavailability, thus identifying with her father, and testing the availability of the analyst. What is not yet clear is what is at stake under the guise of availability. What one can say is that by responding favourably to Ana's request for a special schedule, the analyst was actually filling in the patient's ready-made transference template—which Freud, in ‘The dynamics of transference’ (1912), called a ‘Klischee’—that was only waiting to be actualised in the analysis. This, however, obviously did not (and could not) satisfy Ana's unformulated unconscious desire, hence the return in the course of the analysis of a pattern which at one point irritates the analyst. The question remains of what her desire was about.

[[RunningHead]]

A Critical Enquiry into the Psychoanalytic Theories and Approaches to Psychosomatic Conditions

Betty Denzler and Nicholas Temple

Nicholas Temple

Gorgier

Stanfield House
86 Hampstead High Street
London NW3 1RE
UK

Nicholas Temple  

This panel consisted of three contributions from Marilia Aisenstein, Elsa Rappoport de Aisemberg and Philip Muskin with the discussion by Gustavo Delgado-Aparicio. The Chair, Betty Denzler, and the audience had a challenging task to integrate the complex presentations and the discussion to create a balanced view of modern psychoanalytic approaches to psychosomatic conditions that could be the basis of a critical enquiry.

Dr Aisenstein discussed her theoretical position and illustrated her views by reference to two clinical cases, the second read by her colleague Marina Papageorgiou. Both of these clinical vignettes focused on the patients’ dreams, which threw some light on symbolic aspects of the patients’ psychosomatic symptoms. Dr Aisenstein's view is that psychosomatic medicine cannot be regarded as a rather distant branch of psychoanalysis but is an integral part of it. She bases this on Freud's definition of the instinctual drives as the psychic expression of somatic excitation. Although Freud laid the foundations for the psychosomatic approach, he did not explicitly deal with psychosomatic medicine as a psychoanalytic approach to somatic diseases.

She referred to Freud's idea that thought first emerged at the sight of a dead body, both loved and hated. In the discussion Dr Delgado-Aparicio linked this to Bion's words, ‘Where there is no breast a thought can emerge and a creation starts’, emphasising that loss is the beginning of thought and the capacity to differentiate the self and the object. For a patient with psychosomatic symptoms, the problem is that the thought has been split off to appear as the bodily symptom, which Dr Rappoport concludes is a defensive regression to cope with psychic pain, the individual returning to the somatic code of infancy. Dr Aisenstein pointed out the difficulty of patients suffering from physical illness who show very little or no psychic elaboration with an apparent absence of conflict, although this is not entirely true of her clinical examples where the patients’ dreams give rise to a sequence of thoughts that begin to give symbolic meaning to the physical symptoms. She related these ideas to André Green's view that a psychosomatic illness occurs when excitation from inside the body does not reach the psychic area. He describes this failure as being due to a destructive drive acting to prevent the translation of excitation into thought and feeling. This is a form of psychic blindness leading to the state of la pensée opératorie

responseSet.title: The Rape of Medusa in the Temple of Athena: Aspects of Triangulation in the Girl1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Rape of Medusa in the Temple of Athena

Aspects of Triangulation in the Girl1

Beth Seelig MD

Beth Seelig

1256 Briarcliff Road, N.E. #154, Atlanta, GA 30306, USA—bseelig@learnlink.emory.edu

Beth Seelig  

triangulation, oedipal, female, development, sexuality, mythology, Medusa, Athena, Electra, Persephone.

The relationship between the Greek goddess Athena and her father Zeus, together with the competitive hostility she displays towards other females, is presented as illustrating some previously neglected aspects of triangular developmental conflicts in the little girl. Literature on ‘the Oedipus complex in the female’ is reviewed and discussed. The mythological early histories of both Athena and the female monster Medusa are examined for the light they can shed on female developmental vicissitudes and resultant conflicts in both women and men. Unconscious split representations of women as assertive, phallic and dangerous, or alternatively passive, castrated and receptive result in defensive repudiation of the idea that a woman can be both actively assertive and also feminine and sexual. Athena's enraged action of transforming the beautiful young maiden Medusa into a monster as punishment for the ‘crime’ of having been raped in her temple is discussed as illustrating an outcome of the lack of resolution of the little girl's early triangular conflicts.

responseSet.title: Commentary on Cecilio Paniagua's ‘A Terminating Case’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Cecilio Paniagua's ‘A Terminating Case’

Robert Michels MD

Robert Michels

418 East 71st Street, Suite 41
New York, NY 10021

Robert Michels  

I

The title selected by the analyst, ‘A terminating case’, suggests a particular mental set with which the analyst approached his patient. The patient is 47 years old and has had about nineteen years of treatment with three analysts. He has had many symptoms and life problems, has visited emergency rooms, sat up for extended periods of the analysis, required medication and psychiatric hospitalisation, and, in general, has been a difficult and challenging analysand. However, the analyst believes that the end may be in sight. In fact, a date has been set fourteen months prior to a planned termination (fourteen months or less prior to termination would probably include a third of all analytic sessions that have ever been conducted, but not all would necessarily be titled ‘A terminating case’ by analysts writing descriptions of them). The clue offered by the title is born out by the session. The analyst hopes that the analysis is coming to an end, but also has misgivings, i.e. ‘However, I did not feel thoroughly satisfied … he had not become as psychologically minded and self-regulated as I would have wished him to’ (p. 166). The theme of conflict about termination runs through the session, preoccupying both analyst and patient. The analyst believes that the patient is better than the patient fears, or at least better than he is willing to admit, and that the patient's misgivings about his progress serve to support his longing for a caretaker analyst-father. However, the analyst has his own doubts. The patient is still hypochondriacal and frightened; he complains that his wife provides him with neither children nor sexual pleasure, and that his analyst reminds him of his critical, grumpy father. Who will be there when he needs support in the future? Like the analyst, the patient also has his doubts. Maybe he really is better.

The analyst thinks that ‘prolonging his treatment would mean fostering his childish dependence in an unwholesome way’ (p. 170). Perhaps, but is this an indication for termination, a hint of an issue that could profit from further analysis, or an unanalysable problem that will lead either to an interminable process or to ending the analysis by fiat rather than analytically? The analyst, the patient and the reader all wonder.

responseSet.title: Who Killed Laius?: On Sophocles' Enigmatic Message
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who Killed Laius?

On Sophocles' Enigmatic Message

Beatriz Priel

Beatriz Priel

Beer-Sheva 84105
Israel
bpriel@bgumail.bgu.ac.il

Beatriz Priel  

Using Laplanche's basic conceptualisation of the role of the other in unconscious processes, the author proposes a reading of Sophocles' tragedy, Oedipus the King, according to basic principles of dream interpretation. This reading corroborates contemporary literary perspectives suggesting that Sophocles' tragedy may not only convey the myth but also provide a critical analysis of how myths work. Important textual inconsistencies and incoherence, which have been noted through the centuries, suggest the existence of another, repressed story. Moreover, the action of the play points to enigmatic parental messages of infanticide and the silencing of Oedipus's story, as well as their translation into primordial guilt, as the origins of the tragic denouement. Oedipus's self-condemnation of parricide follows these enigmatic codes and is unrelated to, and may even contradict, the evidence offered in the tragedy as to the identity of Laius's murderers. Moreover, Sophocles' text provides a complex intertwining of hermeneutic and deterministic perspectives. Through the use of the mythical deterministic content, the formal characteristics of Sophocles' text, mainly its complex time perspective and extensive use of double meaning, dramatise in the act of reading an acute awareness of interpretation. This reading underscores the fundamental role of the other in the constitution of unconscious processes.

responseSet.title: Commentary on ‘A Terminating Case’ by Cecilio Paniagua
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on ‘A Terminating Case’ by Cecilio Paniagua

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton

24 Hillfield Road
London NW6 1PZ

Ronald Britton  

In his clinical account Dr Cecilio Paniagua describes his work with a patient who presents a difficult technical problem. It is not simply an illustration of his usual technique. The clear and frank account of a portion of this analysis shows how sensitive the analyst has to be to hold his patient and how much internal dialogue the analyst needs with himself to preserve his analytic identity. The patient is having an analysis of the ‘third kind’ not just a third analysis. The first ‘intrusive’ analysis, we are told, produced a breakdown; the second, prior to the death of the analyst, looked interminable; and the third, with which we are presented, is hopefully leading to termination. With borderline patients, such as this, it is likely that with some analysts there is an abortive analysis, and with others an interminable analysis. Dr Paniagua is very aware of both possibilities and it is clear that his approach is shaped accordingly.

The analytic problem is familiar to anyone who has worked with those hyper-subjective, hypersensitive individuals whom Rosenfeld described as ‘thin-skinned narcissistic patients’. He contrasted them with those ‘whose narcissistic structure provides them with … such a thick skin that they have become insensitive to deeper feelings’, and he warned that ‘when the sensitive narcissistic patient is treated in analysis as if he is the thick-skinned patient he will be severely traumatised’ (1987, p. 274). I have suggested that

in these cases it is the attempted integration of subjective and objective ways of thinking in the patient, or the bringing together of empathic understanding and intellectual comprehension by the analyst, that is believed to cause a catastrophe … As it is an aim of psychoanalysis to integrate subjective experience and objective understanding, the very process of analysis is felt to be a threat to this group of patients. This makes for characteristic difficulties and raises considerable technical problems (1998, p. 43).

In my experience these patients combine a tendency to become addicted to analysis with an inability to tolerate it and are likely to go from analyst to analyst. This, however, is not a product of analysis as they have the same problem in their everyday lives; they depend on others but have difficulty in tolerating otherness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chaos Theory as a New Paradigm in Psychoanalysis a Contribution to the Discussion of Models

David Scharff and Warren Procci

Warren Procci

Chevy Chase, MD

230 S. San Rafael Avenue
Pasadena, CA 91105

Warren Procci  

This panel addressed chaos theory and, more specifically, non-random chaos theory. Since this is an area not familiar to many psycho-analysts, a significant portion of the panel was devoted to an exploration of a number of the fundamental principles of this idea. Chaos theory is to be distinguished from general systems theory, although they are in many ways consistent. General systems theory is derived from biology while chaos theory is derived from mathematical and computer models. Chaos theory is also differentiated from catastrophe theory although it may have been partially derived from it. Following this exposition the panel shifted towards efforts to demonstrate the clinical utility of chaos theory (Savage-Scharff), especially with application to object-relational approaches, and with an even more specific focus on transitional states (Quinodoz, Pragier). An overriding question of the panel was whether or not chaos theory provides a new paradigm for psychoanalysis.

The language and concepts utilised in chaos theory are substantially different from those in traditional psychoanalysis so a number of the basic principles were explored in some detail. Eight were specifically identified (Scharff).

Complex, dynamic systems are characterised by continuous feedback. Such systems can be described mathematically with iterated equations. When an iterated equation is solved, the answer is the starting point for the next run of the equation. Thus iterated systems always begin the next cycle at a place determined by the solution of the previous cycle. All biological life systems work as iterated systems.

In the reporter's view, this is a particularly key concept: that of sensitive dependence on initial conditions (SDIS). This is sometimes referred to as the ‘butterfly principle’. This was described by Lorenz, a weather researcher, who found that computer simulation of long-term weather forecasting yielded completely different outcomes when the program was run repetitively. In examining this Lorenz discovered that he had rounded off some numbers after several decimal points. He discovered that in complex weather systems small differences in initial conditions produce completely different and unpredictable results. Lorenz articulated this as the famous ‘butterfly’ effect, namely that the flap of a butterfly's wing in Brazil could produce a small current that might be unpredictably amplified until it becomes a hurricane in Texas. This idea stands in contrast with the Newtonian principles that underlay most of Freud's theoretical contributions.
