responseSet.title: Special Editorial: A Boycott by Passport
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Special Editorial

A Boycott by Passport

The following Editorial is a joint statement initiated by the editors of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytical Association and the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. It is endorsed and will be published by the editors of Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Psychoanalytic Inquiry, Contemporary Psychoanalysis, America Imago, The Canadian Journal of Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Dialogues, Psychoanalytic Review, Psicoanalisi (Journal of the Italian Psychoanalytic Association), Psyche-Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse und ihre Anwendungen and FEPAL.

As editors of psychoanalytic journals devoted to the advancement of knowledge about the human mind and human relationships, we condemn recent actions that have denied academic freedom to individuals solely on the basis of their nationality. On 18 June 2002, the Chronicle of Higher Education reported (see also the New Yorker, 8 June, and the New York Times, 11 July) that two distinguished Israeli scholars have been dismissed from the boards of two academic journals published in Great Britain. A senior lecturer in translation studies at Bar-llan University was dismissed from the editorial board of The Translator and a professor in Tel Aviv University's School of Cultural Studies was dismissed from the international advisory board of Translation Studies Abstracts. These actions have reportedly been taken in response to demands by a group of European scholars who, in support of one side in the Middle East conflict, would bar all Israeli nationals from academic conferences, publication in scholarly journals and participation on their boards. No suggestion was made in either case that the dismissed board member had written, said or done anything that this group deemed improper or indeed anything that was even relevant to the political issue involved. Had such a suggestion been made, our concern would be no less urgent but it would be different. At issue would be the bounds of civil discourse, the right to express opinions and the place of politics in academic life—difficult issues all. But what was done in the present instance is far simpler: two individuals were dismissed for no other reason than the passport they carry. We find it particularly ironic that two journals dedicated to translation should have compromised the very openness that facilitates the building of bridges, carrying understanding from one side of a divide to the other. As editors of psychoanalytic journals, we deplore this boycott by passport and extend, as always, an invitation to all scholars—without regard to race, religion or national origin—to join us in our attempts to understand and master the deep-seated sources of hatred and prejudice.

responseSet.title: Reassessing the Clinical Affinity Between Melanie Klein and D. W. Winnicott (1935-51): Klein's Unpublished ‘Notes on Baby’ In Historical Context
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reassessing the Clinical Affinity Between Melanie Klein and D. W. Winnicott (1935-51)

Klein's Unpublished ‘Notes on Baby’ In Historical Context

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

11849 West Olympic Boulevard, Suite 202, Los Angeles, California 90064, USA—jaguayophd@earthlink.net

Joseph Aguayo  

Klein, Winnicott, infant development, role of mother, nature, nurture, history, British Psychoanalytical Society, comparative psychoanalysis.

The author investigates the clinical affinity between Klein and Winnicott (1935-46) as a way to historically situate Winnicott's later criticism of Klein's ‘temperamental’ inability to understand the impact of the environment on the infant's development. By setting out Klein's theories at the time when Winnicott began supervision with her in 1935, a context is established for the analysis of an unpublished 1937 manuscript by Klein (‘Notes on baby’). The author argues that this direct and extensive infant observation demonstrates Klein's sensitivity to the familial environment. While Winnicott as a paediatrician showed enthusiasm for Klein's ideas, he also demonstrated a difference of opinion in emphasising the maternal environment of provision after his wartime evacuation experiences with London children. The factors leading to their mutual distancing are outlined as follows: (1) the post-Controversial Discussion atmosphere of the British Psycho-Analytical Society in 1944. The new non-aligned psychoanalytic ‘middle group’ allowed Winnicott to take a pick and choose attitude towards available analytic theories; (2) Winnicott's new clinical practices and theory differed from Klein's, leading to a widening gap between 1946 and 1951. Winnicott's new theory and practice simultaneously represented his technical marginalisation of Klein's emphasis on the direct analysis of the patient's destructiveness by the time he delivered the ‘Transitional objects’ paper in 1951.

responseSet.title: The Triadic Intersubjective Matrix in Supervision: The Use of Disclosure to Work Through Painful Affects1
responseSet.authorMast: Lawrence J. Brown and  Martin Miller
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Triadic Intersubjective Matrix in Supervision

The Use of Disclosure to Work Through Painful Affects1

Lawrence Brown and Martin Miller

Martin Miller

37 Homer Street, Newton Centre, MA 02459, USA — ljbrown@attbi.com

30 Lancaster Street, Cambridge, MA 02140, USA — millerma@massmed.org

Martin Miller  

supervision, countertransference, self-disclosure, intersubjectivity, adolescent analysis.

The use of the psychoanalyst's subjective reactions as a tool to better understand his/her patient has been a central feature of clinical thinking in recent decades. While there has been much discussion and debate about the analyst's use of countertransference in individual psychoanalysis, including possible disclosure of his/her feelings to the patient, the literature on supervision has been slower to consider such matters. The attention to parallel processes in supervision has been helpful in appreciating the impact of affects arising in either the analyst/patient or the supervisor/analyst dyads upon the analytic treatment and its supervision. This contribution addresses the ways in which overlapping aspects of the personalities of the supervisor, analyst and patient may intersect and create resistances in the treatment. That three-way intersection, described here as the triadic intersubjective matrix, is considered inevitable in all supervised treatments. A clinical example from the termination phase of a supervised analysis of an adolescent is offered to illustrate these points. Finally, the question of self-disclosure as an aspect of the supervisory alliance is also discussed.

responseSet.title: Speech Events, Language Development and the Clinical Situation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Speech Events, Language Development and the Clinical Situation

Ana-María Rizzuto

Ana-María Rizzuto

75 Gardner Road, Brookline, MA 02445-4523, USA — arizzuto@massmed.org

Ana-María Rizzuto  

speech event, language development, clinical situation, prosody, affective contact, phatic function, subject communication, pronunciation.

Psychoanalysis brings about psychic change by the mediation of speech. This paper reflects upon the significance of the structure and developmental organisation of the speech event as a verbal and non-verbal unit composed of semantically and prosodically1 encoded messages, interactions and emotional contact between partners. Spoken words communicate semantic meanings and the affects of a given speech event. Words carry personal emotional meanings which are inseparable from their referential significance. Such emotional meanings are very hard to articulate in words. They are conveyed by the ineffable but essential feelings present in their sound and pronunciation. Speech is an intentionally object-related and emotionally engaging social activity resulting from a child having been spoken to early in life by an adult wanting to establish affective verbal contact. The early organisation and later transformation of the structure of the speech event carries private meanings for each person's listening and speaking stance. A refined understanding of the structural and emotional complexities of verbal communicative exchanges during analysis may enhance the analyst's ability to understand the patient's manner of participation in the analytic process.2

responseSet.title: Changing Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy into Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changing Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy into Psychoanalysis

Hermann Beland and Anni Bergman

Anni Bergman

Berlin

224 East 20th Street
New York, NY 10011

Anni Bergman  

The panel ‘Changing Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy into Psychoanalysis’ was not well attended and there was a question at the beginning about whether the panel should go ahead with so few people in attendance. The panel Chair, Dr Hermann Beland, made the decision to go ahead, and while seemingly dealing with a rather straightforward question, the panel in fact touched on profound issues in our field connected to the question of what defines psychoanalysis.

The first paper, read by Dr Ted Jacobs from New York for the author, who unfortunately was absent due to illness, presented a straightforward optimistic approach to the problem of converting psychoanalytic psychotherapy to psychoanalysis. Dr Rothstein expressed his deeply felt conviction that psychoanalysis is the treatment of choice and that it is the task of psychoanalysts to demonstrate this to their patients: in a phase preparatory to treatment, the analyst should show the patient how psychoanalysis works. In the relatively rare cases where this preparatory phase does not lead to an analytic situation, Dr Rothstein prefers to refer the patient to a different analyst for psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Dr Rothstein assumes that people are analysable. He is less interested in diagnosis than in the attitude of the analyst and asserts that the analyst's attitude defines the analysis. He feels that the analyst needs to be tolerant; if the analysis does not work out, it is not the patient who fails, but the analyst. Dr Rothstein suggests that analysts should regard their pessimism as countertransference.

In the discussion of Dr Rothstein's paper, the point was made that there are disagreements about the difference between analysis and therapy. For example, three times a week is not considered analysis in London but is accepted as analysis in some analytic institutes in the United States. Ted Jacobs turned our focus to the analytic process by asserting that where there is not process, there is not analysis. The patient might come four times a week and be on the couch yet still be in intensive therapy, not in analysis. Seen in this way, the decision does not rest either with the analyst or the patient. What defines psychoanalysis vis-à-vis psychoanalytic psychotherapy is the process that goes on between psychoanalyst and patient. The condition of seeing a patient four times a week on the couch facilitates the process but does not define it.

responseSet.title: The External Observer and the Lens of the Patient-Analyst Match
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The External Observer and the Lens of the Patient-Analyst Match

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent St
Brookline, MA 02446
USA

Judy Kantrowitz  

A focus on the match between patient and analyst places attention on the dynamic effect of the interaction of character and conflict of both participants on the process that evolves between them. Match is neither a predictive nor static concept. Rather it refers to an unfolding transaction that itself shifts and changes during the course of analytic work. The treating analyst's perception of the effect of this match is by necessity limited by the analyst's own blind spots and other countertransference phenomena. Reporting the analyst's clinical experience to an analytically trained observer, external to the dyad, may broaden the analyst's perspective. Using the lens of the match, a colleague in the role of supervisor, consultant or peer can provide feedback from which the analyst may acquire insight. As a result of this process, the influence that the participants' similarities and differences have upon each other becomes clear to the analyst. This awareness, in turn, may lead the analyst to appreciate the effect of the analyst's stance of distance or closeness and to evaluate whether at this phase of treatment it is beneficial or detrimental to the analytic process. Clinical illustrations of the effect of the external observer's feedback in relation to the patient-analyst match are provided.

responseSet.title: The Psychodynamics of Orgasm
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychodynamics of Orgasm

Georges Abraham and Elaine Bolton

Elaine Bolton

13 avenue Krieg
1206 Geneva
Switzerland

Elaine Bolton  

Orgasm itself, along with its specific psychodynamic, has stimulated few reflections and little research in the psychoanalytical field to date. In this regard, perspectives relating to orgasm are generally placed within a wider context covering desire, arousal and, of course, satisfaction. Freud himself seemed to show no particular or systematic interest in an orgasmic psychodynamic per se. This article, the aim of which is to consider the specific nature of orgasm, proposes a two-stage reflection. First, it considers the specific aspects of orgasm in order to arrive at a phenomenology of the orgasm itself—within a clearly defined framework. Second, it explains this phenomenology from a psychoanalytical point of view. These reflections lead us to suggest that while the erogenous zones in men and women respectively are at the root of the sexual arousal that leads to orgasm, it is most probably in fantasy that the female orgasm finds its major trigger. This is all the more relevant given that the existence of a real qualitative difference between clitoral and vaginal orgasm is now being challenged. This would tend to confirm the idea of a predominance of fantasy over bodily location, given that, quite clearly, it is not possible to attribute the former role to a precise area of the body. In this respect, only the analytical process is able to lead us to the primary sources of orgasmic problems—both male and female—and, above all, to reconstruct the origin of a satisfactory or unsatisfactory orgasm.

responseSet.title: Impact of Narcissism: The Errant Therapist in a Chaotic Quest.: Peter Giovacchini, Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 2000. Pp. 324.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impact of Narcissism: The Errant Therapist in a Chaotic Quest.

Peter Giovacchini, Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 2000. Pp. 324.

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

833 Chestnut East, Suite 210-C
Philadelphia, PA 19107
USA
Salman.akhtar@mail.tju.edu

Salman Akhtar  

Like any other upper middle-class lad growing up in 1950s post-colonial India, I was deeply engrossed in cricket. Playing the game was only one part of the immersion. One played the game, memorised all sorts of records, developed transferences to various teams and players, and made up acronyms and alliterations for cataloguing one's fund of knowledge. Two such formulas that stand out in mind even today were the ‘Three Ws’ (Worrell, Wolcott and Weeks) and ‘Three Ss’ (Sobers, Solomon and Smith) representing the six great cricket players of West Indies.

All this comes to mind as I note that the book under review belongs to a sort of series by the ‘Three Gs’ of Chicago, that is, Goldberg, Gedo and Giovacchini. Boyish cleverness aside, the fact is that these three senior and distinguished analysts from one particular institute in the United States have recently written books with many common elements. Goldberg's The Prisonhouse of Psychoanalysis (1990) is a robust critique of orthodoxy, hero worship and ritualism rampant in psychoanalysis and its institutions. Gedo's Spleen and Nostalgia (1997) is a no-holds-barred account of how psychoanalysis has evolved during his long and outstanding career in the field. Giovacchini's Impact of Narcissism has shades of both these books. To be sure, there are differences in the three books. Goldberg's book is a plea to overcome stagnation and think anew our cherished propositions and practices. Giovacchini's book, in contrast, is a reminder to us of the explanatory power inherent in models he feels we might be leaving behind. Gedo's book is more of a professional autobiography than a theoretical tract. It is more colourful than the books by Goldberg or Giovacchini. Despite such differences, the three books have a lot in common. All of them show the impact of a common setting, especially in the way the figure of Heinz Kohut (a teacher and senior colleague of the three authors) looms in their backgrounds. All are personal in nature, filled with entertaining anecdotes, replete with lament and outrage and, in the end. unabashedly iconoclastic.

responseSet.title: Psychology and Literature: The Question of Reading Otherwise
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychology and Literature

The Question of Reading Otherwise

Inmaculada Jauregui and Pablo Méndez

Pablo Méndez

Carretera San José de las Vegas, 32-2°, 35.300—Santa Brigida, Gran Canaria, Spain —
email: ijpm@arrakis.es

Pablo Méndez  

Psychoanalysis, psychology, literature, literary creation, intersubjectivity, hospitality, imagination, play, emancipation, encounter, desire.

By making literature the partner of a dialogue with psychology, with the aim of better understanding the psyche, psychoanalysis has truly altered our way of understanding the literary phenomenon. Thus, Freud resorted to literature in an attempt to validate his theories. At the same time, though, in striving for—natural—scientific status, he shifted the emphasis away from any possible deep comprehension of the literature. However, psychoanalysis, trying to delve deeper into literature, has elaborated a whole theory regarding the literary meaning that is still prevalent today. In this paper, the author examines the Freudian conception of literature that points beyond the intrapsychic and moves towards intersubjectivity.

responseSet.title: An Essay on Dreaming, Psychical Working out and Working Through
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Essay on Dreaming, Psychical Working out and Working Through11

Elias Da Rocha Barros

Elias Da Rocha Barros

Rua Tupi 579 apto. 161. 01233-001-SaTo Paulo, Brazil — erbarro@attglobal.net

Elias Da Rocha Barros  

dream, symbolisation, meaning, pictogram, mental structures, working through, thinkability, pictorial images, unconscious representation, dreamwork.

In this paper the author attempts to expand the idea put forward by Freud who considered dreams as a special form of unconscious thinking. It is the author's contention that the psychical working-out function performed by dreams is a form of unconscious thinking, which transforms affects into memories and mental structures. He also attempts to clarify the way in which meaning is built and transformed in mental life. In that respect the unconscious internal world is seen as a form of unconscious thinking, a private theatre where meaning is generated and transformed. He focuses on what happens to feelings in dreams in connection with the meanings as a result of and an expression of the several stages of working through. The dream world is described as the setting where the mind gives expressive pictorial representation to the emotions involved in a conflict: a first step towards thinkability. The dreamwork also constitutes a process through which meaning is apprehended, built on and transformed at an expressive non-discursive level, based on representation through figurative/pictorial images. The author draws on Meltzer's formulation to conjecture that the working-through function of dreams, mainly in response to interpretations, is performed by a process of progression in formal qualities of the representations made available by dreaming in the form he has called affective pictograms. It is through progression in formal qualities of the representation that the thinking capabilities of the affective life develop and become part of the process of what is called metaphorically the metabolisation of emotional life. This process takes place through migration of meaning across various levels of mental process. In this perspective the analyst's interpretations of dreams effect what linguists call transmutation of the symbolic basis, a process that is necessary to help the mind to improve its capacity to think. Something expressed on the evocative plane and condensed into a pictographic image is then transformed into verbal language that expresses meaning. These conceptions are illustrated by a detailed clinical case.

responseSet.title: Attachment Theory and Psychoanalysis.: Peter Fonagy. New York: Other Press. 2001a. Pp. 190.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attachment Theory and Psychoanalysis.

Peter Fonagy. New York: Other Press. 2001a. Pp. 190.

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

6655 Travis, Suite 500
Houston, Texas 77030
USA
GGabbard12@aol.com

Glen Gabbard  

Until quite recently, it was entirely possible for a psychoanalytic candidate to graduate from an IPA accredited psychoanalytic institute without having read a word of attachment theory. The work of John Bowlby might have been mentioned in passing, but ordinarily such allusions were sprinkled with caveats that Bowlby was not truly psychoanalytic in that he was more interested in the external environment of the child than the internal world. In some cases the references to Bowlby might also have been interspersed with ad hoc criticisms about his cantankerous or curmudgeonly nature. Gossip about his clashes with the establishment figures in the British Society and his disillusionment with psychoanalysis might have accompanied such aspersions.

Times have changed. Today it would be highly unlikely for any freshly minted psychoanalyst to have passed through the rigours of psychoanalytic education without some exposure to attachment theory. Peter Fonagy deserves a substantial portion of the credit for this sea change in attitude. Through his prodigious output of publications and presentations, he has convinced the psychoanalytic world that an attachment perspective may bring clinically useful thinking to the psychoanalytic enterprise. Moreover, he has demonstrated that new avenues of empirical research are available if we adopt some of the concepts inherent in attachment theory.

In this slim new volume, Fonagy has succinctly summarised much of his own and others’ work in this area. One chapter concisely surveys the key findings of attachment research. Even more impressive, the author, in a pluralistic tour de force, examines each of the major schools of psychoanalytic thought from the standpoint of how they interface with attachment theory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ordinary People and Extra-Ordinary Protections: A Post-Kleinian Approach to the Treatment of Primitive Mental States

Judith Mitrani London: International Library of Psychoanalysis/Routledge2001. 186 pp.

R. Hinshelwood

R. Hinshelwood

18 Artesian Road
London W2 5AR

R. Hinshelwood  

This book intrigues. It describes excellently in transparent detail the clinical work of the author. Judith Mitrani is a Los Angeles psychoanalyst; trained there as a Kleinian she uses British psychoanalytic ideas of the Kleinian and object relations schools (Bick, Meltzer, Tustin as well as Winnicott). Thus she explores the use of Kleinian and so-called post-Kleinian concepts within the American psychoanalytic culture, dominated by ego-psychology and its derivatives. Mitrani does not work ‘within’ the ego-psychology tradition, but nevertheless there are traces of her argument that suggest an enduring need in California to emphasise differences - for instance the stress on anxiety rather than defences, and on the interpretation of transference. One interest for me in reviewing this book is the question: how do Kleinian views fare when transplanted into another psychoanalytic environment?

The book is full of clinical detail and process records of the author's work, which turn around two main cases, Hendrick and Chloe. As the author says it is a description of how one psychoanalyst works. But it is also an account of the theoretical developments that have led her to work in the way she does. Thus the book has more than one theme. In fact I think there are three - first, the theoretical developments that lead from Klein via Bick and Meltzer to Tustin; second, the clinical process that derives from the notion of transference as the total situation, described by Klein (1952) and Joseph (1985); and, third, the inspirational supervision and friendship with Frances Tustin. As a backdrop to these themes is Mitrani's deep acknowledgement of Bion's ideas (especially the later papers). This is a unique blend, one which the author has worked hard to integrate, and in addition has worked hard to present to her readers. The overriding quality of this book is its transparency, its lucid quality of discourse about the author's work, and the clear presentation of the clinical process she sets going. This leaves the reader always knowing exactly where he/she is with the author's thinking, and in a position to feel oneself there in the room with the analyst and her patients. At the same time this clarity gives one the opportunity to formulate particularly clearly some of the problematics of the author's approach. I shall try to summarise the argument of the book, and in the process make just a few points in the form of a critique.

The Analyst at Work

Two Sessions with Alba

Stefano Bolognini

Stefano Bolognini

Via dell'Abbadia n. 6
40122 Bologna
Italy

Stefano Bolognini  

In this paper, the author presents clinical material that relates to two sessions with a patient called Alba. This analytic work, being of a somewhat unusual character, lends itself to discussion, the author feels, since the technical choices made are undoubtedly very personal and he believes many colleagues might have done things differently. The author endeavours to enrich the account of the sessions with his concomitant thoughts, so as to supply colleagues with the elements, atmosphere and developments of his internal workshop.

responseSet.title: ‘Controversial Discussions’ the Issue of Differences in Method
responseSet.authorMast: Glen O. Gabbard and  Dominique Scarfone
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Controversial Discussions’ the Issue of Differences in Method

Glen Gabbard and Dominique Scarfone

Dominique Scarfone

Houston

2983, avenue de Soissons,
Montréal, Québec
Canada H3S 1W1

Dominique Scarfone  

As the legend goes, German bombs were pouring down on London while two of the three more or less formal groups of British psychoanalysts were fiercely battling each other in what were to be remembered in psychoanalytic history as the ‘Controversial Discussions’. According to the same anecdote, while the discussions carried on, no less violent in a way than the boming itself—which in the meantime grew more intense—a member of the third group, D. W. Winnicott, would raise a timid hand and, when finally given the right to speak, he would suggest that they postpone whatever urgent theoretical issue they were debating and all go for shelter. I do not know what historical truth there is in this vignette, but it certainly depicts the magnitude of affect invested in the ‘Controversial Discussions’, not only for those involved in them at the time, but also for those of us now who can only rely on the more or less accurate narration that percolates through the strata of oral and written psychoanalytic history.

At the most recent IPA Congress in Nice, last July, we did not yet know that we were moving towards a juncture partly resembling that surrounding the Controversial Discussions. Our times are certainly different, though, not only because the sort of war we now live in could not have been imagined during the nineteen forties, but also because those involved in the discussions at that epoch would hardly recognise their psychoanalytic progeny. In Nice, the exponents of the three groups within the British Psychoanalytic Society (Peter Fonagy for the ‘Freudians’, Ronald Britton for the ‘Kleinians’ and David Tuckett for the ‘Middle group’) were no bellicose foes, but courteous debaters who agreed to answer, before a large audience, a series of three questions put to them by the chair of the panel, Glen Gabbard. To answer, that is, not in parallel monologues nor in an overtly competitive fashion, but within the framework of a genuine and warm exchange of ideas such as, I must say, we seldom are able to witness among psychoanalysts of supposedly less ‘controversial’ descent. Need I say that in the 2,000 words allotted to this report I cannot really to justice to the varied and interesting answers given to Gabbard's important questions? However, let me give it a try.
